2. `getProgramFilterOptions` populates filter controls, including a program type selector and any additional controls.
3. The user activates a control that corresponds to filtering by school meal programs (e.g., selecting a program type that represents school meals or toggling a dedicated checkbox). This sets either `programType` and/or `isSchoolMealProgram` in the component state.
4. The page updates the URL parameters (`programType` and/or a mapping to `isSchoolMealProgram`) to reflect this selection.
5. The page calls `searchPrograms` with the updated filters. Results now include only programs matching those criteria.
6. The user scans the list, selects a program, and clicks a “Donate to this program” action on the card.
7. The browser navigates to `donate.html?tab=one_time&programId={selectedProgramId}&donationType=program_restricted`.
8. The user completes the donation on that page. If they navigate back to the programs page, the filters are re-applied from the URL parameters and the same filtered list appears.

Workflow 4: User wants to revisit their preferred view mode later
1. User visits the page and switches from Overview to Programs view using the view switch control.
2. The view switch handler calls `setProgramsPageViewPreference` with `view=programs`.
3. The handler also updates the URL parameter `view` to `programs`.
4. On a future visit, if the user arrives via a generic navigation link without any `view` parameter, the page calls `getProgramsPageViewPreference` during initialization.
5. The returned preference is `programs`, so the page loads directly into Programs view, calls `getProgramFilterOptions`, and triggers `searchPrograms` (with any default or inferred filters).
interactions: On page load, a controller-level initialization reads URL parameters (`view`, `programType`, `regionCode`, `minChildrenServed`), calls `getProgramsPageViewPreference`, decides the active view, and either calls `getProgramsOverviewContent` or `getProgramFilterOptions` plus `searchPrograms`.

When the user toggles between Overview and Programs view, the view mode switch component calls `setProgramsPageViewPreference` with the new view value and updates the `view` URL parameter; the page conditionally renders either overview content or the programs directory.
When the active view changes to Overview, the page calls `getProgramsOverviewContent` (if not already loaded) and displays overviewText, programAreaSummaries, and impactStats.

When the active view changes to Programs, the page calls `getProgramFilterOptions` (if not already loaded), initializes filter controls from URL parameters if present, and calls `searchPrograms` with the current filter state.

When the user changes any filter control (program type selector, region selector, minimum children served selector, or a dedicated school meal program control), the filter component updates the internal filter state, updates the corresponding URL parameters (`programType`, `regionCode`, `minChildrenServed` and logically maps to `isSchoolMealProgram`), and triggers a new `searchPrograms` request.
When the program search completes, the program results list component renders a list of program cards using the Program objects returned by `searchPrograms`.
When the user clicks on a program card’s main area or a dedicated detail action, the page navigates to `program_detail.html` with the `programId` query parameter set to the selected Program’s id.

When the user clicks the donation action on a program card, the page navigates to `donate.html` with `tab=one_time`, `programId` equal to the selected Program’s id, and `donationType=program_restricted`.

When the user modifies filters or view mode, the URL is updated in-place (e.g., using query string updates) to reflect the current state so that a page refresh or copy-paste of the URL restores the same view and filters.

If the user manually edits the URL parameters and reloads the page, the initialization logic reads these parameters and sets the view and filters accordingly before calling the appropriate interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is governed by the URL parameters `view`, `programType`, `regionCode`, and `minChildrenServed` along with the stored programs page view preference accessed via `getProgramsPageViewPreference`. On initialization, the page reads `view` from the URL. If `view` is present and matches an allowed value, it becomes the authoritative source for the current view mode, overriding any stored preference. If `view` is absent, the page calls `getProgramsPageViewPreference` and uses its `view` value if available; if there is no stored preference, the implementation chooses a default view (for example, overview). After the view mode is determined, the page branches: in Overview view, it calls `getProgramsOverviewContent` and stores the returned data in in-memory state used by the overview content component. In Programs view, it calls `getProgramFilterOptions`, then reads `programType`, `regionCode`, and `minChildrenServed` from the URL and uses those values to initialize in-memory filter state. A separate in-memory flag maps user intent to the `isSchoolMealProgram` parameter required by `searchPrograms`; this flag may be derived either from `programType` or from a dedicated control but is always expressed as a boolean when calling `searchPrograms`. The page then calls `searchPrograms` with the current filter state: `programType` (or null/undefined if not filtered by type), `regionCode` (or null/undefined), `minChildrenServed` (or null/undefined), and `isSchoolMealProgram` (boolean or null/undefined). Each time the user changes the view mode or any filter, the page updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new state and then re-runs the relevant data calls (`getProgramsOverviewContent` for Overview, `getProgramFilterOptions` and `searchPrograms` for Programs). No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used; all persistence of preference happens via the `ProgramsPageViewPreference` entity managed by `getProgramsPageViewPreference` and `setProgramsPageViewPreference`, and all shareable state is encoded in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Central initialization and state-orchestration component for the page. On load, it reads URL parameters (`view`, `programType`, `regionCode`, `minChildrenServed`), retrieves stored view preference using `getProgramsPageViewPreference`, determines the active view, and triggers the appropriate data fetching (`getProgramsOverviewContent` for Overview or `getProgramFilterOptions` plus `searchPrograms` for Programs). It also exposes callbacks to update URL parameters and re-fetch data when child components notify about state changes.
event_handlers: onMount: Read URL query parameters; call `getProgramsPageViewPreference`; determine initial view and trigger either overview or programs data loading.

onViewChange(newView): Called by the view-mode-switcher when user switches view; calls `setProgramsPageViewPreference` with `view=newView`, updates `view` URL parameter, and either loads overview content or programs content as needed.

onFiltersChange(newFilters): Called by the program-filters-bar when filters change; updates URL parameters (`programType`, `regionCode`, `minChildrenServed`), maps any relevant filter state to the `isSchoolMealProgram` flag, and calls `searchPrograms` with the updated filters.
onUrlChangeDetected: If the implementation supports reacting to manual URL edits or back/forward navigation, parses the URL, updates internal view/filter state, and re-fetches data accordingly.

components.functionality: Allows the user to switch between Overview view and Programs view. Reflects the current view mode and notifies the page-init-controller when the user changes it.
event_handlers: onToggleToOverview: Invoked when the user selects Overview view. Calls a controller callback that triggers `setProgramsPageViewPreference` with `view='overview'`, updates the `view` URL parameter, and switches rendered content to the overview components.

onToggleToPrograms: Invoked when the user selects Programs view. Calls a controller callback that triggers `setProgramsPageViewPreference` with `view='programs'`, updates the `view` URL parameter, and switches rendered content to the programs components.

components.functionality: Displays the organization’s overarching work and program areas in Overview view. It renders the narrative overview text, program area summaries grouped by programType, and impact statistics provided by `getProgramsOverviewContent`.
event_handlers: onMountWhenVisible: When the active view switches to Overview and the component mounts (or when the page first loads directly into Overview view), call `getProgramsOverviewContent` if the data has not already been retrieved.
onRefreshRequested: Optional event to re-fetch overview content by calling `getProgramsOverviewContent` again (for example, via a user-triggered refresh button if implemented).

components.functionality: Renders filter controls for the programs directory view, including program type selection, region selection, minimum children served selector, and a control that can narrow results to school meal programs via the `isSchoolMealProgram` parameter. It initializes its values from URL parameters and notifies the controller whenever filters change.
event_handlers: onMountWhenVisible: When Programs view becomes active, call `getProgramFilterOptions` (if not already called) and initialize filter controls using the returned programTypes, regions, and minChildrenServedOptions. Also read current URL parameters to set initial selections.

onProgramTypeChange(selectedProgramType): Update internal filter state for programType, update the `programType` URL parameter, and notify the page-init-controller via `onFiltersChange` so it can call `searchPrograms` with the new value.
onRegionChange(selectedRegionCode): Update internal filter state for regionCode, update the `regionCode` URL parameter, and notify the controller so it can call `searchPrograms` with the new filters.

onMinChildrenServedChange(selectedMinChildrenServed): Update internal filter state for minChildrenServed, update the `minChildrenServed` URL parameter, and notify the controller so it can call `searchPrograms`.

onSchoolMealProgramToggle(isOnlySchoolMeals): Update internal filter state that maps to the `isSchoolMealProgram` parameter for `searchPrograms`. Although this flag is not directly represented as a URL parameter in the specification, the implementation can either map it to a custom URL parameter or infer it from a selected programType as long as the mapping is consistent. After updating, notify the controller to call `searchPrograms` with the updated flag.
onResetFilters: Clear internal filter state for all filters, remove related URL parameters, and notify the controller so it can call `searchPrograms` with unfiltered or default parameters.

components.functionality: Displays the list or grid of programs returned from `searchPrograms` according to the current filter state. Each item in the list is rendered as a program card with key data and actions to view details or donate.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdated(programs): Called by the controller when `searchPrograms` returns. Updates the internal list of Program objects and triggers re-rendering of the program cards.

onEmptyStateRender: If the `searchPrograms` result is an empty array, render an appropriate empty-state message and controls (e.g., a reset filters action that triggers program-filters-bar.onResetFilters).

components.functionality: Represents a single Program in the results list. Shows key fields from the Program entity (such as name, shortDescription, regionCode, programType, childrenServedTotal, and imageUrl when provided) and offers actions to view the program detail page or navigate directly to the donation page for that program.
event_handlers: onClickCard(programId): Navigates to `program_detail.html?programId={programId}` using the Program.id from the bound Program object.
onClickViewDetails(programId): Explicit action to open the program detail page, also navigating to `program_detail.html?programId={programId}`.
onClickDonate(programId): Navigates to `donate.html?tab=one_time&programId={programId}&donationType=program_restricted` using the Program.id as `programId`.
onKeyActivate(programId): Keyboard activation handler (e.g., Enter/Space) which triggers the same navigation as onClickCard for accessibility.

components.functionality: Encapsulates all functionality for Programs view, including the filters bar and the results list. It coordinates between filters and the page-init-controller to ensure that `searchPrograms` is called whenever filter state changes and that results are passed down to the program-results-list.
event_handlers: onEnterProgramsView: When the active view switches to Programs, ensure `getProgramFilterOptions` has been called, initialize filters from URL, and call `searchPrograms` with the derived filter state.

onFiltersChangeFromBar(newFilters): Receive normalized filters from program-filters-bar, pass them to the page-init-controller or directly call `searchPrograms` (depending on architecture), and pass the returned programs to program-results-list.

onSearchProgramsError(error): Handle any error from `searchPrograms` by rendering an error message and optionally exposing a retry action that re-invokes `searchPrograms` with the current filter state.

components.functionality: Encapsulates all functionality for Overview view. Manages the retrieval and rendering of overview content via `getProgramsOverviewContent` and passes data to the overview-content-section.
event_handlers: onEnterOverviewView: When the active view switches to Overview, check whether overview data is already available; if not, call `getProgramsOverviewContent` and provide the result to overview-content-section.
onReloadOverview: Optional event for manually refreshing overview data by calling `getProgramsOverviewContent` again.

components.functionality: Responsible for reading and writing URL query parameters to keep them aligned with in-memory view and filter state. It provides utility methods to serialize and deserialize filters and view mode into/from the URL, without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse existing URL parameters into a state object (view and filters) and provide this to the page-init-controller during page initialization.
onStateChange(newState): When view or filters change in in-memory state, update the URL query string accordingly (e.g., using history API to avoid full reloads).

onPopState: When the browser back/forward navigation occurs, re-parse the URL parameters and notify the page-init-controller so it can realign view and filters and trigger the appropriate data fetching.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: global header and hero sit above, then this page’s main content begins with a shallow, full‑width section that mirrors the hero’s two‑column composition. In that section, the left side holds the page title and brief context, while the right side holds the view‑mode switcher as the primary control, echoing the donate stack placement in the mockup. Below this, content is view‑driven: either the overview view or the programs directory view is shown, never both. The overview view uses a two‑column layout (content narrative on the left, supporting visuals or stats on the right), while the programs view uses a vertical stack: filters bar at the top, then the results list composed of repeating program cards in a responsive grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: global header and hero sit above, then this page’s main content begins with a shallow, full‑width section that mirrors the hero’s two‑column composition. In that section, the left side holds the page title and brief context, while the right side holds the view‑mode switcher as the primary control, echoing the donate stack placement in the mockup. Below this, content is view‑driven: either the overview view or the programs directory view is shown, never both. The overview view uses a two‑column layout (content narrative on the left, supporting visuals or stats on the right), while the programs view uses a vertical stack: filters bar at the top, then the results list composed of repeating program cards in a responsive grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header still occupies ~10–15% of viewport height and the hero occupies the remaining ~85–90%, as in the mockup. The custom components for this page all live in the main content area directly beneath the hero, which uses a constrained content width (~70–80% of the viewport, mapped to a 12‑column grid). Within this main area, the initial band with the page title and view switcher is about 15–20% of the first screen of scrollable content. Its left column takes up roughly 60–65% of the content width; the right column (view switcher) takes ~35–40%. In the overview view, the narrative column is about 2/3 width and the supporting column about 1/3. In the programs view, the filters bar spans nearly 100% of the content width, with the results list filling the remainder of the vertical space. Program cards are arranged 3‑up on large desktop (each ~30–32% width with gutters), 2‑up on tablet, and 1‑up on mobile, always within the constrained content width.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate density: few high‑value controls and clear groupings rather than dense text. Each major block (view switcher band, overview text area, filters bar, results grid) is separated by generous vertical spacing of 24–32px internally and 48–64px between sections. Interactive elements (view toggle, filters, cards) have generous padding and are visually separated with white or very dark surfaces against color accents, echoing the mockup’s treatment of the donate CTA and trust badges on a darkened photo. The result is a clear hierarchy where page mode switching, filtering, and program selection are easy to spot without visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. In the main content, the eye starts at the top‑left on the page title and short description, then moves horizontally right to the brightly styled view‑mode switcher (which visually replaces the mockup’s right‑aligned donate block). From there, the gaze drops diagonally into the primary content: either the left‑aligned overview narrative or the top of the programs list. In the programs view, attention then moves rightward across the filters bar and down through the grid of program cards. Any secondary info within cards is scanned vertically. Persistent controls like the sticky filters bar (on scroll) provide a secondary path, analogous to the mockup’s vertical social bar: always visible, but clearly subordinate to the primary mode and program selection actions.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing global header and full‑width hero, a centered content column appears, aligned to the same 12‑column grid the navigation and hero content use. At the very top of this main area, a shallow band introduces the page: on the left, the words “Our Work & Programs” and a one‑ or two‑line description sit against a clean, white or very light background; on the right, a colorful, pill‑styled view switcher invites visitors to toggle between an Overview and a Programs Directory view. This band stretches across most of the viewport width but leaves comfortable horizontal margins on either side, echoing the constrained hero content. Beneath it, only one view is visible at a time. In Overview mode, the content area splits into two columns: a broad left column where the organization’s story, impact narrative, and key imagery stack vertically with generous breathing room, and a narrower right column for supporting blocks such as stats, highlights, or mini cards, all using playful accent colors reminiscent of the mockup’s trust badges. In Programs mode, that same space reorganizes into a vertical flow: a full‑width filters bar sits at the top, with pill‑like filters and a search input arranged in a tight horizontal rhythm, followed by a responsive grid of program cards laid out in rows with clear gaps between them. The filters bar becomes sticky just under the main content’s top edge as the user scrolls, while the cards create a rhythmic pattern of white rectangles on the darkened or neutral background, each with a bright CTA that echoes the hero’s bold buttons. The overall effect is a modern nonprofit interface that feels structured like the mockup’s hero—strong left‑anchored content, high‑impact controls on the right, and a clear priority path—translated into a functional, navigable directory experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non‑visual, structural component that initializes the page state before anything is rendered. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the main content area directly beneath the hero, but it does not occupy visible space or alter layout. All other visible components (the view‑mode switcher band, overview wrapper, and programs wrapper) are rendered under its control along the vertical flow of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑rendered; no spatial footprint. It only affects which view wrapper is mounted and how initial mode and filters are set.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view‑mode switcher is placed at the top of the main content area in a shallow horizontal band that spans roughly 80–90% of the page width and is centered within the viewport. Within this band, the switcher sits in the right column, aligned to the right edge of the constrained content. It occupies about 35–40% of the content width, with its left edge roughly aligned to the 8th column of a 12‑column grid. Visually, it presents as a bold, pill‑like segmented control (e.g., “Overview” and “Programs”) with a bright brand color background for the active state (often yellow or blue, echoing the hero’s donate button) and a subtle border or muted background for the inactive option. Vertical padding is generous (8–12px) and horizontal padding per option is 16–24px, creating a large click target. There is approximately 16–24px of whitespace above and below this control within its band, and at least 24–32px of separation below before the start of the content view wrappers. On smaller screens, the switcher drops to full width and is centered beneath the title, but remains in the topmost portion of the content area, preserving its primary role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top content band; it scrolls away with the page, but because it’s near the top, users can easily scroll back to change modes. It does not overlap other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview content section appears only when the Overview mode is active. It is positioned immediately below the view‑mode switcher band, separated from it by about 32–48px of vertical whitespace. The section is contained within the same centered content width (80–90% of the viewport) and uses a two‑column layout reminiscent of the mockup’s hero: the left column is dominant, taking approximately 65–70% of the width (spanning around 8 of 12 grid columns), while the right column takes the remaining 30–35% (about 4 grid columns). The left column begins near the top of the section and stacks elements such as headings, short paragraphs, and a key image or illustrative graphic, with 24–32px spacing between blocks. The right column is aligned to the top of the left column and contains one or more vertically stacked highlight blocks (e.g., key metrics, featured programs, or impact stats) that visually echo the mockup’s trust cards: cardlike, with 16–24px internal padding, subtle drop shadows or borders, and playful accent colors. There is a clear gutter of 24–32px between the two columns. At the bottom of the overview section, a 48–64px vertical gap separates it from whatever content follows (e.g., footer or subsequent sections), keeping the density comfortable. On tablet and mobile, the columns stack, with the left (narrative) content appearing first and the right (highlights) content directly below, each spanning nearly full width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow. It expands vertically according to the amount of overview content, always maintaining the left‑dominant column proportions on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program filters bar is the topmost element of the Programs view and appears immediately below the view‑mode switcher band when Programs mode is active, replacing the overview section. It stretches across almost the full content width (80–90% of the viewport, aligned to the same grid as the overview) and is visually grouped as a single horizontal band with a light background, subtly raised or bordered to distinguish it from the results list below. Internally, its contents are arranged from left to right: on the left two‑thirds, a row of pill‑shaped filter chips (e.g., by region, age group, program type) aligned to the left, each with 8–12px vertical and 16px horizontal padding and 8–16px horizontal gaps; on the right one‑third, a search field or dropdowns are right‑aligned. The bar’s height is roughly 56–72px, but it can grow if filters wrap onto a second line. There is about 16–24px of spacing above and below the filters bar relative to neighboring elements, creating a clear separation from the view‑mode switcher above and the results list below. The colorful chips and icons echo the hero’s payment icons and trust badges—small, vivid elements against a clean strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the programs view: after the user scrolls past its initial position, the filters bar remains pinned just below the global header/hero (or at the top of the viewport) so that filtering controls stay visible while the user scrolls through program cards. It does not overlay cards; instead, the results list scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program results list sits directly beneath the program filters bar, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the same centered content width (80–90% of the viewport) and lays out the program cards in a responsive grid aligned to the implicit 12‑column system. On large desktop, cards are arranged in rows of three, each card spanning roughly 3–4 grid columns so that the group of three plus their gutters fills the content width. On medium screens, this collapses to two cards per row, and on small screens to a single card per row. Vertical spacing between rows of cards follows the 24–32px scale, with a bit more (32–40px) at the transition between larger logical groupings if grouping is needed. The list itself does not have a visible container border; instead, the white cards float on a neutral background, similar to the hero’s foreground elements on a darkened image. At the bottom of the list, a load‑more button or pagination controls (if present) are centered horizontally with generous vertical padding (16–24px) and at least 48–64px of whitespace before the footer, maintaining the moderate density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content below the sticky filters bar. As new results are loaded or filters change, cards reflow in place within the grid, maintaining consistent gutters and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program card is a rectangular, clickable tile within the program results grid. On large desktop, it occupies about 30–32% of the content width (one‑third of a row with horizontal gutters), and full width of its grid column on smaller screens. The card has a white or very light background with subtle rounding (4–8px radius) and a soft shadow or border to lift it off the background, echoing the trust cards in the mockup. Internal padding is generous—typically 16–24px on all sides. Inside, content is stacked vertically: a small colored label or tag at the top (e.g., program type) in a vibrant brand color; below that, the program title in a bold, legible type; then a short 1–2 line description or key details; and finally, at the bottom, a horizontal row that includes secondary info (location, age range) on the left and a primary CTA button or link (e.g., “View Program” or “Donate to This Program”) on the right. The CTA adopts the same bright, kid‑centric styling as the hero’s donate button (often a bold yellow or blue with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding). Vertical spacing between these internal elements follows the 8–16px increments, leaving comfortable breathing room but keeping the card compact enough for scanning in a grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results grid, responding to hover or focus with a subtle elevation or border color shift. The card height is flexible depending on content, but cards in the same row should align their top edges and, where possible, approximate similar heights for visual rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The programs view wrapper is the container that holds the entire Programs directory experience. It sits directly beneath the view‑mode switcher band and occupies the full width of the main content area (80–90% of viewport width, centered). When Programs mode is active, this wrapper is visible and arranges its children in a vertical stack: the program filters bar at the top, then the program results list below, each separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing. The wrapper’s top aligns vertically with where the overview content would begin in Overview mode, so toggling between modes feels like swapping panels in the same spatial slot, not jumping the layout. The overall vertical extent of this wrapper is determined by the number of rows of program cards, potentially extending well beyond the initial viewport height, with consistent background and padding around its content to maintain the mockup’s sense of a structured, central content area on top of a full‑width page background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Only one of the two view wrappers (overview or programs) is visible at any time. This wrapper is static in the flow, but its child filters bar is sticky as described. It is hidden or collapsed (display: none) when Overview mode is active to avoid taking up space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview view wrapper is the container for all overview‑mode content, with the overview content section as its primary child. It sits directly beneath the view‑mode switcher band in the same vertical position used by the programs view wrapper, sharing the same centered content width and horizontal margins. When Overview mode is active, this wrapper is visible and presents the overview content section laid out in its two‑column arrangement. It may also include optional secondary elements (e.g., a small CTA row) stacked beneath the main overview content, but all remain within the same overall width and spacing rules. Vertical spacing at the top and bottom of the wrapper (around 32–48px) ensures separation from the mode switcher above and any subsequent sections or the footer below, mirroring the moderate density from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the programs view wrapper; it is only visible when Overview mode is active. Static in the document flow, expanding in height based on its content, and hidden (display: none) when Programs mode is selected so that it does not affect layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is an invisible utility component that synchronizes the current view mode (Overview vs Programs) and filter settings with the URL. Conceptually, it is attached to the page at the same hierarchy level as the page‑init controller or view wrappers, but it does not render any visible elements or occupy space. It supports deep‑linking and back/forward navigation without affecting layout, keeping the visual structure entirely governed by the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual; no spatial behavior. It listens for state changes from the view‑mode switcher and filters and updates the URL accordingly, and initializes the correct view on load via the page‑init controller.

name: Program Detail
title: Program Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a specific child hunger relief program, allows users to request an information kit, and provides a direct path to donate to the program.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific program based on the URL parameter programId using getProgramDetails.
Present program objectives, activities, region, and program-level impact metrics such as children served and impact-per-donation figures.
Display impact stories associated with the program using the Program.impactStories field.

Provide a 'Request Info Kit' form tied to the current program, including delivery method options that map to the InfoKitRequest.deliveryMethod enum (including the combined postal mail plus email option).
Collect required contact information (full name, email, postal mailing address fields) and submit info kit requests using requestProgramInfoKit.
Show request submission status and feedback (success or failure) based on the response from requestProgramInfoKit.
Offer a contextual 'Donate to this program' call-to-action that navigates to donate.html with URL parameters tab=one_time, programId set from the current page, and donationType=program_restricted.
Provide a 'Back to all programs' link that navigates to our_work.html with view=programs to return to the programs listing.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for program data when getProgramDetails fails or when programId is missing or invalid in the URL.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View a program’s details: (1) User navigates to program_detail.html with a programId URL parameter from index.html, our_work.html, or donate.html. (2) On page load, the page reads programId from the URL and triggers getProgramDetails(programId). (3) While data is loading, a loading state is shown and all dependent components stay disabled. (4) On success, program information (name, descriptions, programType, regionCode, childrenServedTotal, childrenFedPer10, impactStories, imageUrl) is bound to the corresponding components. (5) If getProgramDetails fails or returns no usable data, an error state is displayed and primary actions such as 'Donate to this program' and 'Request Info Kit' are disabled or hidden until a valid programId is available.

Workflow 2 – Request an information kit: (1) With the program details successfully loaded, the user scrolls to the info kit request form. (2) The form is pre-associated with the current programId from the URL (no separate user selection is required). (3) The user selects a delivery method option corresponding to an InfoKitRequest.deliveryMethod value, including the option that represents receiving materials via both postal mail and email. (4) The user enters fullName, email, addressLine1, optional addressLine2, city, state, postalCode, and country. All required fields must be valid before submission is enabled. (5) The user submits the form, which triggers requestProgramInfoKit with programId from the URL and the entered form values. (6) While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown. (7) On success (success=true in the response), the page stores the returned InfoKitRequest object in local page state (not in URL or localStorage) and shows a success message or confirmation area bound to that object. (8) On failure (success=false or network error), an error message is shown and the user can correct inputs and retry submission.

Workflow 3 – Donate directly to the program: (1) After reviewing program details, the user clicks the 'Donate to this program' button. (2) The click handler reads the current programId from the URL or from the loaded Program object. (3) The page navigates to donate.html with URL parameters tab=one_time, programId set to the current programId value, and donationType=program_restricted. (4) The donation page uses these parameters to preselect the program and donation context.

Workflow 4 – Return to all programs: (1) From the program detail page, the user clicks a 'Back to all programs' link. (2) The click handler navigates to our_work.html with the URL parameter view=programs. (3) The Programs page reads view=programs and shows the programs listing view.

Workflow 5 – Handle invalid or missing programId: (1) If the page is loaded without a programId parameter, or if getProgramDetails(programId) returns an error, the data loader component sets an error state. (2) The page displays an appropriate error message and hides or disables the info kit form and donate CTA because they rely on a valid program. (3) The user may still use the 'Back to all programs' navigation link to return to our_work.html?view=programs to choose another program.
interactions: On initial page load, read the programId value from the URL query string and use it to call getProgramDetails(programId).
During data loading, display a loading indicator in place of program content and disable interactive components that depend on program data.
On successful getProgramDetails response, bind the returned Program object to all program detail components, including summary, objectives/description, impact metrics, and impact stories.
On getProgramDetails error, set a page-level error state so that an error message component is shown and dependent components either show fallback messaging or are hidden/disabled.

When the user clicks the 'Donate to this program' button, trigger navigation to donate.html with the query parameters tab=one_time, programId equal to the current programId, and donationType=program_restricted.
When the user clicks the 'Back to all programs' link, trigger navigation to our_work.html with the query parameter view=programs.

When the user changes the selected delivery method in the info kit form, update local form state and ensure the selection maps to a valid InfoKitRequest.deliveryMethod value (including the postal mail plus email option).

On each keystroke or blur event in the info kit form fields (fullName, email, addressLine1, addressLine2, city, state, postalCode, country), update local form state and validate required fields and formats (e.g., email format).

When the user submits the info kit request form, prevent default browser submission, validate all required fields, and call requestProgramInfoKit with programId from the URL and all form field values.
While requestProgramInfoKit is in progress, disable the submit button and show a submitting state to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful info kit request submission (success=true), store the returned InfoKitRequest object in component state and display a confirmation message or summary of the request; optionally clear or lock the form fields.
On failed info kit request submission (success=false or network error), display an error message bound to the error state and re-enable the submit button so the user can retry.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL parameter programId. On every page load, the program-detail data loader component reads programId from the query string of program_detail.html. If programId is present, getProgramDetails(programId) is called and its response (a Program object) populates all dependent components (summary, descriptions, impact metrics, impact stories, donate CTA, and info kit form context). If programId is missing or invalid, a page-level error state is set, which suppresses actions that require a valid program (info kit submission, donate CTA) and surfaces error messaging instead. Navigation to donate.html is purely URL-based: when the user clicks the donate CTA, the page constructs a URL donate.html?tab=one_time&programId={currentProgramId}&donationType=program_restricted and navigates the browser there. Navigation back to the programs listing uses a URL our_work.html?view=programs. The info kit request does not alter the URL; its submission status is tracked in in-memory component state only. No state is persisted via localStorage or sessionStorage; all persistent state is implied by the URL parameters and by backend data returned from the two assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Reads the programId from the program_detail.html URL query string, calls getProgramDetails(programId), manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded Program object (or error) to child components.
Displays key information from the loaded Program object, such as the program name and high-level summary fields, and provides context that this page is for a specific program.
Shows the program’s objectives, long description, programType, and regionCode to help users understand what the program does and where it operates.

Displays numeric impact metrics from the Program object, including childrenServedTotal and childrenFedPer10, and any other quantitative fields returned by getProgramDetails, to demonstrate the program’s scale and effectiveness.
Iterates over Program.impactStories (if provided) and displays a structured list of impact stories associated with the program, enabling users to read about outcomes for children and communities.

Provides a 'Donate to this program' button that, when clicked, navigates to donate.html with URL parameters tab=one_time, programId (from the current Program or URL), and donationType=program_restricted so that the donation flow is preconfigured for this program.
Renders a link or button that navigates back to the programs listing by setting the browser location to our_work.html?view=programs when activated.

Collects user inputs for an information kit request tied to the current program, including delivery method selection, full name, email, and full postal mailing address. On submit, validates all required fields and calls requestProgramInfoKit with programId (from URL) and the entered data.

Listens for the result of requestProgramInfoKit and displays success or error messaging. On success, it can show key fields from the returned InfoKitRequest object (such as deliveryMethod and createdAt) to confirm that the request was received.
Shows an error message and fallback UI when getProgramDetails fails or when programId is missing or invalid, and encourages users to navigate back to the programs listing.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom flow as the mockup: global fixed‑height header at the very top (not part of this spec), immediately followed by a full‑width, photo‑driven program hero ('program-header'). Inside that hero, content is arranged in a two‑column layout: left for the large program title and summary, right for a vertically stacked donate CTA ('program-donate-cta'). Beneath the hero, the main content sections ('program-overview-section', 'program-impact-metrics', 'program-impact-stories-list', 'info-kit-request-form') are stacked vertically in a single main column, with generous separation between sections. Utility and state components ('back-to-programs-link', 'program-detail-data-loader', 'info-kit-request-feedback', 'program-detail-error-state') are layered into this structure without disturbing the primary two‑column hero pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom flow as the mockup: global fixed‑height header at the very top (not part of this spec), immediately followed by a full‑width, photo‑driven program hero ('program-header'). Inside that hero, content is arranged in a two‑column layout: left for the large program title and summary, right for a vertically stacked donate CTA ('program-donate-cta'). Beneath the hero, the main content sections ('program-overview-section', 'program-impact-metrics', 'program-impact-stories-list', 'info-kit-request-form') are stacked vertically in a single main column, with generous separation between sections. Utility and state components ('back-to-programs-link', 'program-detail-data-loader', 'info-kit-request-feedback', 'program-detail-error-state') are layered into this structure without disturbing the primary two‑column hero pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header consumes roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height; the 'program-header' fills the remaining 85–90% for the first screenful, mirroring the original hero behavior. Within the hero, about 55–60% of the width is devoted to the left column (program title and short context), 25–30% to the right column (donate CTA card), and the remaining ~10–15% is left as breathing room on the far right, visually echoing the mockup's social strip zone even if not directly populated by these components. Below the hero, the main content column (overview, metrics, stories, info kit) spans roughly 60–70% of the page width and is centered within the 12‑column grid, while the sticky donate CTA continues to occupy a narrower right rail (~25–30%) on larger screens. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → overview, overview → metrics, etc.) uses 48–64px, with 24–32px between sub-elements inside each section, consistent with the observed spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a moderate density, focusing the top of the page on a small set of critical elements: program name, high‑impact messaging, a dominant donate CTA, and a simple way to go back. The hero has limited text and large typography so it feels spacious despite the background image. Secondary sections (overview, metrics, stories, info kit form) are structured into digestible blocks with clear headings, cards, and generous internal padding (16–24px) so each piece of information has breathing room. The page avoids long, unbroken text columns by using cards and visual groupings in the metrics and stories sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup. The user’s eye starts at the top left global brand (in the header), moves horizontally across navigation to the global header CTA, then drops into the 'program-header' where the large, stacked program title on the left catches attention. From there, the gaze shifts to the right‑side 'program-donate-cta' card, which is bright and high contrast. After processing this primary CTA, the eye travels downward into the main content: first the 'program-overview-section' for context, then the bold 'program-impact-metrics', followed by the more narrative 'program-impact-stories-list'. As the user scrolls, the sticky donate CTA remains visible on the right, acting as a persistent, secondary focal point, while inline feedback ('info-kit-request-feedback') momentarily grabs attention only within the info kit form area.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s clean, white global header, the Program Detail page opens with a full‑width, photo‑driven hero for the specific program. A dark overlay softens the background image of children and volunteers so foreground content stands out. Within a centered, constrained content area, the left two‑thirds of this hero are dominated by a large, stacked program title and short supporting copy, echoing the bold, child‑centric logotype treatment from the mockup. At the right, a tall, bright yellow donate card sits as a vertical stack: a large primary “Donate to this Program” button, a short reassurance line, and room for small trust cues, occupying a narrow right rail that feels visually separate from the main text. The hero nearly fills the first viewport, with the next section just peeking at the fold to invite scrolling. As the user scrolls down, the background gives way to a clean white page where the program overview, impact metrics, and impact stories are arranged in a single central column, each section separated by generous 48–64px vertical gaps and subtle color accents. The donate card stays visible in a sticky right column on larger screens, echoing the mockup’s persistent right‑side CTA stack. Further down, a softly colored info‑kit request card spans most of the content width, with an inline notification bar that appears at the top of the card when the form is submitted. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this structure in a way that keeps the user centered in the viewport, preserving the overall modern, playful yet clean nonprofit aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When program data is loading, this component occupies the entire main content area from just below the global header down to the bottom of the viewport, using a full‑width, full‑height overlay that sits above all other Program Detail content. The overlay follows the hero’s visual bounds: the top aligns to the bottom edge of the site header, and the width spans edge to edge of the viewport. Within this overlay, a centered loading treatment sits approximately at the vertical and horizontal center of the viewport. To echo the eventual layout, the loader can present as a large, semi‑transparent skeleton block on the left (about 55–60% width, mimicking the hero title area) and a taller, narrower skeleton on the right (about 25–30% width, mimicking the donate CTA card), with additional horizontal skeleton lines below to suggest upcoming overview text. Generous 24–32px gaps between skeleton blocks keep the density light and convey the spacious, modern feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Covers the program detail region with a fixed-position overlay beneath the global header; blocks interaction with underlying components until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program header forms a full‑width hero section immediately beneath the global header, occupying roughly 85–90% of the initial viewport height. A hero image—ideally related to the specific program—is darkened with an overlay so foreground elements remain legible. Inside, content is constrained to a centered container that reflects a 12‑column grid and a max width of about 1120–1200px. Within this container, the left 7 columns (about 55–60% of the width) are dedicated to the core header content: the program title appears in a large, stacked style (e.g., two to three short lines) centered vertically in the hero’s lower half, mimicking the bold ‘No / Child / Hungry’ treatment but adapted to the program name. Directly under the title, there is room for a one‑sentence summary and small metadata such as location or program category, arranged in a vertical stack with 8–16px spacing. The right 3–4 columns (about 25–30% of the width) are reserved for the donate CTA card component, which aligns vertically roughly with the middle of the program title block, creating a clear two‑column hero composition. There is intentional empty space (10–15% of total width) toward the far right edge of the viewport, keeping the right column visually framed and echoing the mockup’s margin near the social bar. Top and bottom padding inside the hero content container use 48–64px so the text and CTA never feel cramped against the edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page; initial height scales with viewport to keep a near full-screen hero on load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program overview section begins immediately below the hero, separated from it by 48–64px of vertical whitespace to clearly mark the transition from image‑driven hero to content body. The section sits on a white background and shares the same centered max‑width container as the hero for alignment. Horizontally, the overview is organized as a main text column occupying roughly 60–70% of the container width, aligned with the left edge of the hero’s title block, while the remaining width to the right acts as breathing room and aligns visually with the sticky donate CTA rail. At the top of the overview, a section heading and short intro line are stacked with 16–24px spacing. Below that, the main descriptive text, bullet lists, and any quick facts are arranged in vertical blocks with consistent 24px gaps, preventing the section from becoming text‑dense. The right side stays relatively open on larger screens, preserving the sense of a two‑column layout while ensuring the focus remains on the left narrative. The section’s bottom edge sits 48–64px above the next section (impact metrics).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block that scrolls normally; aligned to the same grid as the hero content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Immediately beneath the overview, the impact metrics section spans the full width of the content container, but is set off with a subtle tinted background band (e.g., a very light blue or yellow) extending full‑bleed or at least edge‑to‑edge within the constrained width to echo the colorful trust strip in the mockup. Vertical spacing above and below this band is 48–64px, giving it a strong but not overwhelming presence. Inside the band, metrics are arranged into a horizontal row of 3–4 metric tiles, each taking up roughly 20–25% of the container width with 24–32px horizontal gaps. Each tile is a compact card with 16–24px internal padding, a large numeric value at the top (e.g., number of meals served), a short label underneath, and optional microcopy below. The tiles align with the left main column and stop before the area occupied by the sticky donate CTA rail on the right, keeping that rail visually distinct. The overall effect is a low‑density, scannable row that quickly communicates impact without overcrowding the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal card row; it scrolls with the page and visually anchors the middle of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The impact stories list sits below the metrics, with 48–64px of vertical whitespace separating the two. It returns to a white background and the same centered content width as the overview, maintaining consistent grid alignment. At the top of this section, a heading and brief introduction are stacked with 16–24px spacing. Below, individual stories are presented as cards in a 2‑column grid: each card spans roughly 45–48% of the content width with a 24–32px gutter between columns, and they align underneath the main left column so that the right edges stop short of the sticky donate CTA area. Each story card includes a thumbnail image at the top (if available) and a text block below, with 16–24px internal padding to keep content airy. Vertical spacing between story rows is about 32px, echoing the moderate density of the mockup. The section can extend downward across multiple scroll lengths, but the layout ensures clear separation between stories and maintains visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid of cards that scrolls normally beneath the hero and metrics.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program donate CTA is a visually dominant card that first appears within the right column of the 'program-header' hero. In the hero, the card occupies roughly 25–30% of the content container width and is vertically aligned so that its top edge is near the vertical midpoint of the hero, drawing the eye from the left program title across to the right CTA. The card has a bright yellow background (or equivalent brand accent), rounded corners, and 24px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, elements are stacked vertically: a bold heading or label at the top (e.g., 'Support This Program'), followed by a large, pill‑like primary donate button that spans almost the full card width, then one or two lines of brief reassurance, and optionally small trust cues (e.g., small icons or text about secure payments) arranged horizontally or in a compact stack below with 8–12px spacing. The card visually echoes the dominant yellow PayPal donate block from the mockup. As the user scrolls past the hero, this donate card transitions into a sticky right‑rail element: it maintains its width and positioning relative to the right edge of the content container, remaining visible along the right side of the viewport while the main content (overview, metrics, stories, info kit) scrolls on the left. The top of the sticky card pins to a comfortable offset below the global header (e.g., 64–80px) to avoid overlap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially static within the hero’s right column, then becomes a sticky right-rail card that stays fixed within the viewport once scrolled into position, until the bottom of the main content is reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back‑to‑programs link is a small, tertiary navigation element positioned within the 'program-header' hero, anchored at the top left of the hero’s content container. It sits above or slightly to the upper left of the main program title block, with about 16–24px of vertical offset from the top boundary of the hero content area and 16–24px of horizontal inset from the left content edge. The link is rendered as a subtle text link with a left‑pointing arrow icon, and it occupies only a single line, ensuring it doesn’t compete visually with the title. A small buffer of 16–24px separates it from the top of the title to maintain clarity. As the user scrolls, the link moves with the hero content and disappears once the hero is out of view, reinforcing its role as an entry‑level control rather than a global navigation element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; scrolls away with the hero content and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The info kit request form appears after the impact stories section, separated from it by 48–64px of vertical whitespace, creating a distinct, action‑oriented zone near the lower half of the page. It is presented as a wide card centered within the same content container as the other sections, spanning approximately 60–70% of the container width so it feels substantial yet not edge‑to‑edge. The card uses a soft brand‑tinted background (e.g., pale blue or lime) and rounded corners, with generous internal padding of 24–32px. At the top of the card, a short explanatory heading and one or two lines of body text are stacked with 16–24px spacing. Below, the form fields are arranged in a single column for clarity, each field spanning the full width of the card, with 16–24px vertical spacing between fields. The submit button is placed at the bottom, full‑width or nearly full‑width, with ample top margin (24px) and clear contrast. The card aligns with the left main content column, and its right edge comfortably clears the sticky donate CTA rail, maintaining visual hierarchy: the donate card remains on the right as a consistent option, while the info kit request is framed as a secondary, but still prominent, action within the main flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the main content column; scrolls normally and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The info kit request feedback is an inline notification bar that appears associated with the info kit request form, never detached from it. When active (after submission or on error), it sits at the top of the info kit card, directly above the form fields and immediately below the card’s heading or descriptive text. It spans the full width of the card’s inner content area, matching the card’s padding on the left and right, and has a height defined by its content (typically one or two lines of text), with 12–16px vertical padding. The background is a solid brand color (e.g., a soft green for success, a soft orange/red for errors), and the text is high‑contrast and concise. There is 12–16px of space between this notification bar and the nearest elements above and below, so it doesn’t feel cramped. The notification scrolls with the form; if the user moves away from the form section, it leaves the viewport along with the card, preserving the local, contextual feel of the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the info kit card; appears and disappears in place without affecting the global scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs and program data cannot be loaded, this component replaces the entire Program Detail content area beginning just below the global header. Instead of the hero and subsequent sections, the user sees a clean, centered error layout on a white or very light background. The error content is confined to a central card or block that spans roughly 40–50% of the page width, centered horizontally, and positioned vertically around the middle of the viewport (with a slight upward bias to feel more connected to the header). Inside this block, an icon or illustration can sit at the top, followed by a bold error title, a short explanatory message, and one or two actions (e.g., a button to go back to the program list and maybe a secondary link to retry). Vertical spacing of 16–24px between these elements keeps the density comfortable. The rest of the page around this block is intentionally empty, echoing the spacious hero approach and ensuring the error state is clearly understandable and not cluttered by partial or failed content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the main program content; occupies the primary content region beneath the global header and scrolls as a single, self-contained view if taller than the viewport.

name: Volunteer Opportunities
title: Volunteer Opportunities
description: Search and filter volunteer opportunities by location, distance, date, day of week, level of need, activity type, and sort order, then navigate to individual event pages to register.

core_features: Initialize filter controls (distance, quick date ranges, days of week, need levels) using getVolunteerFilterOptions.
Read incoming URL parameters (zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, sortBy) to pre-populate filters and initial search state.
Provide a location search with ZIP code and radius filters bound to searchVolunteerOpportunities parameters zipCode and radiusMiles.
Offer date filtering using quick date ranges (dateRangeCode mapped to startDate/endDate) and optional custom date range, passed as startDate and endDate to searchVolunteerOpportunities.
Allow day-of-week filtering using values from getVolunteerFilterOptions.daysOfWeek, mapped to the dayOfWeek parameter for searchVolunteerOpportunities.
Provide a need level filter (including a high option) using getVolunteerFilterOptions.needLevels, mapped to the needLevel parameter.
Support an activity type filter mapped to the activityType parameter, using values derived dynamically from the activityType field in VolunteerOpportunity search results.
Enable sorting of results (e.g., by date ascending) using a sortBy control mapped directly to searchVolunteerOpportunities.sortBy.
Display search results as a list of cards, each card bound to a VolunteerOpportunity record from searchVolunteerOpportunities.
Navigate to volunteer_event.html with volunteerOpportunityId set to the selected VolunteerOpportunity.id when a card is clicked.
Keep filters and sorting synchronized with URL parameters so that the current search is shareable and back/forward navigation restores state.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Find nearby high-need weekend volunteer shifts
1. User navigates to volunteer.html via site navigation (no URL params) or a basic link.
2. On page load, the page calls getVolunteerFilterOptions with no parameters to fetch distanceOptionsMiles, quickDateRanges, daysOfWeek, and needLevels.
3. The page reads any existing URL parameters; in this workflow, there are none, so default filter values are set (e.g., blank ZIP, default radiusMiles from the first distance option, no dateRangeCode, no dayOfWeek, no needLevel, no activityType, default sortBy like date_asc).
4. User enters a ZIP code in the location field and selects a radius from the distance options.
5. User selects a quick date range corresponding to an upcoming period (e.g., next month) and chooses a weekend dayOfWeek option from the list returned by getVolunteerFilterOptions (e.g., 'saturday' or 'sunday').
6. User selects a high-need filter value from needLevels (the option with code 'high').
7. User optionally selects an activityType from the dropdown populated with unique activityType values found in the current result set (this can be empty initially and update after each search).
8. User clicks the search or apply filters action, triggering construction of a URL query string with zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, and sortBy, and an invocation of searchVolunteerOpportunities using those values plus the computed startDate and endDate.
9. The page displays a list of VolunteerOpportunity cards for matching shifts; each card shows key information (bound fields like title, startDateTime, city/state, needLevel, and activityType) without hardcoding any specific values.
10. User clicks a specific opportunity card, which navigates to volunteer_event.html?volunteerOpportunityId=<VolunteerOpportunity.id>.

Workflow: Adjust search radius and sort while preserving other filters
1. User arrives on volunteer.html with existing filters active (either from a previous search or a shared URL with parameters like zipCode, needLevel, and dayOfWeek).
2. On load, the page reads URL parameters and sets the filter controls accordingly, then calls getVolunteerFilterOptions and searchVolunteerOpportunities with the derived arguments.
3. User modifies only the radiusMiles dropdown while leaving zipCode, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek, needLevel, and activityType unchanged.
4. User changes sortBy to another sort option (e.g., still date-based, but possibly reversed or with different logic) using the sort control.
5. On radiusMiles or sortBy change, the page updates the URL query string (replacing radiusMiles and sortBy but preserving all other parameters) and calls searchVolunteerOpportunities again with the updated radiusMiles and sortBy.
6. The results list refreshes to show the updated set/order of VolunteerOpportunity cards; the user can compare options without losing their previous filter selections.

Workflow: Deep-linked high-need Saturday meal-packing opportunities
1. User clicks a promotional link from index.html that leads to volunteer.html with specific query parameters, such as zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek='saturday', needLevel='high', activityType='meal_packing', and sortBy='date_asc'.
2. On page load, volunteer.html parses these URL parameters and applies them as the initial filter state.
3. The page calls getVolunteerFilterOptions to populate dropdowns and buttons; it then sets the selected entries matching the incoming parameters (e.g., selects the high need level, Saturday, the corresponding quick date range if present, the given radius, and leaves the ZIP field prefilled with zipCode).
4. The page computes startDate and endDate from dateRangeCode (using the options from quickDateRanges) and invokes searchVolunteerOpportunities with zipCode, radiusMiles, startDate, endDate, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, and sortBy from the URL.
5. VolunteerOpportunity results are rendered immediately, so the user sees a curated list of high-need Saturday meal-packing opportunities near the specified ZIP without having to adjust filters.
6. If the user changes any filter, the URL is updated to reflect the new state, and searchVolunteerOpportunities is called again, allowing the user to refine the deep-linked search.
interactions:
On page load (onMount / onInit):
  - Read zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, and sortBy from the URL query string.
  - Call getVolunteerFilterOptions to retrieve distanceOptionsMiles, quickDateRanges, daysOfWeek, and needLevels.
  - Derive initial UI selections from URL parameters and available options (e.g., match radiusMiles to one of distanceOptionsMiles, match dateRangeCode to one of quickDateRanges).
  - Compute startDate and endDate (if dateRangeCode is present) using quickDateRanges metadata and call searchVolunteerOpportunities with the resolved parameters.

Location input and radius selection:
  - onChange(zipCode field): Update in-memory filter state; do not necessarily trigger a search until user explicitly submits or an auto-search rule is defined.
  - onChange(radiusMiles dropdown): Update filter state; optionally trigger immediate search or wait for explicit apply, depending on chosen interaction model.
  - onSubmit(search form): Serialize filter state (including zipCode and radiusMiles) into URL query parameters and call searchVolunteerOpportunities with these values and any existing date/day/need/activity/sort filters.

Date and day-of-week filters:
  - onClick(quickDateRange button or dropdown item): Update selected dateRangeCode in state, recompute startDate and endDate from quickDateRanges, update URL (adding/updating dateRangeCode and removing explicit startDate/endDate if they are not used directly in the URL), then call searchVolunteerOpportunities.
  - onChange(custom date range inputs, if implemented): Set explicit startDate and endDate in state, clear dateRangeCode if necessary, update URL with startDate and endDate parameters (in a consistent format), then call searchVolunteerOpportunities.
  - onChange(dayOfWeek control): Update dayOfWeek in state, write dayOfWeek to URL, then call searchVolunteerOpportunities.

Need level and activity type filters:
  - onChange(needLevel control): Update needLevel in state (e.g., high/medium/low based on codes from getVolunteerFilterOptions.needLevels), sync to URL parameter needLevel, then call searchVolunteerOpportunities with the updated needLevel.
  - onChange(activityType control): Options are derived from distinct activityType values present in current search results; when the selection changes, update activityType in state and URL, then call searchVolunteerOpportunities with the chosen activityType.

Sorting and result list interactions:
  - onChange(sortBy dropdown): Update sortBy in state and URL, then call searchVolunteerOpportunities with the updated sortBy while preserving all other filter parameters.
  - onClick(volunteer opportunity card): Read the bound VolunteerOpportunity.id from the clicked card and navigate to volunteer_event.html?volunteerOpportunityId=<id>. No API is called on click; navigation is purely via URL.

URL and browser navigation:
  - onPopState (browser back/forward): When the URL changes via browser navigation, re-parse query parameters, update all filter control states to match, and call searchVolunteerOpportunities (and getVolunteerFilterOptions if not already loaded) so that visible results align with the URL.
  - onShareLinkCopy (if implemented as a separate action): Use the current window.location (which already encodes filters) as the shareable URL; no additional API calls are required.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters combined with data from getVolunteerFilterOptions. On initial load, the page parses zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, and sortBy from the URL (if present). These values are stored in an in-memory filter state object. getVolunteerFilterOptions is then called to retrieve distanceOptionsMiles, quickDateRanges, daysOfWeek, and needLevels. The UI binds to these options, selecting the entries that match the current filter state (for example, selecting the radiusMiles option equal to the radiusMiles query param, or the quickDateRanges entry whose code equals dateRangeCode). If dateRangeCode is present, the page uses the metadata from quickDateRanges to derive the actual startDate and endDate values passed to searchVolunteerOpportunities; if explicit startDate/endDate parameters are used instead, they override dateRangeCode. When the user interacts with filters or sorting, the filter state object is updated first, then rewritten to the URL as query parameters (replacing or removing parameters according to the interaction). After each state change that affects search criteria, searchVolunteerOpportunities is invoked with zipCode, radiusMiles, startDate, endDate, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, and sortBy derived from the current URL-synchronized filter state. The resulting array of VolunteerOpportunity records is used to render the results list. The activityType filter options are derived dynamically from distinct VolunteerOpportunity.activityType values in the current results, ensuring no hardcoded option values. Browser back/forward actions are handled by listening for URL changes (e.g., popstate), re-parsing the query parameters into state, updating UI controls, and re-calling searchVolunteerOpportunities so that displayed results always match the URL. Navigation to volunteer_event.html uses only the volunteerOpportunityId URL parameter, preserving deep linkability without relying on local storage.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, global filter state, and orchestrates calls to getVolunteerFilterOptions and searchVolunteerOpportunities. Provides shared context (current filters and results) to child components.

Calls getVolunteerFilterOptions on initialization to retrieve distanceOptionsMiles, quickDateRanges, daysOfWeek, and needLevels, then exposes these values to filter components and merges them with current filter state derived from URL parameters.

Provides inputs for zipCode and radiusMiles. Initializes values from URL parameters and available distanceOptionsMiles, updates the shared filter state and URL on submit or change, and triggers searchVolunteerOpportunities through the page shell.

Displays quick date range options (from quickDateRanges) and day-of-week options (from daysOfWeek). Maps user selection to dateRangeCode, startDate/endDate, and dayOfWeek in filter state, synchronizes these with URL parameters, and triggers searchVolunteerOpportunities when changed.

Renders need level options from getVolunteerFilterOptions.needLevels and an activityType selector whose options are derived dynamically from the unique activityType values in the current VolunteerOpportunity result set. Updates needLevel and activityType in filter state and URL, then triggers searchVolunteerOpportunities.

Provides a dropdown or toggle for selecting sortBy (e.g., date ascending or other supported sort codes). Uses the sortBy value from URL/filter state, updates it on change, rewrites the URL, and triggers searchVolunteerOpportunities with the new sortBy while preserving other filters.

Displays the array of VolunteerOpportunity records returned by searchVolunteerOpportunities as a list of child cards. Handles empty-state messaging when no results are returned, without hardcoded opportunity data.

Represents a single VolunteerOpportunity in the results list. Binds to fields such as id, title, startDateTime, endDateTime, timeZone, locationName, city, state, postalCode, needLevel, activityType, and tags, and exposes a click action that navigates to volunteer_event.html with volunteerOpportunityId set to the bound id.

Centralizes logic for reading and writing URL query parameters based on the current filter and sort state. Ensures that every relevant user interaction (filter change, sort change, form submit) updates URL parameters (zipCode, radiusMiles, dateRangeCode or startDate/endDate, dayOfWeek, needLevel, activityType, sortBy), and listens to browser navigation events to restore state and re-trigger searchVolunteerOpportunities.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: fixed‑height white header with centered navigation and a right‑aligned primary DONATE CTA, followed by a full‑width hero with a darkened volunteer photo background. Inside the hero, content uses a two‑column layout: left column is brand and page title ('No Child Hungry' + 'Volunteer Opportunities'), right column is a tall, narrow stack of search and filter controls (location/radius at the top, then date/day filters, then need/activity filters). Below the hero, a white content area holds the sort control and the vertical stack of volunteer result cards. A slim, fixed vertical social bar rides along the far‑right edge of the viewport, independent of the main grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: fixed‑height white header with centered navigation and a right‑aligned primary DONATE CTA, followed by a full‑width hero with a darkened volunteer photo background. Inside the hero, content uses a two‑column layout: left column is brand and page title ('No Child Hungry' + 'Volunteer Opportunities'), right column is a tall, narrow stack of search and filter controls (location/radius at the top, then date/day filters, then need/activity filters). Below the hero, a white content area holds the sort control and the vertical stack of volunteer result cards. A slim, fixed vertical social bar rides along the far‑right edge of the viewport, independent of the main grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On initial load, the header takes roughly 10–12% of the viewport height, the hero about 50–60%, and the top of the results area is visible just below the hero fold (hinting that more content continues). Horizontally within the hero’s constrained content width, about 60% is given to the left branding/title column and 25–30% to the right filters/search stack, with the remaining 10–15% reserved for the fixed social bar outside the main container. Below the hero, the results content spans the same constrained width, with full‑width sort controls on top and result cards stacked vertically. Generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separates hero and results; smaller spacing (16–24px) separates controls within the filter stack and between cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains moderate: a small number of high‑impact elements in the hero (logo, page title, three filter blocks), each with ample padding and clear separation from the background via overlays or cards. The filters are visually compact but not cramped, using pill‑like controls and clean grouping. The results list can contain many cards, but each card has generous internal padding and clear spacing between cards to prevent visual overload. There is breathing room above and around the primary search (location/radius) to keep it feeling like a standout action rather than just another form field.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the top‑left logo in the header, sweeps right across the navigation to the bright DONATE CTA. From there, it drops diagonally into the hero’s left center where the large 'No Child Hungry' logotype and 'Volunteer Opportunities' page title sit.
