The next strong pull is toward the right column’s bright, card‑like location/radius search block at the top of the filter stack (echoing the yellow donate button). From that block, the gaze flows downward through the date/day and need/activity filters, then continues down into the main content area where the sort control sits above the first result card. The fixed vertical social bar along the far right is discovered later as a tertiary element.

layout.overall_description: The Volunteer Opportunities page presents itself as an extension of the nonprofit’s bold, child‑focused homepage. A crisp white header spans the top, with the playful logo on the left, navigation centered, and a vivid yellow DONATE button on the far right. Below, a full‑width hero image of volunteers working with kids fills over half the initial viewport, darkened by a subtle overlay so UI elements stay legible. Centered within this hero is a constrained content area that adheres to a 12‑column grid: on the left, the oversized 'No Child Hungry' logotype stands tall, with a 'Volunteer Opportunities' title and one short supporting line sitting beneath it, grounded above the lower third of the hero. On the right, occupying roughly one quarter of the content width, a stacked column of cards floats over the background: at the top, a bright, high‑contrast location and radius search block; directly below it, two slimmer filter cards organize date/day and need/activity chips. Together, these echo the donation stack from the mockup, but tailored for finding volunteer roles. On the far‑right edge of the viewport, a narrow yellow vertical strip is fixed from just below the header to above the fold, hosting social icons in a single column independent of the main grid. Scrolling down, the hero yields to a clean white results area that maintains the same centered width: a compact row at the top houses the sort control on the right and optional result summary on the left, followed by a vertical column of volunteer opportunity cards. Each card is roomy, with clear hierarchy for title, date, location, level of need, and a primary sign‑up CTA, separated by consistent spacing to keep the list approachable even when long. The experience feels like a natural, practical extension of the hero: from an emotional, brand‑driven invitation down into a structured, easy‑to‑scan list of ways to help.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser width and height and anchors the overall structure. At the very top, it reserves a fixed‑height (roughly 72–96px, about 10–12% of viewport height) white header bar that is not part of this component list but it frames the content below: logo on the left, navigation items centered, and a right‑aligned yellow DONATE button. Directly beneath the header, the shell contains a hero section that occupies roughly 50–60% of the viewport height on desktop. This hero runs full width edge‑to‑edge, with a darkened photographic background and a semi‑transparent overlay. Centered within the hero is a constrained content container (around 1120–1200px wide) using an implicit 12‑column grid. Inside this container, the left 7–8 columns are left to the branding and page title stack, while the right 3–4 columns are dedicated to the vertical stack of search and filter components. The shell also provides a reserved area along the far‑right edge of the viewport, outside the main container, for a fixed vertical social bar: a 48–64px wide yellow strip aligned to the right edge and spanning from just below the header through most of the hero height. Below the hero, the shell defines a main content section with a white background that extends down the page, keeping the same centered 1120–1200px width. This main content area first hosts the sort control in a slim bar, then the results list beneath it. Margins between these major sections follow the 48–64px vertical spacing guideline: about 56px between the bottom of the hero content and the top of the sort/results area. On smaller viewports, the shell allows the hero to reduce in height and stacks the right column filters below the branding while keeping the overall linear flow intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural container that controls max‑width and section spacing. The hero remains scrollable content (not sticky), while the header is fixed and the social bar is fixed along the right edge within the shell’s coordinate system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter options loader is a non‑permanent overlay element that appears in the same spatial footprint as the filter controls it serves. When loading filter data, it occupies the right‑hand hero column where the location-and-radius-filter-form, date-and-day-filter-controls, and need-and-activity-filter-controls live. Visually, it can appear as a semi‑transparent cover over that entire stacked column (about 25–30% of the content width), starting just below the hero’s top content alignment and extending to just above the hero’s bottom alignment, or as skeleton placeholders matching the exact shapes of the three stacked cards. The loader respects the internal spacing: there is still 16–24px of implied gaps between the stacked skeletons. At smaller breakpoints where filters stack below the main title, the loader similarly fills that lower block, keeping the same left/right margins as the filters themselves. It does not intrude into the left branding area or the results list below; instead, it feels like a localized loading state for the right filter panel and any related filter controls just above the results list if those are added later.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally as an overlay or skeleton set in place of the filter controls; it is positioned relative to the filter column/card positions and scrolls with the page rather than being fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is the hero‑level primary CTA block for the Volunteer page and sits at the top of the right‑hand hero column. Within the hero’s centered content container, it occupies roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–30% of total content width), aligned flush to the right edge of the grid and vertically centered in the middle third of the hero height. It is styled as a prominent, pill‑edged card (roughly 280–360px wide) with a vivid, brand‑color background (e.g., yellow) to echo the PayPal 'Donate' block from the mockup. Inside, it uses 16–24px internal padding on all sides, with the location input field spanning nearly full width at the top, a radius selector (slider or dropdown) aligned underneath, and a full‑width 'Find Opportunities' or equivalent button anchored at the bottom. Elements inside are vertically stacked with 8–16px gaps. The form’s height is compact enough to avoid overwhelming the hero, roughly 140–200px depending on content, leaving 16–24px of clear space above it (to the hero’s top content padding) and below it (to the next filter block). On scroll, this entire right filter column can become sticky beneath the header on larger screens, keeping this location form pinned near the top right of the viewport while the user scrolls through results, maintaining its primary action status.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the right hero column’s stack, with the option to use sticky positioning on desktop: once the user scrolls past the hero’s top, the right column (including this form) sticks just below the fixed header, maintaining a 16–24px offset from the header until the end of the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date and day filter controls sit directly beneath the location-and-radius-filter-form in the same right‑hand hero column, forming the middle block of the vertical filter stack. They are wrapped in a card that is the same width as the location form (using the same right 3–4 grid columns, roughly 280–360px wide) and aligned edge‑to‑edge with it for a cohesive tower. There is a vertical gap of about 16px between the bottom of the location form and the top of this block. The card uses a white or slightly off‑white background with subtle shadow or border to separate it from the dark hero overlay, and contains pill‑like chips or compact buttons for date selection, day‑of‑week toggles, and possibly a small calendar icon. Internal padding is 16–20px on all sides, with chip groups arranged in one to two rows and 8–12px spacing between rows. The top of the card may host a small label ('Date & Day') aligned left, with the controls arranged beneath it. Visually, this block is clearly subordinate to the bright location form above but still clearly interactive and legible against the background. On mobile or narrow viewports, this component stacks below the location form and spans approximately 100% of the content width, maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing between adjacent filter sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Travels with the location form as part of the potentially sticky right column on desktop; it remains in the same vertical order between the location form above and the need/activity filters below, and scrolls or sticks as a unit with that column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the bottom block of the hero’s right‑column filter stack. It is positioned directly below the date-and-day-filter-controls card, matching its width and alignment in the right 3–4 grid columns. A vertical gap of about 12–16px separates the two cards. Like the date/day block, it appears as a white or lightly tinted card with rounded corners and subtle shadow, but it may use more vibrant accent colors (lime, orange, purple) for chip backgrounds or level‑of‑need tags to visually signal urgency. Inside, 16–24px of padding surrounds activity‑type chips (e.g., 'Meal Packing', 'Delivery', 'Fundraising') and need‑level toggles (e.g., 'High Need', 'Moderate', 'Lower'). Content is arranged in one or more rows of pill buttons with 8–12px gaps between chips, and the top left features a small label like 'Need & Activity Type'. The height is slightly taller than the date/day card if more chips are present, but still compact enough to keep the entire filter column comfortably within the hero’s vertical center band. On small screens, this component stacks beneath the date/day controls, maintaining consistent vertical rhythm and full‑width behavior inside the content container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the same right‑column filter stack that can adopt sticky behavior on larger screens. It scrolls as part of that column and always remains below the date-and-day-filter-controls and above the transition into the results section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned at the top of the results area, immediately below the hero section and aligned with the centered content container. Vertically, there is a generous gap (about 48–56px) between the bottom of the hero’s content and this sort row to clearly signal the transition from hero to results. The sort control sits in a horizontal bar that spans the full width of the content container (roughly 1120–1200px), with 16–24px top and bottom padding. Within this bar, an optional result summary ('X opportunities near [Location]') appears on the left, while the sort control itself (a dropdown or segmented control) is right‑aligned. The control’s width is compact (about 160–240px), with a 40–48px height to keep it clickable and consistent with the spacing system. On narrower screens, this bar reflows to a two‑row arrangement: summary or label on the first row, sort control centered or left‑aligned below it, with 8–12px vertical spacing. The bar sits directly above the first result card, with a 16–24px gap between the bottom of the bar and the top of the first card, maintaining the feeling of a clean, structured list header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrolling results area. It is not sticky by default, but because of its compact height, it remains close to the top of the viewport when the user is near the top of the results list. It scrolls away naturally as users move deeper into the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list sits directly below the sort control within the main content area. It aligns to the same centered content width (about 1120–1200px) and occupies the full available width inside that container. The list is a vertical stack of volunteer-result-card components, starting approximately 16–24px below the sort control and extending downward as far as needed. Each card is separated from the next by 16–24px of vertical whitespace, preserving a moderate density and clean rhythm. The list’s left edge is aligned with the left edge of the brand/title column in the hero, creating a strong vertical alignment from hero into content. Horizontally, there is generous whitespace between the cards and the browser edges thanks to the constrained content width and outer margins. If the right hero filter column is sticky on desktop, the list occupies the central/left 70–75% of the page beneath the hero, while the sticky filter column hovers to the right; but within the results section’s grid, the list itself is effectively a single main column of cards. On smaller screens, the results list spans nearly full width with comfortable side padding (16–20px), and cards remain stacked in a single column for easy scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard scrolling column: it grows vertically with content and scrolls under the fixed header and alongside any sticky right‑column filters. It does not employ sticky behavior itself, ensuring smooth, continuous scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each volunteer result card is a horizontally oriented card that fills nearly the entire width of the results list column. It maintains a consistent max width (matching the content container’s inner width minus standard side padding), centered within the page. The card uses a white background with subtle rounded corners and a soft shadow or 1px border to distinguish it from the page background. Internal padding is generous—typically 16–24px on all sides—giving room for a clear content hierarchy: the opportunity title is placed at the top left, with high contrast and slightly larger text. Directly beneath the title, a row contains key metadata (date, time, location, distance) laid out horizontally with small icons and 8–12px spacing between items; on smaller screens these elements wrap to a second line. Off to the upper right or immediately under the metadata row, a colored 'Need level' badge (using vivid lime, orange, or red‑orange) stands out, echoing the playful brand palette. A short description or bullet list appears below the metadata with 8–12px spacing, and at the bottom right of the card a primary 'View & Sign Up' button is anchored, sized with approximately 10–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding. The bottom of the card maintains 16px of padding below this button so it doesn’t feel cramped. Vertically, cards are separated by 16–24px of whitespace so each card feels distinct. On hover, the entire card can slightly lift or shadow intensify to signal clickability, in keeping with the modern, flat‑plus‑depth aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely within the flow of the results list; each card is a block‑level element that stretches to fill the list width and stacks vertically. It scrolls naturally with the list and does not use sticky or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non‑visual behavioral component that does not occupy its own visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits beneath the UI of the filter controls, sort control, and results list, synchronizing their state with the URL’s query parameters. It affects layout only indirectly by ensuring that when users load or share a URL, the right‑hand hero filters, sort order above the results, and the visible set of result cards reflect that encoded state. There is no dedicated box, card, or bar for this component; it operates in the background, tied programmatically to the existing spatial positions of filters and results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual and non‑spatial. It behaves as an invisible layer within the page shell, updating and responding to scroll, navigation, and filter interactions but not impacting layout dimensions or positions directly.

name: Volunteer Event Detail
title: Volunteer Event
description: Displays detailed information for a specific volunteer opportunity and allows users to register themselves and their group for that event.

core_features: Read the volunteerOpportunityId from the URL query string and validate that it is present.
Fetch full volunteer opportunity details using getVolunteerOpportunityDetails(volunteerOpportunityId).
Display event information including title, description, date, time, time zone, location, address, need level, tags, and whether it is a weekend event.
Show capacity and availability information based on maxVolunteers and volunteersRegistered, including whether registration should be open or closed.
Present requirements for volunteers, including age limits from minAge and maxAge and any descriptive tags returned with the opportunity.

Provide a registration form bound to the selected VolunteerOpportunity, with fields for numAdultParticipants, numYouthParticipants, primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, primaryContactPhone, and comments.
On form submission, call registerForVolunteerOpportunity with user-entered values and the current volunteerOpportunityId, handling loading, success, and error states.
Display a confirmation state after successful registration, showing key event details and registration details (e.g., participant counts and contact info) from the returned VolunteerRegistration.
Allow users to navigate back to the main Volunteer Opportunities listing (volunteer.html) via a dedicated link.
Surface clear error states for missing or invalid volunteerOpportunityId, failed event detail loading, and failed registration submissions, with options to retry where applicable.
user_workflows: View event details workflow: 1) User navigates to volunteer_event.html?volunteerOpportunityId={id} from volunteer.html or index.html. 2) Page reads volunteerOpportunityId from the URL. 3) Page calls getVolunteerOpportunityDetails(volunteerOpportunityId). 4) While loading, a loading state is shown instead of event content. 5) On success, event summary, requirements, and availability information are displayed. 6) If the event is determined to be closed (e.g., past start time or maxVolunteers reached), the registration form is disabled and a message explains that registration is no longer available.

Register for an event workflow: 1) From the event detail view, the user reviews event information and requirements. 2) If registration is open, the user enters the number of adult participants and, optionally, the number of youth participants. 3) The user provides primaryContactName and primaryContactEmail, and optionally primaryContactPhone and comments. 4) On submit, client-side validation checks for required fields and basic numeric validity on participant counts. 5) If validation passes, the page calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity with volunteerOpportunityId and all form values. 6) While waiting for a response, the form is disabled and a submission-in-progress state is shown. 7) On success (success=true), the page shows a confirmation panel with event details and information from the returned registration object. 8) On failure (success=false or network error), the page shows an error message using the returned message (if present) and allows the user to correct inputs and resubmit.

Navigate back to all opportunities workflow: 1) From either the event details view or the registration confirmation view, the user clicks the 'Back to all opportunities' link. 2) The page navigates to volunteer.html without additional URL parameters (or with whatever parameters volunteer.html itself uses). 3) The user can then browse or search for another volunteer opportunity.

Retry after load or registration failure workflow: 1) If getVolunteerOpportunityDetails fails (e.g., invalid volunteerOpportunityId or network error), an error component explains that event details could not be loaded and offers a 'Try again' control. 2) When the user retries, the page re-invokes getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with the same volunteerOpportunityId from the URL. 3) If registration submission fails, the registration form shows the error message; the user can adjust inputs and resubmit, which calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity again with updated values for the same volunteerOpportunityId.
interactions: On initial page load, the URL parameter volunteerOpportunityId is read; if it is missing, the page shows an error state and hides event-specific components.

When volunteerOpportunityId in the URL changes (for example, via in-page navigation to a different event), the event detail loader re-fetches data using getVolunteerOpportunityDetails with the new id and updates all bound components.
Clicking the 'Back to all opportunities' control triggers navigation to volunteer.html as defined in outgoing navigation.

Entering or changing values in the registration form fields (numAdultParticipants, numYouthParticipants, primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, primaryContactPhone, comments) updates the form’s in-memory state and validation status.
Submitting the registration form triggers client-side validation; if validation fails, error messages are shown inline and no API call is made.

If validation succeeds, submitting the form calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity with the current volunteerOpportunityId and the user-entered form values, sets the form into a loading state, and prevents duplicate submissions until a response is received.

On successful registration response, a registration success event is emitted internally; the registration form can be hidden or minimized, and the confirmation component becomes visible using the returned VolunteerRegistration data.
On failed registration response, an error message is displayed using the response.message (if provided), and the form is re-enabled so the user can adjust inputs and retry.

If the event is determined to be full (volunteersRegistered is greater than or equal to maxVolunteers when both are available), the registration form’s submit action is disabled and an explanatory availability notice is shown instead of or in addition to the form.
If the event startDateTime is in the past relative to the client’s current time, the page indicates that the event has already occurred and disables registration-related actions.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the volunteerOpportunityId URL query parameter (volunteer_event.html?volunteerOpportunityId={id}). When the page loads, it attempts to read this parameter. If it is missing or empty, the page enters a 'no-opportunity-selected' error state, suppresses calls to getVolunteerOpportunityDetails, and invites the user to return to volunteer.html. When a valid volunteerOpportunityId is present, the page enters a 'loading-event' state and invokes getVolunteerOpportunityDetails(volunteerOpportunityId). While this request is in progress, event display and registration components show loading placeholders. On a successful response, the page transitions to the 'event-loaded' state; the returned VolunteerOpportunity object becomes the source of truth for event-specific components (summary, requirements, availability, and registration form). If the details request fails, the page enters an 'event-load-error' state, which shows an error message and a retry control; retrying reuses the same volunteerOpportunityId from the URL. Within the 'event-loaded' state, additional derived sub-states are computed in-memory: 'registration-open' vs. 'registration-closed' (based on comparing volunteersRegistered to maxVolunteers when available and/or comparing the current date-time to startDateTime), and 'need-level' (directly from needLevel). Registration itself has its own internal state machine: 'idle' (form ready for input), 'submitting' (after onSubmit is triggered and before a response), 'success' (after a successful registerForVolunteerOpportunity response with success=true), and 'error' (after a failed submission or when success=false). These registration states are in-memory and are not encoded in additional URL parameters; refreshing the page returns the user to the 'event-loaded' view with an empty form. All state transitions are driven either by URL changes (volunteerOpportunityId) or by user interactions (e.g., submitting the form), and no state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage.

components.functionality: Reads the volunteerOpportunityId from the page URL query string, validates that it exists and is non-empty, and exposes it to other components. If the parameter is missing or invalid, it sets a flag that the rest of the page can use to display a 'no event selected' error state.

Uses the volunteerOpportunityId provided by the URL param reader to call getVolunteerOpportunityDetails(volunteerOpportunityId). Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the resulting VolunteerOpportunity object (or error info) to downstream components.

Displays key fields from the loaded VolunteerOpportunity, including title, description, activityType, startDateTime, endDateTime, timeZone, needLevel, isWeekend, regionCode, and any tags. It also assembles and displays the location information from locationName, addressLine1, addressLine2, city, state, and postalCode. This component is read-only and updates whenever the bound VolunteerOpportunity data changes.

Presents volunteer requirements based on the VolunteerOpportunity data, including age limits (minAge and maxAge when present) and any relevant tags that indicate special requirements. It formats these fields into human-readable requirement text and updates dynamically if the underlying VolunteerOpportunity changes.

Calculates and displays availability status for the event using maxVolunteers and volunteersRegistered from the VolunteerOpportunity, and the event timing via startDateTime. It exposes derived boolean flags such as isEventFull and isEventInPast, which other components (especially the registration form) can use to enable or disable registration actions. It also shows contextual messages like whether additional volunteers are urgently needed based on needLevel.

Provides an input form for users to register for the current VolunteerOpportunity. It includes fields for numAdultParticipants, numYouthParticipants, primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, primaryContactPhone, and comments. Before submission, it runs client-side validation to ensure required fields (such as numAdultParticipants and primaryContactName/primaryContactEmail) are completed and that participant counts are valid numbers. On successful validation, it calls registerForVolunteerOpportunity with the volunteerOpportunityId from the URL and the form values. It manages internal states for idle, submitting, success, and error, and emits events for success and failure so that the confirmation and error-display components can react.

Displays a confirmation state after a successful registration. It uses the VolunteerRegistration returned by registerForVolunteerOpportunity along with the current VolunteerOpportunity to show the event title, date and time, location summary, participant counts (numAdultParticipants and numYouthParticipants), and primary contact details. It also provides brief next-step instructions that do not depend on hardcoded event data (for example, general guidance that a confirmation email will be sent to the primary contact’s email). This component is visible only when the registration form emits a success event.

Provides a simple navigation control that returns the user to the main Volunteer Opportunities listing page. When activated, it navigates to volunteer.html as defined in the outgoing_connections for this page. This control should be accessible both from the event detail view and from the post-registration confirmation view.

Aggregates and displays error messages arising from several sources: missing or invalid volunteerOpportunityId (reported by the URL param reader), failure of getVolunteerOpportunityDetails (reported by the data loader), and failed registration attempts (reported by the registration form). It shows appropriate messages based on the error context and, when possible, offers a retry action that re-triggers the originating operation (e.g., reload event details or resubmit registration).

Coordinates visibility of the other components based on the overall page state. For example, it hides event-specific components when volunteerOpportunityId is missing, shows loading placeholders while getVolunteerOpportunityDetails is in progress, displays the summary, requirements, availability, and registration form once the event is loaded, and switches focus to the confirmation panel after a successful registration. It uses state signals from the URL param reader, data loader, availability indicator, and registration form to determine which components should be visible and interactive at any given time.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a hero-style, top-to-bottom flow modeled on the mockup: after the global fixed-height header, a full-width hero block fills most of the initial viewport. Inside this hero, content is split into two main columns aligned to a centered max-width container: the left 55–60% holds the volunteer event summary (analogous to the large brand logotype), and the right ~25–30% holds a vertical registration stack (availability indicator + registration form, analogous to the donate CTA stack). The far-right edge of the viewport is visually reserved for a slim, persistent vertical accent (e.g., global social bar), independent of the centered content grid. Below the hero, a lighter section presents detailed event requirements in a more informational layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a hero-style, top-to-bottom flow modeled on the mockup: after the global fixed-height header, a full-width hero block fills most of the initial viewport. Inside this hero, content is split into two main columns aligned to a centered max-width container: the left 55–60% holds the volunteer event summary (analogous to the large brand logotype), and the right ~25–30% holds a vertical registration stack (availability indicator + registration form, analogous to the donate CTA stack). The far-right edge of the viewport is visually reserved for a slim, persistent vertical accent (e.g., global social bar), independent of the centered content grid. Below the hero, a lighter section presents detailed event requirements in a more informational layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 85–90% of the initial viewport height, with the header taking the top 10–15%. Within the hero, the left content column (event summary) takes about 60% of the content width, the right registration column about 30%, with a 10% gutter between them. The detailed requirements section beneath the hero spans about 80–90% of the page width inside a centered container. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale: 48–64px between major sections (header-to-hero, hero-to-requirements), and 24–32px between stacked components (summary vs. registration card, availability vs. form, requirements subsections). Interactive panels (forms, confirmations, cards) have 16–24px internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains moderate. Above the fold, only a few focused elements are visible: event title and key info on the left, a prominent registration card on the right, and a subtle back link. The darkened photo hero background supports legibility without adding text clutter. Detailed requirements are pushed just below the hero to keep the primary decision (register or not) simplified. Generous whitespace around the registration card, between form sections, and between hero and lower content ensures the page feels open, despite multiple components.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. On load, the eye starts at the top-left brand logo (global header), moves right across the main navigation and then down to the top-right area of the hero where the bright registration card and availability pill sit. From there, the eye drops diagonally into the central-left hero area to read the large event title and key details. After digesting the summary, the gaze returns right toward the registration form fields and primary action button. Following that, the user naturally continues downward into the lighter, full-width requirements panel. The far-right vertical accent/social bar is discovered later as a tertiary path.

layout.overall_description: Below the white, fixed-height top navigation bar, a full-width hero section fills most of the viewport, anchored by a darkened background photo of volunteers that bleeds edge-to-edge. Centered within this hero is a constrained content container that follows an implicit 12-column grid. On the left, occupying a bit more than half the width, a tall event summary block sits slightly above the hero’s vertical center, with a small ‘Back to all opportunities’ link above it. The event title is large and bold, echoing the stacked ‘No Child Hungry’ feeling from the mockup, with key details like date, time, and location arranged in a clean vertical list below. On the right, a narrow, bright registration card floats against the dark backdrop, aligned vertically with the middle of the left content: at its top, a colorful availability pill signals whether spots remain; directly below, the registration form fields stack toward a highly visible primary ‘Register’ button at the bottom of the card. If the user completes registration, this same right-hand column transforms into a cheerful confirmation card in the same footprint. At the very edge of the viewport, independent of the main grid, a thin vertical bar (consistent with the site’s social strip pattern) adds a playful brand accent. Scrolling down, the hero’s photo gives way to a white or very light section where a wide requirements panel spans most of the page width, providing more detailed expectations and guidelines for volunteers. Throughout, spacing is generous, typography is bold but minimal, and key actions are visually elevated against the dark hero and light content blocks, preserving the playful yet modern nonprofit aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly at the very top of the page’s main content structure, just inside the layout controller’s scope. It does not render any UI. Conceptually, it sits above the hero section in the document flow, resolving the event identifier from the URL before any visible content (hero, summary, registration) appears. It occupies no visual space and does not affect the positioning or spacing of surrounding components; the hero content begins immediately below the global header as if this resolver were not present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual utility component; participates only in data/control flow and occupies no layout space. It runs before any visible hero or content sections are rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader component also has minimal or no persistent visual footprint, but when active it temporarily occupies the hero content area. While data is being fetched, a centered loading indicator appears within the same constrained content width and vertical band where the event summary and registration card will eventually sit. The indicator is horizontally centered, roughly at mid-viewport height within the hero, ensuring it feels integrated with the hero rather than floating at the top. Once loading completes, this placeholder disappears and the hero’s two-column layout (summary left, registration right) slides into its place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the hero content area during data loading, then removes itself entirely from the layout, allowing the main hero sections to occupy the space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event summary panel forms the dominant left column of the hero. It sits within a centered max-width container, aligned to the left 55–60% of that container’s width, starting about 48px below the bottom edge of the global header. Horizontally, it spans approximately 7 out of 12 grid columns, leaving a clear vertical gutter (about 8–10% of the width) before the right-hand registration card. Vertically, the summary content is positioned so that the main event title sits slightly below the vertical midpoint of the hero, echoing the way the ‘No Child Hungry’ logotype was anchored closer to the bottom of the hero in the mockup. Inside, the event title appears large and bold, occupying one to three lines and consuming most of the left column’s width. Directly beneath the title, there is 16–24px of vertical space, followed by a compact stack of key metadata (date, time, location, short description) in 1–3 lines, each separated by 8–16px. All text is light on dark, sitting over the photo with a subtle gradient overlay behind this panel area if needed to maintain legibility. The left edge of the summary aligns with the content container’s left margin, and there is generous right padding (16–24px) to keep text well away from the gutter toward the registration column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the hero, participating in the responsive two-column grid. On smaller screens, it stacks above the registration column while maintaining strong vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The requirements panel appears in its own section directly below the hero, separated from it by 48–64px of vertical space. It rests on a white or very light background that contrasts with the darker hero photo, giving this content a more informational, document-like feel. The panel is centered within the same max-width container, spanning roughly 80–90% of the page width (about 9–10 of 12 grid columns) and aligned with the left edge of the hero’s summary column, so the overall layout feels continuous. Internally, the requirements are organized in a vertical stack with 16–24px padding on all sides and 16–24px spacing between subsections (e.g., age requirements, physical expectations, what to bring), mimicking the trust-card spacing style. The top edge of this panel visually lines up under the hero’s main content, but with enough whitespace so the user senses a clear section change while still perceiving it as part of the same event detail experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section-level panel below the hero. It scrolls with the page, with no sticky behavior, and expands vertically to fit content while maintaining the consistent top and side margins of the main layout grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability indicator is integrated into the top of the right-side registration stack within the hero, occupying the full width of the registration card’s content area. Visually, it appears as a pill or badge with bright brand color (e.g., yellow or lime) that contrasts sharply with the darker hero background and the clean, white registration card behind it. It is positioned at the very top inside the card, with 16px top padding to the card’s edge and 16–24px bottom spacing before the first registration form field. The pill is aligned left within the card but does not extend edge-to-edge; it occupies about 60–70% of the card’s width, leaving breathable whitespace on the right side. When the event is nearly full or full, this pill may change color (e.g., to orange or red) but retains the same size and position, acting as the visual starting point for the right column. In the page’s visual hierarchy, the user encounters this pill immediately after noticing the right-hand card’s outline and before scanning down into any form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the registration card stack. It moves with the card on scroll and remains anchored to the card’s top border, scaling and reflowing with the card in responsive layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration form is presented as a tall, vertical card in the hero’s right column, mirroring the narrow donate stack in the mockup. The card sits on top of the darkened hero photo with a solid white or very light-colored background, subtle rounded corners, and a soft shadow to pull it forward. It occupies approximately 3–4 out of 12 grid columns, or about 25–30% of the content width, and is aligned to the right edge of the content container, leaving a clear vertical gutter between it and the event summary on the left. Vertically, the card is centered in the hero so that its midline roughly matches the midline of the event title on the left. Inside the card, the availability indicator is at the top, followed by 16–24px of vertical space, then the form fields (e.g., name, email, group size, optional notes) stacked with 16px spacing between field groups. At the bottom of the card, separated from the last field by 24px, a bold, full-width primary button (e.g., ‘Register to Volunteer’) stretches nearly edge-to-edge, with 16–24px horizontal internal padding and 10–12px vertical padding, styled similarly to the prominent yellow donate button in the mockup. The card maintains generous internal padding (24px) so that no text or control feels cramped, and the overall height of the card ends slightly above the bottom of the hero section, echoing the visual rhythm of the right-side donate stack from the reference design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero-side card. On large screens, it remains in a fixed column to the right of the summary; on tablet and mobile, it reflows below the summary but maintains its card styling and strong prominence. It does not stick on scroll; once the user scrolls past the hero, the card scrolls away with the rest of the hero content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel occupies the same spatial footprint as the registration form card in the hero’s right column. When a user successfully submits the form, this panel replaces the form content inside the card’s container, preserving the card’s position, size, and alignment within the hero. The card remains anchored to the right side of the content container, centered vertically, with the same white/light background, border radius, and shadow. Internally, the confirmation content is more compact: a bold confirmation headline (e.g., ‘You’re Registered!’) sits at the top with 16–24px padding above, followed by a short, friendly message and key event details (date, time, location) stacked with 8–16px spacing. A secondary action (e.g., ‘Add to Calendar’ or ‘Share with your group’) appears as a button or link near the bottom, aligned left or centered within the card. Overall height is somewhat shorter than the form card, leaving more dark hero background visible around it, but still vertically aligned so the midlines of the left summary and right confirmation feel balanced. This preserves the strong two-column hero composition while signaling a completed state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the registration form within the same hero-side card container. It shares the same responsive behavior as the form: on smaller screens, it appears stacked below the summary while fully replacing the form content; the card itself maintains its top and side margins and scrolls normally with the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-opportunities link is a small but clearly visible navigational element positioned just above the event summary in the hero’s left column. It aligns with the left edge of the summary’s text block and sits approximately 16–24px above the event title, at the same horizontal starting point, visually anchoring it to the main content. The link is styled as a subtle text link with an optional left-pointing arrow icon, using a lighter weight than the event title so it doesn’t compete for attention. It does not extend the full width of the column; instead, it occupies only as much width as its text, leaving plenty of whitespace around it. There is about 48px of vertical space between the bottom edge of the global header and this link, allowing the hero photo and overlay to be perceived before the eye lands on it. Its placement ensures that users scanning from the top left (brand logo) downwards will quickly discover how to return to the broader opportunities list without visually disrupting the hero’s main two-column composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the left hero column. It scrolls away as the user scrolls past the hero and respects the same margin and padding rules as text content in the summary column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display component takes over the main content region when the event fails to load or cannot be found. In this state, instead of the standard hero with summary and registration card, a single centered error panel appears within the hero’s bounds. The hero still uses the full-width background (solid brand color or subtle image), but the two-column layout is suppressed. The error panel is a medium-width card (about 5–6 of 12 columns, or 45–55% of the container width), horizontally centered and aligned near the vertical center of the viewport. It has generous 24px internal padding, a clear error icon and headline at the top, followed by an explanatory message and a prominent ‘Back to Opportunities’ button. Spacing within the card uses 16–24px between elements. This arrangement ensures that when an error occurs, the user’s eye is drawn directly to the message in the center, with no competing primary CTA on the right. The back link and registration card are not shown in this state, preventing confusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the hero’s normal two-column content. When active, it occupies the central area of the hero and scrolls like a normal section. When resolved, it is removed from the layout entirely, allowing the standard summary and registration components to render.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The layout controller governs the overall structure and responsive behavior of the page rather than rendering visible UI. Conceptually, it defines the full-width hero region below the header, the central max-width content container inside it, the two-column split between event summary and registration stack, and the placement of the requirements section beneath the hero. It also manages breakpoint-based changes: for example, collapsing the two-column hero into a vertical stack (summary first, registration card second) on smaller screens, and adjusting margins (from 64px to 48px to 32px) between sections according to the spacing system. While users never see this component directly, its rules ensure that the visual relationships—left-dominant summary, right CTA stack, centered error or loader states, and the transition from dark hero to light requirements section—remain consistent across viewports.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural controller that defines and orchestrates grid, alignment, and responsive behavior for all visible components. It ensures that major components snap to the implicit 12-column grid and that the hero behaves as a large, above-the-fold section with a full-bleed background.

name: Child Sponsorship Search
title: Child Sponsorship Search
description: Search for children available for sponsorship using age, region, and sorting options, then open an individual child’s profile to begin sponsorship.

core_features: Load available sponsorship filter options (age range presets, regions, sort options) using getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions.
Read initial filter state from URL parameters (minAge, maxAge, regionCode, sortBy) and apply them to the filter controls and search query.
Allow users to adjust age range, region, and sort order via a search form and re-run searchChildrenForSponsorship with the selected parameters.
Display search results as a list of child cards, each showing data bound to the Child model (e.g., age, region-based location information, waiting duration as provided).
Allow users to click a child card to navigate to child_profile.html with the selected childId in the URL.
Reflect the current filter and sort state in the URL parameters whenever the user changes filters, enabling shareable and back/forward-compatible search URLs.
Provide feedback for loading, empty results, and error states while invoking getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions and searchChildrenForSponsorship.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Initial visit from navigation:
1) User clicks a “Sponsor a Child” navigation link.
2) Browser loads sponsorship_search.html without or with minimal URL parameters.
3) On page initialization, the page reads URL parameters (if present) but primarily relies on defaults.
4) The page calls getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions to obtain age range presets, region list, and available sort options.
5) The filter form is populated using the returned options; if URL parameters are present, they override corresponding defaults.
6) The page calls searchChildrenForSponsorship with the resolved minAge, maxAge, regionCode, and sortBy values.
7) Child results are rendered as cards. The user browses the list and may refine filters or click a child card.

Workflow 2 – Campaign-driven visit with preselected filters:
1) User arrives on sponsorship_search.html via a campaign link containing URL parameters (e.g., minAge, maxAge, regionCode, sortBy).
2) On initialization, the page parses these URL parameters into the initial filter state.
3) The page calls getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions, then aligns the loaded options with the URL parameters (e.g., selects the matching region and sort option and sets age fields accordingly).
4) The page invokes searchChildrenForSponsorship using the URL-derived parameters.
5) Results are displayed according to the campaign-intended filters. The user can change filters; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new search.
6) If the user shares the URL or uses browser back/forward, the page re-reads the URL parameters and re-runs the search for a consistent experience.

Workflow 3 – Refining search and resorting:
1) User sees initial list of children based on current filters/sort.
2) User adjusts the age range (either by selecting an age preset from getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions or by editing minAge/maxAge fields).
3) On age change, the page updates minAge/maxAge in the URL and calls searchChildrenForSponsorship with the new age bounds and the current regionCode/sortBy.
4) User selects a different region from the region list returned by getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions; regionCode is applied, the URL is updated, and searchChildrenForSponsorship is called again.
5) User changes sort order by selecting a sort option (one of sortOptions codes); sortBy is updated in the URL and a new search is triggered.
6) The user can repeat changes until satisfied with the result set.

Workflow 4 – Navigating to a child profile to start sponsorship:
1) After reviewing search results, the user clicks on a specific child card.
2) The click handler reads the child’s id from the Child data bound to that card.
3) The browser navigates to child_profile.html, appending childId in the query string (e.g., child_profile.html?childId=<id-from-card>).
4) The child_profile.html page (outside the scope of this design) uses childId to load detailed information and sponsorship options.

Workflow 5 – Using browser navigation with URL-based state:
1) User performs several filter and sort changes; each change updates URL parameters and triggers searchChildrenForSponsorship.
2) User clicks the browser’s Back button.
3) The browser reverts the URL to a previous set of parameters.
4) The page listens for URL/popstate changes, re-parses minAge, maxAge, regionCode, and sortBy, and re-invokes searchChildrenForSponsorship with these values.
5) The filter controls and child results update to reflect the restored state, keeping the user’s navigation intuitive.
interactions: On page load, the page reads minAge, maxAge, regionCode, and sortBy from the URL query string (if present).
On page load, after reading URL parameters, the page invokes getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions with no arguments and binds ageRangePresets, regions, and sortOptions to the filter form controls.

On page load, once filter options are available and initial filter state is determined, the page calls searchChildrenForSponsorship, passing the current minAge, maxAge, regionCode, countryId (if used), and sortBy values.

When a user selects an age preset option, the corresponding minAge and maxAge values from ageRangePresets are applied to the age filter state, written to the URL parameters, and used to re-call searchChildrenForSponsorship.
When a user manually edits the minimum age field, the new numeric value updates the page’s minAge state, updates the minAge URL parameter, and triggers searchChildrenForSponsorship.
When a user manually edits the maximum age field, the new numeric value updates the page’s maxAge state, updates the maxAge URL parameter, and triggers searchChildrenForSponsorship.
When a user selects a region option from the region filter control, regionCode is updated in page state and URL parameters, and searchChildrenForSponsorship is called with the updated regionCode.

When a user clears or selects a region option representing no specific region, the regionCode URL parameter is removed or adjusted accordingly, and searchChildrenForSponsorship is called with the updated region parameter (including a null/undefined value if supported).
When a user selects a sort option from the sort control, the selected option’s code is stored as sortBy, written to the URL parameter, and used to re-call searchChildrenForSponsorship.
When the search is running, the page displays a loading indicator and temporarily disables filter controls if desired until the searchChildrenForSponsorship promise resolves or rejects.
If searchChildrenForSponsorship returns an empty array, the page shows an empty-state message while still presenting the active filters so the user can adjust them.

If searchChildrenForSponsorship or getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions fails, the page shows an error state and offers a control to retry the operation (e.g., a retry button that re-invokes the failed interface).

For each Child entry returned by searchChildrenForSponsorship, the page renders a child card bound to that Child’s properties (including age, regionCode/location-related information, and waitingSince/daysWaiting as provided).
When a user clicks anywhere on an interactive area of a child card, the page reads the bound childId and navigates to child_profile.html with childId as a query parameter.

When the browser URL changes due to back/forward navigation (popstate or similar), the page re-parses minAge, maxAge, regionCode, and sortBy from the URL and re-syncs the filter controls and search results with these values.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is derived from and synchronized with URL query parameters and the data returned from getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions and searchChildrenForSponsorship.

URL parameters:
- minAge: When present, this numeric value is used as the current minimum age filter. The age filter UI and the request to searchChildrenForSponsorship both use this value. When the user changes the minimum age via the UI, this parameter is updated. If the user clears the minimum age, the parameter is removed from the URL.
- maxAge: When present, this numeric value is used as the current maximum age filter. The UI and searchChildrenForSponsorship both use this value. UI updates propagate to the URL; clearing it removes it from the URL.
- regionCode: When present, this string is used to select a region in the region filter (matching against regions from getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions) and is passed into searchChildrenForSponsorship. Changing or clearing the region via the UI updates or removes this parameter.
- sortBy: When present, this string is matched to one of the sortOptions codes from getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions, determines the selected option in the sort control, and is passed to searchChildrenForSponsorship. Changing sort in the UI updates this parameter.

Initialization logic:
1) On initial load, the page parses these URL parameters and stores them as the initial filter state.
2) The page then calls getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions to obtain ageRangePresets, regions, and sortOptions. Once loaded, it reconciles the URL-based filter state with the options (e.g., selects matching region and sort option if available; otherwise falls back to defaults but keeps the raw URL values for the request if valid).
3) After filter options are ready and state is determined, the page calls searchChildrenForSponsorship with minAge, maxAge, regionCode, countryId (if applicable, derived from state), and sortBy.

Update logic:
- Whenever the user changes any filter (age, region, sort), the page updates the corresponding state variables and synchronizes them to the URL query string without a full reload (e.g., using history APIs). It then calls searchChildrenForSponsorship with the updated values.
- The search results list is always derived solely from the latest response of searchChildrenForSponsorship using the current state.
- The page listens for browser history changes (back/forward). On such events, it re-parses the URL parameters, updates the filter state and UI controls, and re-invokes searchChildrenForSponsorship using the new parameter values.

No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; URL parameters and live API responses are the single sources of truth for the page’s filters and results.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and synchronization between filter state, search results, and browser history for the Child Sponsorship Search page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse minAge, maxAge, regionCode, sortBy from URL; store as initial filter state; call getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions; then trigger initial searchChildrenForSponsorship using combined URL and default option data.

onFilterStateChange: When notified by the filter form of changes to minAge, maxAge, regionCode, or sortBy, update the URL query parameters accordingly and invoke searchChildrenForSponsorship with the updated parameters.
onSearchResultsSuccess: Receive the array of Child entities from searchChildrenForSponsorship and pass them to the child-results-list component along with loading/empty state flags.
onSearchResultsError: Set an error state and notify the results-state-indicator component to display an error message and retry affordance.
onFilterOptionsError: Handle errors from getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions by setting an error state for filter initialization and providing a way to retry the call.

onPopState: When the browser history changes (back/forward), re-parse URL parameters, update the filter state in the sponsorship-search-form, and re-invoke searchChildrenForSponsorship with the new parameters.

components.functionality: Provides controls for users to define search criteria (age range, region, sort order) and communicates changes to the page controller to run searchChildrenForSponsorship.
event_handlers: onOptionsBind: After getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions completes, bind ageRangePresets to an age preset selector, bind regions to a region selector, and bind sortOptions to a sort order selector; initialize control values based on the current URL-derived filter state.

onAgePresetChange: When a user selects an age preset, set the minAge and maxAge fields according to the chosen preset values from ageRangePresets and emit a filter change event to the page controller.

onMinAgeChange: Validate the numeric input, update internal minAge state, and emit a filter change event to the page controller with the updated minAge along with current maxAge, regionCode, and sortBy.

onMaxAgeChange: Validate the numeric input, update internal maxAge state, and emit a filter change event to the page controller with the updated maxAge along with current minAge, regionCode, and sortBy.
onRegionChange: When a user selects or clears a region option, update internal regionCode state and emit a filter change event with the new regionCode and current minAge, maxAge, and sortBy.

onSortChange: When a user selects a sort option, update internal sortBy state (using the selected option code from sortOptions) and emit a filter change event with the new sortBy and current minAge, maxAge, and regionCode.

onExternalStateSync: When notified by the page controller (e.g., onPopState), update the form controls (age fields, region select, sort select) to reflect the filter values derived from the URL so UI state stays consistent with URL state.

components.functionality: Displays the list of children returned from searchChildrenForSponsorship as interactive cards and delegates click events to navigate to detailed profiles.
event_handlers: onResultsBind: Receive the latest array of Child models from the page controller and render one child-card component per entry in the list, ordered according to the current sortBy setting used in searchChildrenForSponsorship.
onEmptyResults: When bound result array is empty, notify results-state-indicator to show an empty state message while leaving filter controls active.
onChildCardClick: When a child-card emits a click with a specific childId, trigger navigation to child_profile.html by constructing a URL with childId as a query parameter.

onLoadingStateChange: When informed by the page controller that a search is in progress, display a loading state (e.g., placeholder content) and prevent interactions that would conflict with the current request if needed.

components.functionality: Represents a single child in the search results, binding to Child properties and exposing a click action that navigates to the child’s detailed profile page.
event_handlers: onBindChild: Bind the Child entity fields (including id, age, region-related information, and waitingSince/daysWaiting or equivalent duration field) to display-only properties within the card.
onClick: When the card’s interactive area is clicked, emit an event carrying the bound childId to the child-results-list component for navigation handling.
onKeyActivate: Support keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space on focused card) by emitting the same event as onClick for accessibility.

components.functionality: Shows the current state of the search results area, including loading, error, and empty states, and provides retry controls when appropriate.
event_handlers: onLoadingStart: When the page controller initiates getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions or searchChildrenForSponsorship, set and display a loading state indicator.
onLoadingEnd: When the relevant API call completes successfully, hide the loading state indicator.
onSearchError: When searchChildrenForSponsorship fails, display an error message and a retry control; store context about which call failed.
onFilterOptionsError: When getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions fails, display an error message indicating that filters could not load and offer a retry control.

onRetryClick: When the user clicks retry, inform the page controller to re-run the last failed API call (either getChildSponsorshipFilterOptions or searchChildrenForSponsorship) using the current filter state.
onEmptyResults: When child-results-list notifies that there are no Child records, display an empty state message and guidance to adjust filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-first pattern as the mockup. A fixed-height global header sits at the very top (logo left, centered nav, right CTA, white background). Directly beneath, a full-width hero section with a darkened background photo of children/volunteers hosts the search experience. Inside this hero, content is arranged in a loose two-column layout: the left ~60% holds the page title, short explanatory copy, and a subtle branding treatment; the right ~30% holds a vertically stacked search panel (the sponsorship-search-form), visually analogous to the donate stack in the mockup. The remaining ~10% at the far right is visually reserved for a tall vertical accent strip/social bar (global element, not part of these components). Below the hero, the child-results-list occupies the main content area, arranged as a grid of child-card components with the results-state-indicator anchored at the top of this section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-first pattern as the mockup. A fixed-height global header sits at the very top (logo left, centered nav, right CTA, white background). Directly beneath, a full-width hero section with a darkened background photo of children/volunteers hosts the search experience. Inside this hero, content is arranged in a loose two-column layout: the left ~60% holds the page title, short explanatory copy, and a subtle branding treatment; the right ~30% holds a vertically stacked search panel (the sponsorship-search-form), visually analogous to the donate stack in the mockup. The remaining ~10% at the far right is visually reserved for a tall vertical accent strip/social bar (global element, not part of these components). Below the hero, the child-results-list occupies the main content area, arranged as a grid of child-card components with the results-state-indicator anchored at the top of this section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header keeps its ~10–15% of initial viewport height with a clean white background. The hero fills most of the remaining initial viewport height (roughly 65–75%), giving a strong, photo-driven stage for the search experience. Within the hero, the left content (title/description) uses about 55–60% width of the constrained content area; the right search form stack occupies about 25–30%; the far-right 10–15% remains visually clear for the social strip and breathing room. The results section below spans a centered content width (about 75–85% of the viewport), using a 12-column grid to place child cards in 2–3 columns depending on screen size. Vertical spacing between header and hero, and between hero and results, follows the 48–64px range, with 24–32px spacing between stacked elements (title, form, state indicator, card rows).
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate content density. The hero presents only a few focused elements: a large title, one or two short supporting lines, the search form, and minimal helper text. Generous padding around the search panel and between form fields ensures clarity over the darkened photo. The results area introduces many items (cards) but arranges them in a clean grid with consistent gutters and white space, preventing visual overload. Each child-card has concise information (name, age, region, one key badge, and a primary CTA) with enough internal padding to feel airy and approachable, echoing the trust cards and donate CTA spacing from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The user’s gaze starts at the top-left site logo in the header, travels horizontally through the centered navigation, then lands on the bright, right-aligned header CTA. From there, the eye drops diagonally into the hero, first catching the bold page title and short explanation on the left. The gaze then slides right to the brightly colored, card-like search form panel, where the primary action (e.g., 'Search Children') stands out like the yellow donate button in the mockup. After configuring or submitting the search, attention moves downward to the results-state-indicator at the top-left of the results section, then sweeps across the grid of child-cards. The far-right vertical social strip remains a tertiary visual path, discovered after the main search and sponsorship content.

layout.overall_description: The Child Sponsorship Search page feels like a natural extension of the hero-driven nonprofit homepage. A clean white header bar anchors the top, with the logo on the left, a centered navigation, and a vibrant 'Donate' button on the right, establishing the familiar brand rhythm. Immediately below, a full-width hero image of children and volunteers, dimmed by a dark overlay, sets an emotional context. Within this hero, the left two-thirds of the constrained content area carries a large, playful yet modern title like 'Find a Child to Sponsor', stacked over one or two short lines explaining how age and region filters work. On the right, a tall, pill-cornered search panel floats above the photo, its bright brand color and generous padding echoing the original yellow donate block. This panel stacks dropdowns and inputs vertically, ending in a bold primary 'Search' button, with small, calm helper text or trust microcopy beneath. As the user scrolls past the hero, the background transitions to a lighter, quiet surface where the results-state-indicator appears at the top-left, followed by a structured grid of child-cards that extend down the page. Each card shows a child’s photo, key details, and a 'Sponsor' CTA, spaced with consistent gutters and aligned to the same invisible 12-column grid, creating a balanced, trustworthy search experience that still carries the playful nonprofit brand energy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-search-initializer functions as the invisible page-level frame that positions the hero search experience above the results grid. It defines a full-width, full-viewport-height layout region below the global header: the top segment is the hero band (filling most of the initial viewport height), and the segment below hosts the results section. Within this frame, the initializer aligns the hero’s inner content to a centered, constrained width (approximately 75–85% of the viewport), applying a 12-column grid: the left 7–8 columns are reserved for the title/description zone, and the right 3–4 columns are reserved for the search form column. It also ensures consistent vertical spacing: roughly 48–64px between the bottom of the header and the hero’s main content, and another 48–64px between the hero bottom edge and the start of the results section. On scroll, this controller can keep the page’s main content anchored while allowing the hero to compress naturally, so the search form and results transition smoothly into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page layout orchestrator with no visible UI. It defines the hero and results regions, manages the constrained content width and grid columns, and can coordinate sticky behavior for the search form or header on scroll, but itself does not occupy an explicit visible element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-search-form is a prominent, card-like panel positioned in the hero’s right column, echoing the donate stack in the mockup. Within the hero section, the form is anchored to the right side of the constrained content area, occupying roughly 25–30% of the overall width (about 3–4 of 12 columns). It sits vertically centered within the hero’s height on large screens, with at least 48px of breathing room from the hero’s top and bottom edges, so it appears floated but grounded. The form’s container has a bright brand-colored background (e.g., yellow or lime), rounded corners, and generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides), clearly separating it from the darkened background photo. Inside the panel, form fields (age range selector, region dropdown, sort dropdown) are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them. At the bottom of this stack, a wide primary button spans nearly the full width of the panel, with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, styled similarly to the hero’s main donate button. Below the primary button, a thin, subdued strip of helper text or micro trust copy (e.g., 'Your sponsorship changes a child’s life') sits with 8–12px top margin, mimicking the placement of payment logos/trust badges in the mockup without overcrowding. On smaller screens, the form drops below the title and spans close to 100% of the content width, but retains the same generous padding and vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the hero, the form behaves like a fixed-position column element relative to the content grid. On larger screens it can become sticky relative to the viewport while the user scrolls through the first rows of results, staying pinned near the top-right until the results section scrolls past a certain threshold, ensuring easy adjustment of filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-results-list begins immediately below the hero, aligned to the same centered, constrained content width as the hero’s inner content. A 48–64px vertical gap separates the bottom of the hero from the top of this section, preserving the airy feeling. At the very top-left of this section, the results-state-indicator sits as a leading element, followed by the grid of child-cards. The list uses the implicit 12-column grid: on desktop, each child-card occupies 4 columns, yielding three cards per row with equal-width cards and consistent horizontal gutters (24–32px). On narrower tablet widths, cards shift to 2 per row (6 columns each), and on mobile to a single column spanning nearly the full width. Vertical spacing between rows of cards is 24–32px, aligning with the overall spacing system. The entire results area rests on a light, neutral background (e.g., a soft off-white or pale gray) to distinguish it from the dark hero, with modest top and bottom padding of 48–64px inside the section. Pagination or load-more controls, if present, align to the center below the last row of cards with 24–32px top margin, keeping a clean, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally as the main page content. The list expands vertically as more children are loaded or paginated, always maintaining its grid alignment and consistent gutters. It responds fluidly to breakpoints, reflowing child-cards from 3-column to 2-column to single-column layouts while remaining centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each child-card is a compact, vertically oriented tile within the child-results-list grid. On desktop, it occupies roughly one third of the content width per row (about 4 of 12 columns), with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px spacing from neighboring cards via grid gutters. Within the card, a child’s photo occupies the top half, spanning the full card width with slightly rounded top corners. Below the photo, the card’s lower half is a flat, light surface that presents the child’s name in a bold, friendly typeface at the top, followed by a smaller line combining age and region. A slim, playful color accent (e.g., a small tag or pill indicating 'High Need' or 'New') can sit to the right of the name line, echoing the colorful trust badges from the mockup. At the bottom of the card, a primary 'Sponsor [Name]' button stretches nearly edge-to-edge, separated from the text by 16px of vertical space. The button uses vivid brand color (similar to or slightly varied from the hero CTAs) with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. The overall height of cards is visually consistent within a row (subject to text wrapping) to maintain grid rhythm, and the cards cast a subtle, soft shadow or use a contrasting outline to stand out from the results background without feeling heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child-cards are standard grid items within the results list. They resize responsively based on the number of columns at each breakpoint and maintain consistent aspect ratios for photos to preserve a tidy mosaic. Hover or focus states gently elevate or highlight the card, but the card does not become sticky or detached from its row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-state-indicator is positioned at the top-left of the child-results-list section, visually tethered to the list but acting as a small, attention-guiding element. It sits flush with the left edge of the content column and appears about 0–8px below the section’s top padding, preceding the first row of child-cards. The indicator may appear as a pill-shaped badge or compact horizontal bar stating something like 'Showing 24 of 132 children' or 'No children found – adjust your filters', styled in a subtle but clearly readable way. It uses a light background with a colored border or text accent in one of the brand colors (e.g., blue or purple), giving it enough presence without competing with the hero or the primary CTAs. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of this indicator and the top edges of the first row of child-cards, establishing a clear hierarchy between the summary state and the individual cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The indicator scrolls with the results section and remains attached to the top of the grid content. In long lists, it naturally moves off-screen as the user scrolls down, but could optionally reappear or update at the top whenever new filters are applied, always reoccupying the same anchor position above the first row of cards.

name: Child Profile & Sponsorship
title: Child Sponsorship Profile
description: Displays a specific child's profile and supports starting a monthly sponsorship commitment using a standard configured monthly amount.

core_features: Load and display a specific child's profile using the childId URL parameter via getChildProfile.
Show key child details (name, age, location, days waiting, short bio) and indicate how the child fits any criteria passed from the prior search (e.g., age range, region, sort by longest waiting).
Provide a primary 'Sponsor this child' action that opens or focuses the sponsorship form for the selected child.

Prefill the monthly sponsorship amount with a configurable default (e.g., the current standard amount), allow the user to adjust it within allowed rules, and clearly indicate that the commitment is monthly.
Collect sponsor contact information (full name, email, phone) and mailing address required to create a SponsorshipCommitment.
Collect payment details (payment method selection and card details) required by startChildSponsorship for a recurring sponsorship.
Submit the sponsorship via startChildSponsorship, handle success and error states, and show a confirmation view summarizing the new SponsorshipCommitment.
On confirmation, offer navigation to find another child (link to sponsorship_search.html) while preserving any relevant search parameters received via URL.
