user_workflows: Viewing a child profile from a search or homepage: 1) User clicks a child in sponsorship_search.html or index.html, which links to child_profile.html?childId={id}&optionalFilterParams. 2) On page load, childId is read from the URL and getChildProfile is called. 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the child’s profile is displayed along with any matching context (e.g., filters like age range, region, or sort order passed as URL params). 5) If loading fails or childId is invalid/missing, an error message is shown with a link back to sponsorship_search.html.

Starting and completing a sponsorship: 1) On the loaded child profile, the user clicks the primary 'Sponsor this child' button. 2) The page scrolls/focuses to the sponsorship form section and sets an internal form state (optionally reflected as view=form in the URL). 3) The amount field is prefilled with the system’s default monthly sponsorship amount and the frequency is clearly indicated as monthly; the user may adjust the amount according to configured rules. 4) The user enters contact details and mailing address. 5) The user enters payment details (supported payment method and card information). 6) The user clicks 'Confirm sponsorship'; the form controller validates required fields and value formats. 7) If validation passes, startChildSponsorship is called with childId from the URL and all form values (amount, currency, sponsor info, address, payment details). 8) While the request is pending, a submitting state is shown and the submit button is disabled. 9) On success, the page state switches to a confirmation view (optionally updating the URL, e.g., view=confirmation), populated with the returned SponsorshipCommitment and the child data. 10) The user may then click 'Find another child' to go to sponsorship_search.html, optionally including prior filter parameters in that link.

Handling errors, retries, and navigation: 1) If getChildProfile fails (e.g., invalid childId or network error), an error notification component shows the message and a 'Back to child search' link to sponsorship_search.html. 2) If startChildSponsorship returns success=false, the error notification shows the message from the response and re-enables the form for correction and resubmission. 3) The user can modify the amount or any form fields and retry submission. 4) After a successful sponsorship, the user can optionally use the browser back button (which may return them to the form state or previous page, depending on URL state) or click 'Find another child' to start a new search.
interactions: On initial page load, read the childId URL parameter; if present, trigger getChildProfile; if absent, show a missing-parameter error with navigation back to sponsorship_search.html.
While getChildProfile is in progress, display a loading indicator and disable sponsorship actions until the Child data is available.
When getChildProfile returns a Child, bind its fields to the child profile summary (name, age, regionCode, countryId, communityName, daysWaiting, shortBio, profilePhotoUrl).

Optionally, read additional URL parameters (such as ageMin, ageMax, regionCode, sort, or other search context) and compare them to the loaded Child to display a note indicating that the child matches the user’s previously selected criteria.
When the user clicks 'Sponsor this child', set the internal form state to active, optionally update the URL (e.g., add or update view=form), and scroll/focus to the sponsorship form component.

When the user changes the monthly amount input, update the bound startChildSponsorship.amount parameter in state, re-calculate any dependent display (e.g., total monthly commitment), and validate according to configured minimum/maximum rules (no hardcoded numeric thresholds in the page logic).

When the user fills in contact and address fields, store the values in the form controller state mapped to the corresponding startChildSponsorship parameters and SponsorshipCommitment fields; run inline validation on blur or submit (e.g., required fields, email format, postal code format).

When the user enters payment details, bind the values to the startChildSponsorship payment-related parameters; perform basic client-side validation (card number length/format, expiration in the future, CVV length) before allowing submission.
On 'Confirm sponsorship' click, perform full form validation; if any field is invalid, prevent submission, show validation messages, and do not call startChildSponsorship.
If validation passes, call startChildSponsorship with the assembled parameters; set a submitting state that disables the submit button and shows a spinner or progress indicator.

On startChildSponsorship success, update the page state with the returned SponsorshipCommitment, optionally update the URL to reflect a confirmation state (e.g., view=confirmation&commitmentId={returnedId}), hide the form, and show the confirmation panel with details (amount, currency, sponsorFullName, and masked card info derived from cardLast4 and cardBrand).

On startChildSponsorship failure (success=false), show the message from the response in the error notification component, keep the form visible with all user inputs preserved, and allow the user to correct and resubmit.

When the user clicks 'Find another child' from the confirmation view, navigate to sponsorship_search.html, optionally reconstructing search parameters from the current URL parameters (e.g., ageMin, ageMax, regionCode, sort) to keep the experience consistent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the childId URL parameter. On load, if childId is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalidChildId' state and displays an error with a link back to sponsorship_search.html; no API calls are made. If childId is present, the page enters a 'loadingChild' state and calls getChildProfile(childId). On success, the state transitions to 'profileLoaded' with the Child entity stored in memory for binding; on failure, state becomes 'childLoadError' with an error message. Optional URL parameters (such as ageMin, ageMax, regionCode, sort, or any other filter context originating from sponsorship_search.html) are read but not sent to any API; they are used solely for display (to show that the child matches the selected criteria) and to construct the 'Find another child' link. A secondary view state can be controlled via an optional view URL parameter with allowed values like 'profile' (default), 'form', and 'confirmation'. When the page is first loaded from a search result, the default is 'profile', and the sponsorship form is available on the same page but not focused. When the user clicks 'Sponsor this child', the client can update the URL to view=form (using history.pushState or similar) and set internal formActive=true. After a successful startChildSponsorship call, the client updates state to 'sponsorshipConfirmed', stores the returned SponsorshipCommitment in memory, and may update the URL to view=confirmation&commitmentId={SponsorshipCommitment.id}. The page does not call any additional interfaces with commitmentId; if the user reloads the confirmation URL, the page will still show a generic confirmation state based on view=confirmation and the Child data from getChildProfile, but detailed sponsorship data will only be available immediately after the successful response in the same session. All business values such as the default monthly amount and any minimum allowed amount are sourced from configuration or higher-level page initialization and are not hardcoded in this page, even though the UX is designed around a standard monthly amount.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading childId from the URL, calling getChildProfile, and managing loading or error states for the child profile data.
event_handlers: onInit (read childId; if present, call getChildProfile; if missing, set invalidChildId state)
onLoadSuccess (store Child entity in shared state; transition to profileLoaded)
onLoadError (store error message; transition to childLoadError)

components.functionality: Displays the loaded child's core information and contextual matching against any search criteria passed via URL parameters, and exposes the primary 'Sponsor this child' call-to-action.
event_handlers: onClickSponsorButton (set formActive=true in the form controller; optionally update URL view=form; scroll or focus to the sponsorship form component)

components.functionality: Coordinates the sponsorship form sections (amount, contact, address, payment), maintains the in-progress form state mapped to startChildSponsorship parameters, performs validation, submits the sponsorship, and manages transitions between form and confirmation views.
event_handlers: onInit (initialize form state with childId from URL and default amount/currency from configuration or higher-level props)
onFieldChange (update corresponding startChildSponsorship parameter in local form state)
onValidate (run client-side validations for all required fields and business rules before submission)
onSubmit (if validation passes, call startChildSponsorship with current form state; set submitting=true)
onSubmitSuccess (receive sponsorshipCommitment; set submitting=false; store SponsorshipCommitment; update view state to confirmation; optionally update URL view=confirmation&commitmentId=... )
onSubmitError (set submitting=false; store error message; expose it via the error notification component)
onUpdateUrlState (synchronize internal view state changes with URL parameters without full page reload)

components.functionality: Manages the monetary aspects of the sponsorship, including displaying and allowing adjustment of the monthly sponsorship amount and showing the fixed monthly frequency.
event_handlers: onAmountChange (update startChildSponsorship.amount in form state; validate against configured rules; update any dependent summaries)
onCurrencyChange (if multiple currencies are supported, update startChildSponsorship.currency and related display; otherwise keep it fixed based on configuration)

onFrequencyConfirm (display and confirm with the user that the commitment will recur monthly; frequency is not sent as a parameter but is implicit in the sponsorship product and will always be monthly)

components.functionality: Collects the sponsor’s contact details required to create the SponsorshipCommitment, including full name, email, and optional phone.
event_handlers: onFieldChange (update the corresponding contact field in form state)
onBlurValidate (validate name non-empty, email format, and optionally phone format; surface field-level errors to the user)
onSectionValidate (run as part of overall form validation, ensuring required fields are present before submission)

components.functionality: Collects the sponsor’s mailing address fields that will be stored in the SponsorshipCommitment and are required for communication and receipts.
event_handlers: onFieldChange (update corresponding address field in form state)
onBlurValidate (validate required fields and basic postal code constraints, based on country where applicable)
onSectionValidate (used by the form controller during full-form validation prior to calling startChildSponsorship)

components.functionality: Collects the payment method type and card details required by startChildSponsorship to set up the recurring monthly charge.
event_handlers: onPaymentMethodChange (set paymentMethodType in form state; currently allows selection of supported types consistent with available fields, typically credit_debit_card)
onCardFieldChange (update individual card fields in form state as user types)
onPaymentValidate (check that a supported payment method is chosen and card fields pass basic validation rules before allowing form submission)

components.functionality: Shows a live summary of the sponsorship being created, including child identity, monthly amount, currency, and high-level terms, and hosts the final 'Confirm sponsorship' action.
event_handlers: onClickSubmitSponsorship (delegates to sponsorship-form-controller.onSubmit; triggers full form validation and the startChildSponsorship call if valid)
onFormValidationFail (displays a general validation error message or highlights invalid sections)
onAmountOrFieldChange (refreshes the displayed summary as form values change)

components.functionality: Displays a confirmation after a successful sponsorship submission, summarizing the new SponsorshipCommitment along with the child’s details and providing navigation to find another child.
event_handlers: onShow (render when sponsorship-form-controller transitions to confirmation state; use SponsorshipCommitment and Child to populate amount, currency, sponsor name, and masked payment info based on cardLast4 and cardBrand fields in SponsorshipCommitment)
onClickFindAnotherChild (navigate to sponsorship_search.html, optionally appending ageMin, ageMax, regionCode, sort, or other search context parameters from the current URL)
onClickDone (optional; close or hide confirmation or navigate back according to site-wide navigation patterns)

components.functionality: Shows loading or submitting states during getChildProfile and startChildSponsorship calls, and disables interactions that should not occur while asynchronous operations are in progress.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange (show or hide child-level loading indicator based on getChildProfile status)
onSubmittingStateChange (show or hide form-level submitting indicator and disable the submit button while startChildSponsorship is in progress)

components.functionality: Displays error messages related to loading the child profile or submitting the sponsorship, and can offer recovery actions such as retrying or navigating back to search.
event_handlers: onShowChildLoadError (display when child-profile-loader encounters an error; include a 'Back to child search' link to sponsorship_search.html)
onShowSponsorshipError (display when startChildSponsorship returns success=false or fails; allow dismissal so the user can edit and resubmit the form)
onRetryLoadChild (optional button to re-trigger getChildProfile using the same childId)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing fixed-height header, the page uses a single, tall hero-style sponsorship area that fills most of the initial viewport. Inside this hero, content is arranged in a two-column composition similar to the mockup: the left side is dedicated to the child’s identity (photo and key profile details), while the right side is a narrow vertical stack for the sponsorship interaction (amount, forms, and summary). A tertiary, slim vertical social/share bar can sit on the far right edge outside the main content width, mirroring the mockup’s independent social strip. Within the right sponsorship column, components follow a top‑to‑bottom stack: sponsorship amount at the very top, then payment method, then contact and mailing address forms, with a compact sponsorship summary panel pinned near the top-right. The confirmation panel appears as an overlay or replaces the right column content once sponsorship is complete.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing fixed-height header, the page uses a single, tall hero-style sponsorship area that fills most of the initial viewport. Inside this hero, content is arranged in a two-column composition similar to the mockup: the left side is dedicated to the child’s identity (photo and key profile details), while the right side is a narrow vertical stack for the sponsorship interaction (amount, forms, and summary). A tertiary, slim vertical social/share bar can sit on the far right edge outside the main content width, mirroring the mockup’s independent social strip. Within the right sponsorship column, components follow a top‑to‑bottom stack: sponsorship amount at the very top, then payment method, then contact and mailing address forms, with a compact sponsorship summary panel pinned near the top-right. The confirmation panel appears as an overlay or replaces the right column content once sponsorship is complete.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes roughly 10–15% of viewport height. The hero section underneath occupies the remaining 85–90%, using a full-width background image (child/community), darkened with an overlay. Inside a centered max-width content container, the left child profile column uses about 55–60% of the width, the sponsorship interaction column uses about 30–35%, and the far-right 5–10% is reserved for a vertical social strip sitting outside the main grid. Vertical spacing between major blocks inside the right column (e.g., between each form section and the summary card) is 24–32px, with 16–24px internal padding on all cards and forms to keep elements legible against the rich background.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains moderate density: only essential information and inputs are visible at once. The left side focuses on a large photo, the child’s name, age, and location with minimal copy, echoing the bold logotype treatment from the mockup. The right column shows one vertically stacked sequence of sponsorship controls and forms, each card-like section clearly separated by whitespace. Inputs are grouped logically with concise labels, ensuring the forms feel approachable despite multiple steps. No long text blocks appear in this view; everything is short, scannable, and separated with generous padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts in the top-left at the site logo in the header, then sweeps right across navigation toward the bright primary CTA in the header (e.g., global Donate). From there, the gaze drops into the hero’s left-center region where the large child photograph and name stand out, similar to the original ‘No Child Hungry’ mark. The eye then moves right to the bright, high-contrast sponsorship amount panel and its main action at the top of the right column. Next, attention flows downward through the right column: payment method, contact form, mailing address, and finally the summary panel. Error and loading indicators briefly interrupt this flow by appearing near the top of the right column. The far-right vertical social strip is noticed later as a tertiary path for sharing the child’s profile.

layout.overall_description: Under a clean, fixed-height white header with centered navigation and a right-aligned global Donate button, the page opens onto a full-width, nearly full-height hero section featuring a darkened background photo of a child or community scene. In the middle of this hero, a constrained content container follows a 12-column grid. The left two-thirds are dominated by the child’s profile: a large portrait and key details sit in a tall card-like block centered slightly above the vertical midpoint, echoing the oversized logotype in the original mockup. To the right, a narrower column aligns with the top of the child’s card and presents a stacked sequence of sponsorship controls: at the very top, a vivid sponsorship amount panel with a bold monthly figure and a primary action button; directly below, a compact payment method area that hints at accepted payment types; and beneath that, neatly spaced contact and mailing address forms. A compact sponsorship summary panel is pinned toward the upper part of this right column, visually akin to a trust badge or testimonial card, summarizing the child’s name, the monthly amount, and key assurances. All of these right-column elements are housed in pill-like or card-like sections with generous padding and rounded corners to stand off from the darkened photo. When the sponsorship is processing or complete, a semi-opaque overlay dims the hero and a central confirmation panel appears, thanking the sponsor and summarizing the commitment. Along the far-right edge of the viewport, a slim vertical colored strip with social icons runs from near the midpoint of the hero, independent from the main grid, echoing the playful yet modern aesthetic of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-profile-loader occupies the same footprint as the child-profile-summary on the left side of the hero. While data is being fetched, this loader appears as a tall, centered skeleton card that spans roughly 55–60% of the content width from the left, starting a bit below the header and extending toward the vertical center of the hero. It fills about 60–70% of the hero height in this left column, with placeholder blocks where the child photo and key text will later appear. The loader aligns horizontally with the top of the sponsorship stack to its right, preserving the two-column structure even in loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in place of the child-profile-summary within the left hero column; it does not scroll independently, but moves with the page content. When active, it temporarily hides the actual profile summary content beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-profile-summary is a large, visually dominant card placed in the left column of the hero, occupying approximately 55–60% of the content width. It sits within the darkened background photo, centered vertically but slightly weighted toward the lower half of the hero so that there is comfortable space above it under the header. The card’s left edge aligns with the overall content container’s left margin, while its right edge roughly aligns with the grid break before the right sponsorship column. Inside this card, the child’s portrait appears prominently on the upper half, with the name, age, location, and a short tag line stacked directly beneath in large, bold typography. There is generous internal padding (16–24px) around the content, and the bottom of the card leaves a margin of 48–64px above the bottom of the hero to match the airy feel of the mockup’s logotype area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content container; it scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, this card moves to the top of the content, spanning nearly 100% width, with the right column content stacking below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-form-controller is an invisible structural container that anchors the entire right column of form-related content. It occupies roughly 30–35% of the content width, starting at the top-right of the hero’s inner content area, aligned horizontally with the top of the child-profile-summary. Vertically, it runs from just below the header down to near the bottom of the hero, providing a column in which the sponsorship-amount-panel, payment-method-form, sponsor-contact-form, sponsor-mailing-address-form, and sponsorship-summary-sidebar are stacked with consistent gaps of 24–32px. This controller aligns its left edge with the right edge of the child-profile-summary card (with an 8–16px gutter) and leaves a clear gap between its right edge and the viewport’s far-right social strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static layout container within the hero content; it establishes a vertical stacking context and can be used as a sticky column on taller screens, where inner segments can scroll within the viewport while the left profile column remains visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-amount-panel sits at the very top of the right column inside the sponsorship-form-controller and is the most visually striking element in that column. It occupies the full width of the right column (about 30–35% of the overall content width) and has a fixed height similar to a large CTA card, using a vivid brand color (e.g., yellow) to echo the dominant PayPal donate block from the mockup. Positioned just below the header, its top edge aligns horizontally with the upper portion of the child-profile-summary card. Inside, the monthly sponsorship amount is displayed in large, bold type, with a short line of supporting copy and a primary ‘Start Sponsorship’ button stacked vertically. Generous padding (16–24px) and rounded corners create a pill-like feel, and there is a vertical gap of 24–32px between its bottom edge and the payment-method-form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it can become sticky at the top of the viewport on taller screens when scrolled, ensuring the key amount and primary CTA remain visible while the user moves through the lower form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsor-contact-form appears in the mid-section of the right column, below the payment-method-form and above the sponsor-mailing-address-form. It spans the full width of the right column and presents a compact, card-like collection of name, email, and phone inputs arranged in one or two vertical groups. Its top edge is positioned about 24–32px beneath the payment-method-form, maintaining consistent spacing. The form card uses a white or very light background to contrast against the dark hero photo, with internal padding of 16–24px and clearly separated input rows. The card’s height is moderate, not extending more than about one-third of the hero height, so that several form sections are visible at once without overwhelming the user.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column stack; on smaller screens it stacks below the sponsorship-amount-panel and payment-method-form, occupying nearly full width with the same padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsor-mailing-address-form is placed directly beneath the sponsor-contact-form within the right column, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. Like the contact form, it occupies the full width of the right column and is rendered as a light-background card with 16–24px internal padding. Address fields are stacked vertically with clear grouping (street, city, state, postal code, country) while maintaining enough whitespace so the card visually aligns with the moderate density of the page. The height of this card is slightly taller than the contact form but still well within the lower half of the right column, leaving room for the summary panel nearby without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column stack; it scrolls with the page content. On mobile, it follows the contact form as a full-width block beneath the child profile summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-form is positioned directly under the sponsorship-amount-panel in the right column, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the column and initially presents a compact overview state similar to the row of payment icons in the mockup: a short header (e.g., 'Payment Method') with horizontally arranged icons or labels for cards, PayPal, etc., aligned across the top of the card. Expanded inputs for payment details are stacked below this row as needed. The card height is moderate—tall enough to show the overview icons and a small number of fields, but not so tall that it dominates the column; it occupies roughly 20–25% of the hero height. Internal padding of 16–24px ensures each control is well separated and readable against the hero background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column stack. On larger screens, it stays within the upper half of the right column so that amount, payment, and at least the top of the next form are visible together without scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-summary-sidebar is a compact summary card that visually echoes the trust/testimonial badges from the mockup. It is positioned in the right column but slightly offset to visually feel like a sidebar: on desktop, it appears either directly beneath the payment-method-form or floated to the right within the same column, with a small 16–24px gap from the edge of the column. Practically, it occupies about 70–80% of the right column’s width, aligned to the right, with the remaining space to its left aligning with the other form cards. Vertically, it sits within the upper to middle third of the right column so that it’s visible early in the flow—often between the payment-method-form and the sponsor-contact-form. The card is shorter than the forms, with summary lines for the child’s name, monthly amount, next charge date, and a brief reassurance statement, all packed into a tight vertical stack with 16px padding. Its visual treatment (slightly different background color or border) sets it apart as a trust-focused highlight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static but visually pinned near the top of the right column. On long screens, it can be configured as a sticky element within the right column so the summary stays visible while the user completes contact and address details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sponsorship-confirmation-panel appears after a successful form submission as a prominent overlay centered within the hero area. When active, it sits on top of both the left child profile and the right form column, covering roughly 60–70% of the content width and about 50–60% of the viewport height. It is horizontally centered in the page, with its top edge starting just below the header. The panel uses a light background with subtle shadow and rounded corners, and generous internal padding (24–32px) to host a large thank-you message, a summary of the child and sponsorship amount, and a secondary call to action (e.g., 'View More Children' or 'Share'). The underlying hero content is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark overlay to direct full attention to this panel, mimicking a focused modal state that still references the background imagery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a modal-like overlay on top of the main hero content; it is fixed relative to the viewport once shown and typically disables interaction with underlying components until dismissed or navigated away.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is a compact spinner or progress bar that appears in context near the top of the right column, just beneath the sponsorship-amount-panel, whenever the user triggers a key asynchronous action (e.g., submitting payment). It aligns left with the right column content and spans no more than 30–40% of that column’s width, sitting in the space between stacked cards or temporarily overlaying the lower edge of the payment-method-form. On full-page loading states (e.g., initial load), it can also appear centered over the hero background, directly above the child-profile-loader, reinforcing that the entire profile is being retrieved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient; appears and disappears based on load states. In its in-context mode, it scrolls with the right column; in full-page mode, it can be fixed at the center of the viewport over a subtle dim overlay to indicate blocking operations.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification is displayed as a slim, high-contrast banner or pill that appears near the top of the right column when a validation or transaction error occurs. It spans most of the right column width (80–100%), with its left edge aligned with the column’s content and its top edge positioned directly above the affected form section—for global errors (e.g., payment declined), it appears just below the sponsorship-amount-panel; for field-specific errors, it can appear immediately above the corresponding form card. The banner has 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with clear text and a small icon. It sits within the hero content, not overlapping the header, and pushes the forms below it down slightly rather than floating on top, keeping the layout stable and predictable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the right column content. It appears and disappears based on error states and scrolls with the page. On very small screens, it becomes full-width above the form stack to ensure visibility.

name: Events
title: Events
description: Filterable listing of fundraising events where users can search by type, location, date range, and keywords, then navigate to individual event details.

core_features: Load available event filter options (event types, cities, date range presets) using getEventFilterOptions and bind them to the filter controls.
Initialize filters from incoming URL parameters (eventType, city, startDate, endDate, keywords, sortBy) to support deep links from other pages such as index.html.
Search fundraising events with searchFundraisingEvents based on the current filters and sort order, and render results as event cards.
Allow users to adjust filters (event type, city, date range, keywords) and sorting, automatically updating URL parameters and re-running searchFundraisingEvents.

Display event cards showing key FundraisingEvent fields (e.g., title, eventDate, city, state, isFamilyFriendly, keywords) with a clear indication for family-friendly events and 5K-related keywords when returned by the API.
Provide sorting controls (e.g., by event date ascending or descending) to help users identify the earliest qualifying event in the current result set.
Enable navigation from an event card to event_detail.html by passing fundraisingEventId in the URL parameters.
Handle empty states (no events found for selected filters) and loading states without relying on static or hardcoded event data.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Arrive via deep link with preselected filters (e.g., from index.html) > The URL includes eventType, city, startDate, endDate, keywords, and/or sortBy > On load, Events page parses these URL parameters into the filter state > Events page calls getEventFilterOptions to populate filter controls > Events page calls searchFundraisingEvents with the parsed parameters > Results list displays only matching events > User may click an event card to go to event_detail.html?fundraisingEventId={id}.

Workflow 2: Manually search for events by type, city, and date range > User opens Events page without specific URL filters or with defaults > Page loads filter options via getEventFilterOptions and applies any URL parameters found > User selects an event type from the event type dropdown > User selects a city from the city dropdown > User either selects a date range preset or enters custom startDate and endDate > User optionally enters keywords (e.g., to find 'Family 5K' if those words are part of the event data) > User submits or changes filters > Page updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter values > Page calls searchFundraisingEvents with updated parameters > Events list refreshes to show matching results.

Workflow 3: Identify the earliest qualifying event in a filtered list > User sets desired filters (eventType, city, date range, keywords) > User selects a sort order that sorts by event date ascending through the sortBy control > Page updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-calls searchFundraisingEvents with the same filters and new sortBy value > Results are returned sorted by date ascending > The earliest event in the current filter context appears first in the list (or is highlighted by a dedicated component) > User scans or clicks the first event card to navigate to event_detail.html with fundraisingEventId set.

Workflow 4: Adjust filters after viewing initial results > User sees initial event list from any of the above workflows > User modifies one or more filter fields (e.g., changes city or adjusts date range) > Filter component notifies the URL state manager to update query parameters accordingly > After URL is updated, the page calls searchFundraisingEvents again with the new parameters > Event cards update to reflect the new results while preserving the sortBy selection from the URL.

Workflow 5: Use browser navigation with preserved state > User applies filters which update the URL and result list > User navigates to an event_detail.html page via an event card > User clicks the browser back button > Browser returns to events.html with the same query parameters in the URL > On reload or reactivation, Events page reads the current URL parameters, reconstructs the filter state, and calls searchFundraisingEvents with those parameters > The user sees the same filtered and sorted results list as before.

Workflow 6: Handle no-results scenarios > User selects very restrictive filters > Page updates URL parameters and calls searchFundraisingEvents > API returns an empty array of FundraisingEvent items > Results list component shows an empty-state message (without hardcoded event data) and may suggest broadening filters (e.g., by clearing keywords or widening date range) > Filters remain visible and usable so the user can adjust and trigger a new search.
interactions: On page load, the events-url-state-manager parses URL parameters (eventType, city, startDate, endDate, keywords, sortBy), initializes internal filter state, calls getEventFilterOptions, then calls searchFundraisingEvents with the current state.

When the event type dropdown value changes, the events-filter-bar updates its internal filter model and notifies the events-url-state-manager, which updates the eventType URL parameter and triggers a new searchFundraisingEvents call.
When the city dropdown value changes, the events-filter-bar updates its model, updates the city URL parameter through the state manager, and triggers a new searchFundraisingEvents call.

When the user selects a date range preset, the filter bar resolves that preset into startDate and endDate values according to logic defined outside this spec (no static dates), updates those URL parameters, clears any conflicting custom date inputs if necessary, and triggers searchFundraisingEvents.

When the user manually edits startDate or endDate fields and confirms (e.g., by blur or explicit Apply action), the filter bar updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers searchFundraisingEvents.

When the user types into the keywords input and confirms (e.g., presses Enter or clicks an Apply/Update button), the filter bar updates the keywords URL parameter and triggers searchFundraisingEvents.

When the user changes the sort option in the events-sort-controls component, it updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers searchFundraisingEvents, using existing filter parameters for eventType, city, startDate, endDate, and keywords.

When searchFundraisingEvents is in-flight, the events-loading-indicator component displays a loading state and results components show a non-interactive or subdued state; once the API returns, the loading state is removed and event cards render from the returned FundraisingEvent array.
When the user clicks an event card in the event-card-list, the browser navigates to event_detail.html?fundraisingEventId={id}, where {id} is the FundraisingEvent.id of the clicked item.

When the user clicks a reset or clear-filters control (if implemented within the filter bar), the events-url-state-manager removes specific filter parameters from the URL (or sets them to neutral values), then re-calls searchFundraisingEvents with the resulting filter state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page treats the URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. On initial load, events-url-state-manager reads eventType, city, startDate, endDate, keywords, and sortBy from the URL query string if present. Any parameter that is absent uses a neutral or implementation-defined default (for example, no specific eventType filter if eventType is missing), but these defaults are not hardcoded event data. After reading URL parameters, the page calls getEventFilterOptions to obtain available event types, cities, and date range presets, then normalizes any URL-provided values to these options (e.g., verifying that eventType matches an available eventTypes.code and city matches a provided city entry). Once filters are normalized, the page calls searchFundraisingEvents with exactly the parameters reflected in the URL. Whenever a user interaction changes a filter or sort control, the corresponding control notifies events-url-state-manager, which immediately updates the URL query string (using techniques such as pushState/replaceState or standard navigation) to reflect the new values. Only after the URL is updated does the state manager re-run searchFundraisingEvents so that the visible results always correspond to the current URL. Browser back/forward navigation is supported automatically because filter state is reconstructed from URL parameters on each load or history event. No filter or sort state is stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; all state is derived solely from URL parameters combined with data returned by getEventFilterOptions and searchFundraisingEvents.

components.functionality: Central controller that synchronizes filter and sort state with the URL query parameters and orchestrates API calls to getEventFilterOptions and searchFundraisingEvents.
Provides user controls to select eventType, city, date range (preset or custom start/end), and keywords, and emits normalized filter changes to the state manager.

Allows the user to select a sort order for the event results (e.g., by event date ascending or descending, or other available sort criteria) and informs the state manager to update the sortBy URL parameter.

Displays the count of events returned by searchFundraisingEvents and a human-readable summary of the active filters (derived from URL parameters and filter bar state). Optionally emphasizes the earliest event when the list is sorted by date ascending.

Renders a list of event cards from the FundraisingEvent array returned by searchFundraisingEvents, in the order determined by sortBy. Each card shows key event details such as title, eventDate, city, state, and family-friendly indicators based on isFamilyFriendly and keywords.

Represents a single FundraisingEvent in the list, mapping fields like title, description (or excerpt), eventDate, city, state, registrationOpen, isFamilyFriendly, and keywords into a structured card and exposing a click action that navigates to event_detail.html.

When the sortBy parameter indicates ascending date order and there is at least one result, highlights the first event as the earliest matching event, optionally providing a direct link to its detail page.

Displays a message and contextual guidance when searchFundraisingEvents returns an empty FundraisingEvent array for the current filters, encouraging users to adjust filters without referencing any static events.
Shows and hides a loading state while calls to searchFundraisingEvents (and initial getEventFilterOptions) are in progress so users understand that results are being refreshed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The Events page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: a fixed-height global header, then a full-height hero-like search section for events, and finally the scrollable event results. Within the hero area, the content is again split into a two-column composition on desktop: the left ~60–65% shows the page title and brief context (replacing the big logotype), while the right ~25–30% is a vertically stacked filter panel (replacing the donate stack). A narrow far-right margin (~10%) is visually kept light and may align with the site-wide vertical social bar, but the Events components remain within the main 12-column content width. Beneath this hero search band, a light-background results zone hosts a summary + sort row at the top and a left-aligned list of event cards underneath.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Events page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: a fixed-height global header, then a full-height hero-like search section for events, and finally the scrollable event results. Within the hero area, the content is again split into a two-column composition on desktop: the left ~60–65% shows the page title and brief context (replacing the big logotype), while the right ~25–30% is a vertically stacked filter panel (replacing the donate stack). A narrow far-right margin (~10%) is visually kept light and may align with the site-wide vertical social bar, but the Events components remain within the main 12-column content width. Beneath this hero search band, a light-background results zone hosts a summary + sort row at the top and a left-aligned list of event cards underneath.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header retains roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height with a white background. Below it, the events hero (darkened photo background) takes up the remaining 85–90% of the first viewport: on the left, the title and short description occupy the lower-middle portion to echo the logotype positioning; on the right, a bright, card-like filter bar occupies the top-right quadrant. The actual event results region begins just below the hero overlap: the results summary and sort controls are in a shallow band (about 15–20% of the viewport height at first appearance), with the event list filling the remaining vertical space and extending as the user scrolls. Horizontally, the main content width respects a centered 12-column grid: event cards occupy about 7–8 columns (~60–65%), the filter bar occupies about 3–4 columns (~25–30%), and a small remaining margin on the far right visually lines up with the global social bar. Generous vertical spacing uses 48–64px between major bands (hero → summary, summary → list starter) and 24–32px between stacked components within those bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page balances a moderate content density similar to the mockup: the hero keeps text minimal and relies on strong imagery and a prominent filter panel instead of dense copy. The filter panel is compact but clearly separated into logical clusters of controls with 16–24px internal padding. The results summary and sort controls are light-weight, using a single line of text and a few compact controls. Event cards are readable and moderately detailed, but spaced 24–32px apart to avoid crowding. Overall, there is ample breathing room between groups: major blocks are separated by roughly 48px, and within blocks fields and elements are grouped with 8–16px spacing, so the screen feels active but not cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the F/Z pattern: the user’s eye begins at the top-left brand logo in the global header, moves horizontally across the centered navigation, then lands on the top-right header CTA. From there, the gaze drops diagonally into the hero, picking up the bold 'Events' title and contextual line on the left. Next, attention is pulled to the bright, high-contrast filter bar on the right (acting like the mockup’s donate stack). After filters are understood or adjusted, the eye moves downward into the light-background results area, first reading the results summary text on the top-left of that band, then scanning rightward to the sort controls. Finally, the eye travels vertically down through the event cards, with the earliest-event highlight card at the very top of the list acting as a visual anchor before the standard cards. The far-right vertical social bar (global element) remains a tertiary path, discovered after core tasks are addressed.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar white header with centered navigation and a bright right-aligned CTA, the Events page opens into a full-width hero section featuring a darkened photo of children and volunteers at an event. Within this hero, the main content is constrained to a centered 12-column grid. In the lower-middle left of this frame, occupying roughly two-thirds of the grid width, the bold 'Events' page title and a single line of explanatory text sit above the bottom of the hero, echoing the strong, stacked logotype treatment from the mockup. On the upper-right side, taking about three to four columns, a bright, card-like filter bar floats over the photo background, with its fields arranged top-to-bottom and a primary action button anchored at the bottom of the card. The far-right edge of the screen remains visually aligned with the site-wide vertical yellow social bar, though the Events components themselves stay just inside the main content width. As the user scrolls, the dark hero gives way to a light-background results area. At the top of this area, a slim band shows a concise results summary on the left and sort controls on the right, all sitting on a clean, white or very light surface. Immediately beneath this, a highlighted earliest event card spans the same width as the main list column, standing out with a subtle brand color accent so it feels like a featured result. Below it, a vertical stack of event cards flows down the left two-thirds of the page, evenly spaced and easy to scan. The filter panel on the right stays visible and sticky as the user scrolls, mirroring the fixed feeling of the donate stack in the original hero. When no events are found, the list area is replaced by a centered, playful empty state illustration and message that still respects the left-column alignment. During loading, a semi-transparent overlay with a spinner covers just the results column, leaving the header and filter controls visible but visually subdued. The overall effect is a playful yet professional layout that clearly guides the user from navigation to filtering to exploring specific events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual infrastructure component that conceptually sits behind the entire Events page layout. It does not occupy any specific on-screen space; instead, it binds the filter bar, sort controls, results summary, and event list together by synchronizing their state with the URL. Spatially, you can imagine it as an invisible layer spanning the full width and height of the events content area, from just below the global header to the bottom of the page, listening to interactions from the filter panel on the right and the sort controls above the list, then updating the visible event cards and summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-page logical scope; no rendered box or fixed position. It governs how other components react and update but does not influence layout directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar appears as a prominent, card-like panel anchored to the right side of the hero and then continuing as a sticky right column as the user scrolls into the results. On desktop, it occupies roughly 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid (about 25–30% of the content width) and is aligned with the top of the hero’s right content area. Vertically, it begins near the top of the hero’s inner content (about 80px below the global header, leaving one base section margin of 48–64px from the header) and extends downward, but typically not past the fold. The panel has generous internal padding (around 24px on all sides) and a bright brand color or white surface that clearly lifts it off the darker hero photo. Inside, filter controls are stacked top-to-bottom with 8–16px between individual fields and 24px between logical groups: keyword search at the top, followed by location and event type controls, then a date range selector, and finally a wide, high-contrast 'Apply filters' or 'Find events' button locked to the bottom of the card. On scroll, as the hero photo scrolls away, the filter bar remains visually docked along the right side of the page in line with the event list, separated from the list by a 32px gutter. On smaller viewports it would shift above the list as a horizontal block, but on desktop it maintains this vertical, right-side, card-like presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right-hand column: once the user scrolls past the hero, it stays pinned to the top of the viewport (offset slightly below the fixed header, e.g., 80px) while the event list scrolls independently on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls are placed within the first light-background band directly under the hero, sharing the horizontal row with the events-results-summary. They sit on the right side of the constrained content width, aligned horizontally with the summary text on the left. The row spans nearly the full width of the main content (about 80–90% of the page width), with the sort controls consuming roughly the rightmost 25–35%. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal cluster: a label like 'Sort by' followed by a dropdown or segmented pill buttons, laid out left-to-right, with 8–16px between elements. There is 16–24px of padding above and below this row, creating a shallow band that feels like a continuation of the navigation-to-hero rhythm. Visually, they align with the right side of the event list column below, creating a clean vertical edge on the right of the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results summary band: it scrolls off the screen as the user moves down the list, without sticky behavior. On narrower viewports it can wrap under the summary text but remains part of the same band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary sits directly below the hero section, forming the top of the light-background results area. It spans the full constrained content width, centered on the page and aligned to the same 12-column grid as the hero content. Occupying a shallow horizontal band about 64–80px tall, its primary text ('18 events near Seattle between June 1–30') is left-aligned and placed in the left two-thirds of the band. If filter chips are used, they appear directly beneath or to the right of this summary text with 8px spacing, but the entire block remains visually light and uncluttered. The sort controls occupy the right third of this band, on the same baseline as the summary text. A 24–32px vertical gap separates this band from the first element of the list below (earliest-event-highlight), preserving the mockup’s pacing between header-like areas and content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and full-width within the content container; it acts as a divider between the hero and the scrollable results, scrolling normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-card-list occupies the main left column of the results area, beginning immediately below the earliest-event-highlight (or directly below the results summary when no highlight is shown). On desktop, it spans approximately 7–8 columns of the 12-column grid (about 60–65% of the content width), starting from the left content margin and stopping at a consistent gutter (24–32px) before the right-hand filter bar. The list runs vertically down the page, with each event-card separated by 24–32px of vertical space to maintain a comfortable reading rhythm. The top of the list is positioned roughly 24–32px below the results summary band, so the transition from summary to first card feels similar to the mockup’s shift from donate CTA to trust cards. As users scroll, this left column is the primary scrolling content, while the right-hand filter bar remains sticky, mirroring the two-column dynamic of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical column within the main content area. It scrolls independently relative to the sticky filter bar, but remains aligned to the same horizontal grid and does not have its own sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event-card is a horizontally oriented, full-width card within the event-card-list column. It sits on a white or very light surface with a subtle shadow or 1px border, and includes 16–24px of internal padding, echoing the trust badge styling from the mockup but scaled up for richer content. At the very top, aligned to the left, a compact date or month/day badge and possibly an event-type pill (colored using one of the vivid brand colors) create a strong visual header. Below that, the event title is set as the dominant text block, spanning most of the card width; the location and time sit beneath in a lighter treatment. If an image is used, it appears either as a narrow strip across the top or as a thumbnail on the left with text on the right, but in both cases the card maintains a roughly 3:2 width-to-height ratio (not too tall). The primary 'View details' CTA is placed at the bottom-right inside the card, styled as a pill or button with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, and a secondary action like 'Share' might appear as an icon button nearby with more subtle styling. Vertically, the content within the card is stacked with 8–16px spacing between elements, ensuring it feels information-rich but not dense. Cards are aligned to form a clean vertical edge on the left and right within the list column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical list, each card maintains consistent width and spacing. On hover or focus, it may raise slightly (shadow/scale) but does not change its spatial positioning relative to other cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earliest-event-highlight appears as a featured card at the very top of the event-card-list column, immediately below the results summary band and above the first standard event-card. It shares the same width as the list (about 60–65% of the page, aligned to the left grid edge) but stands out with a stronger brand treatment—e.g., a light yellow background with dark text, or a colored left border using a vivid brand color. Internally, it uses similar padding to a normal event-card (16–24px) but compresses content slightly to stay compact: a small label like 'Soonest event' or 'Coming up next' appears at the top-left, followed by the event title and key details (date, location) on a single or double line. A concise CTA such as 'View event' is placed in the bottom-right, matching the visual style of other CTAs but perhaps using a slightly more saturated color. Vertically, this card is separated from the results summary above by 24–32px and from the first standard event-card below by another 24–32px, mirroring the spacing of trust badges under the donate button in the original hero. If no earliest event can be computed, this block simply collapses, and the first standard event-card slides up to occupy its space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list column; it scrolls with the other cards. It conditionally appears or disappears without affecting the overall grid alignment, only adjusting the vertical starting point of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no events match the current filters, the events-empty-state occupies the same region as the event-card-list and earliest-event-highlight combined, preserving the two-column layout with the filter bar still visible on the right. It is centered horizontally within the left list column and vertically within the first viewport of the results area, giving it plenty of white space around. The empty state might include a simple illustration or icon aligned centrally, with a headline ('No events found') and one or two lines of explanatory text stacked beneath, using 8–16px spacing. A secondary button ('Clear filters' or 'Browse all events') appears below the text, aligned center within the column. The block’s width is constrained to about 60–70% of the left column’s width so it feels compact and friendly rather than stretched. There is a 24–32px gap between the results summary band and the top of the empty state, similar to the gap before cards, maintaining consistent vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state replaces the event-card-list and earliest-event-highlight when there are zero results. It scrolls like normal content but typically fits within the first viewport, with the sticky filter bar remaining visible to the right for quick adjustment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears as an overlay focused on the event results column whenever filters or sorting changes trigger a data fetch. Spatially, it is a semi-transparent layer that covers the entire area occupied by the earliest-event-highlight and event-card-list, from just below the results summary band down to the current bottom edge of the visible list. It is constrained to the left column bounds so that the filter bar on the right and the header above remain visible. At the center of this overlay—both horizontally within the left column and vertically within the visible portion of that column—a spinner or playful loading animation is displayed, with an optional short status line beneath. The background of the overlay slightly dims the underlying cards (or empty state), indicating they are temporarily inactive, but the overlay does not extend into the filter bar area, preserving the structure of the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the event-card-list and earliest-event-highlight regions with a fixed position relative to the scrolling container: it moves with the page as the user scrolls during loading but always covers just the results column, never the filter bar or header.

name: Event Detail & Registration
title: Event Details and Registration
description: Displays details for a specific fundraising event and allows users to register multiple participants for a selected start time.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific fundraising event using getFundraisingEventDetails based on the fundraisingEventId URL parameter.
Show event date, location, and capacity/registration status to indicate whether registration is open or closed.
Display a start time selector populated from the event's startTimeOptions, defaulting to the earliest available start time (using earliestStartTime when provided).
Provide a multi-participant registration form that allows adding/removing participants and specifying participantType (adult, child, teen), fullName, and age, plus optional emergency contact fields.
Collect primary contact information (name, email, phone) for the registration.
Submit registrations via registerForFundraisingEvent, passing fundraisingEventId, selectedStartTime, primary contact data, and the participants list.
Display a registration confirmation state using the EventRegistration and EventParticipant data returned by registerForFundraisingEvent, along with event details from getFundraisingEventDetails.
Provide navigation back to the events listing (events.html) via a 'Back to all events' interaction.
user_workflows:
View event details and decide to register:
1. User opens event_detail.html with a fundraisingEventId URL parameter.
2. Page invokes getFundraisingEventDetails with fundraisingEventId.
3. Event details, including title, description, eventDate, venue and address, startTimeOptions, earliestStartTime, registrationOpen, maxParticipants, and participantsRegistered, are displayed.
4. The page shows whether registration is open, closed, or full based on registrationOpen and capacity fields.
5. If registration is allowed, the registration form and start time selector become available; otherwise, the form is disabled/hidden with an explanatory message.

Select a start time:
1. After event details load, the start time selector initializes.
2. If the URL contains a startTime parameter that matches one of startTimeOptions, that start time is preselected.
3. Otherwise, the component selects earliestStartTime if provided; if not, it selects the earliest value from startTimeOptions.
4. User can change the selected start time from the dropdown.
5. When the user changes the selection, the component updates internal registration state and may update the URL startTime parameter to reflect the choice.

Add and manage multiple participants:
1. User sees an empty participants list with controls to add participant entries.
2. User clicks 'Add participant' to create a new participant row.
3. For each participant row, the user enters fullName and age and selects participantType (adult, child, teen). Optional emergencyContactName and emergencyContactPhone can be entered when needed (e.g., for minors).
4. User can add additional participant rows, remove existing rows, and edit any participant fields.
5. The form dynamically calculates totalParticipants from the current participant list and keeps this total in registration state.

Enter primary contact information:
1. User fills in primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, and optionally primaryContactPhone in the primary contact section.
2. As the user types, the values are stored in registration state and validated (e.g., required fields and email format).

Submit registration and view confirmation:
1. User reviews a summary showing the selected start time, number of participants (and breakdown by participantType), and basic event information.
2. User clicks a 'Submit registration' button.
3. The page validates that fundraisingEventId is present, at least one participant is added, selectedStartTime is set and valid, registrationOpen is true, the event is not full, and primaryContactName and primaryContactEmail are provided and valid.
4. If validation fails, the page shows inline error messages and does not call registerForFundraisingEvent.
5. If validation passes, the page calls registerForFundraisingEvent with fundraisingEventId, selectedStartTime, primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, primaryContactPhone, and the participants list mapped to the expected structure.
6. If registerForFundraisingEvent returns success=true, the page updates internal state with the returned registration (EventRegistration) and participants (EventParticipant[]), and updates the URL to include registrationStatus=success and registrationId equal to the returned registration.id using pushState (without reloading).
7. The page switches to a confirmation view that hides or de-emphasizes the form and displays confirmation details: event title and date, selectedStartTime, totalParticipants, participant list, and primary contact info.
8. If registerForFundraisingEvent returns success=false, the page displays the returned message as an error and leaves the form available for correction and resubmission.

Handle direct access to confirmation state via URL:
1. If the page loads with fundraisingEventId and registrationStatus=success in the URL, it calls getFundraisingEventDetails with fundraisingEventId as usual.
2. If there is registration data in the current page session (e.g., just after submission), the confirmation view displays full confirmation details using that state.
3. If there is no in-memory registration data (e.g., user reloaded or came from outside), the page still shows a generic confirmation state tied to the event details (e.g., indicating that registration may have been completed and that details were emailed to the primary contact), without attempting to fetch registration details from any additional interface.

Navigate back to all events:
1. User clicks a 'Back to all events' control.
2. The page navigates to events.html with no additional parameters, as specified in outgoing connections.
interactions:
Page load (onInit):
- Reads fundraisingEventId (required), startTime (optional), and registrationStatus (optional) from the URL query string.
- If fundraisingEventId is missing, displays an error state and hides the registration form.
- If fundraisingEventId is present, invokes getFundraisingEventDetails(fundraisingEventId) and manages loading and error states.
- When event data is successfully loaded, initializes dependent components (event info, status banner, start time selector, registration form).

Start time selection (onStartTimeChange):
- Triggered when the user selects a start time from the dropdown.
- Validates that the chosen value exists in startTimeOptions.
- Updates registration state with the new selectedStartTime.
- Optionally updates the URL parameter startTime to match the selection using pushState, without reloading the page.

Participant management (onAddParticipant, onRemoveParticipant, onParticipantFieldChange):
- onAddParticipant: Adds a new blank participant row to the participants list in state.
- onRemoveParticipant: Removes the selected participant row from the list.
- onParticipantFieldChange: Updates the corresponding field (fullName, age, participantType, emergencyContactName, emergencyContactPhone) in state for that participant row.
- Each change recalculates totalParticipants and updates any summary display.

Primary contact input (onPrimaryContactChange):
- Triggered on changes to primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, and primaryContactPhone fields.
- Performs immediate validation for required fields and basic email format.
- Stores values in registration state to be used when calling registerForFundraisingEvent.

Registration submission (onSubmitRegistration):
- Triggered when the user activates the 'Submit registration' control.
- Performs client-side validation, including:
  - fundraisingEventId present.
  - registrationOpen is true and event is not full (derived from maxParticipants and participantsRegistered if available).
  - selectedStartTime is set and present in startTimeOptions.
  - At least one participant with valid fullName, age, and participantType.
  - primaryContactName and primaryContactEmail are provided and valid.
- If validation fails, prevents submission and displays error messages near the relevant fields and/or in a central error banner.
- If validation passes, calls registerForFundraisingEvent with:
  - fundraisingEventId from URL.
  - selectedStartTime from registration state.
  - primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, primaryContactPhone from registration state.
  - participants: mapped from the participant list in registration state to the structure expected by the interface.
- Handles the response:
  - If success=true: stores registration and participants from the response, updates URL (registrationStatus=success and registrationId), and displays the confirmation view.
  - If success=false: displays the response message in an error banner and keeps the form active.

Back to all events navigation (onClickBackToEvents):
- When the user clicks the 'Back to all events' control, the browser navigates to events.html.
- No additional URL parameters are added to that navigation.

Error handling for event loading (onEventLoadError):
- Triggered if getFundraisingEventDetails fails or returns no event.
- Displays an appropriate error message and hides or disables the registration form.
- Keeps the 'Back to all events' navigation available so the user can return to the event list.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the URL query parameter fundraisingEventId, which identifies the event to load. On page initialization, the page reads fundraisingEventId from the URL. If present, it calls getFundraisingEventDetails(fundraisingEventId) to retrieve the FundraisingEvent. If missing, the page shows an error state and does not attempt to load event data.

Additional URL parameters manage sub-state for this page:
- startTime (optional): If present, after the FundraisingEvent is loaded, the page checks whether the value matches one of event.startTimeOptions. If it does, that value becomes the selectedStartTime in registration state. If not, the page ignores the parameter and selects earliestStartTime or the earliest option instead. When the user changes the start time selection, the page may update the startTime parameter via history.pushState so that the current selection is reflected in the URL.
- registrationStatus (optional): After a successful call to registerForFundraisingEvent, the page updates the URL via history.pushState to add registrationStatus=success and registrationId equal to the returned registration.id. When the page renders, if registrationStatus=success is present and the page still has in-memory registration data from the current session, it shows the detailed confirmation view and hides the registration form. If registrationStatus=success is present but no registration data is available in memory (e.g., after a full reload), the page still loads FundraisingEvent via getFundraisingEventDetails and shows a generic confirmation state that references the event details without making any additional API calls.

Derived state from FundraisingEvent is also used:
- eventIsFull is true when both maxParticipants and participantsRegistered are available and participantsRegistered is greater than or equal to maxParticipants.
- registrationAllowed is true when registrationOpen is true, eventIsFull is false, and there is at least one available startTimeOption.
These derived values control whether the registration form and submit button are enabled or disabled and which status messages are shown.

All dynamic data on this page is obtained via the assigned interfaces getFundraisingEventDetails and registerForFundraisingEvent, and navigation-related state is managed using URL parameters rather than localStorage.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading fundraisingEventId from the URL, calling getFundraisingEventDetails, and distributing the loaded FundraisingEvent data (or errors) to child components.

Displays core event information such as title, description, eventDate, venueName, address, and indicators showing whether registration is open, closed, or full, based on the loaded FundraisingEvent data.

Shows a concise status message about the event's registration state (open, closed, or full) using registrationOpen, maxParticipants, and participantsRegistered from the FundraisingEvent, and controls whether the registration form is available.

Provides a dropdown selector of available start times for the event, initializes the default selection using the earliest available time, and updates registration state and the startTime URL parameter when the user changes the selection.

Allows the user to add, edit, and remove multiple event participants, capturing fullName, age, participantType (adult, child, teen), and optional emergency contact information for each, and maintaining totalParticipants in registration state.
Collects and validates primaryContactName, primaryContactEmail, and primaryContactPhone, storing these values in registration state to be sent with the registration request.
Displays a summary of the pending registration including the selected start time, totalParticipants, breakdown by participantType, and key event details so the user can review before submitting.

Coordinates validation of all registration inputs and, upon successful validation, calls registerForFundraisingEvent with the fundraisingEventId, selectedStartTime, primary contact information, and participants list, then processes the response and updates the page to show confirmation.

When registrationStatus=success is indicated and registration data is available from the recent submission, displays a confirmation view using the returned EventRegistration and EventParticipant data along with the FundraisingEvent details; otherwise, shows a generic confirmation state tied to the event.
Provides a control that returns the user to the main events listing page (events.html) when activated.
Displays high-level error messages related to loading event details or submitting the registration (e.g., when getFundraisingEventDetails or registerForFundraisingEvent returns an error).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom, two-column hero pattern. Below the existing site header, a full-width event hero with a darkened photo background holds a left-aligned event info block and a right-aligned vertical registration stack. Directly under the hero, the left column continues with the detailed registration forms (participants list, then primary contact), while the right column continues the compact summary + submit stack as a sticky panel. Global/system banners sit above the hero; contextual banners sit within the right-hand registration stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom, two-column hero pattern. Below the existing site header, a full-width event hero with a darkened photo background holds a left-aligned event info block and a right-aligned vertical registration stack. Directly under the hero, the left column continues with the detailed registration forms (participants list, then primary contact), while the right column continues the compact summary + submit stack as a sticky panel. Global/system banners sit above the hero; contextual banners sit within the right-hand registration stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header keeps its 10–15% viewport height. The hero occupies the next ~60–70% of the initial viewport, with the background photo running edge-to-edge. Within the hero, about 55–60% of the central content width is dedicated to the left event info block, ~25–30% to the right-hand registration stack, and the remaining horizontal margin is left as breathing room rather than a social bar. Below the hero, the left form column spans roughly 7–8 of 12 grid columns; the right summary column spans 4–5 columns and remains visually compact. Major vertical gaps between hero, forms, and confirmation follow the 48–64px rhythm, with 16–24px between related subcomponents in stacks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero emphasizes clarity over volume: one event, one primary action (register for a time), and a concise set of trust/status elements. Text is kept brief, with generous padding inside cards, selectors, and buttons. The form section is more information-dense but still broken into clearly separated cards with strong headings and whitespace, following the moderate density of the mockup. Visual noise is controlled by using the dark photo overlay and flat cards to isolate interactive elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow echoes the F/Z pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left (back-to-events link and event title in the hero), moves horizontally toward the right-hand registration stack, landing on the bright time-selection/submit area as the primary action. From there, the gaze drops diagonally into the main left column content (event details and then form fields), then returns to the right summary card to confirm price and totals. Error and status banners interrupt this flow intentionally by spanning near the top, while the sticky summary panel on the right keeps the core call to action in peripheral view as users scroll through the forms.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s white header, the page opens on a full-width hero featuring a darkened photo of children and volunteers, tinted slightly with the brand’s deep blue. Across the center, a constrained content container uses a 12-column grid. On the left ~60% width, a bold, stacked event title, date, location, and a short description sit toward the lower half of the hero, echoing the strong logotype treatment from the original design.
