On the right ~30%, a clean, bright vertical registration stack floats above the photo: a large, colorful start-time selector at the top, followed by a compact availability/status banner, a flat summary card, and a vivid primary “Register” button, all padded generously and aligned into a single column. Above everything, any global error banner stretches full-width beneath the header, and a subtle back-to-events link sits at the top-left of the content, leading into the hero. As users scroll past the hero, the left column transitions into carded form sections for participants and primary contact, while the right column’s summary stack becomes sticky, remaining in view. The spacing feels airy: large 48–64px gaps separate major sections, while 16–24px paddings keep each card, field group, and button distinct against the darker background. When registration is completed, the left column is replaced with a celebratory confirmation panel, but the right summary card stays visible, reinforcing what the user just accomplished. The overall effect is modern, playful, and trust-building, with a clear emphasis on the single key action of registering for the event.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay centered over the main event hero and form area, but confined to the content region below the global header. It covers from just under the header down to the bottom of the viewport, spanning the full content width (including both the left event info column and the right registration stack). A circular loader and short status text sit in the absolute center of the viewport, with at least 64px of empty space around them in all directions, slightly dimming but not completely obscuring the background photo and cards beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay within the content area; sits above all other components (including banners) while active and scrolls with the viewport (content beneath is non-interactive until loading completes).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-info-section sits within the hero on the left side, aligned to the same constrained content width as the navigation above. Vertically, it is anchored in the lower half of the hero, starting roughly 25–30% up from the bottom edge so that it visually overlaps the darker area of the photo. It occupies about 55–60% of the available width, roughly 7 of 12 grid columns, leaving a generous 16–24px gutter before the right-hand registration stack. Inside this block, the event name is displayed as a bold, stacked heading (two to three lines) similar in impact to the “No Child Hungry” logotype, with 16–24px gaps between title, metadata (date, time, location), and a brief description. The text sits on a subtle dark gradient strip or translucent overlay to ensure legibility over the photo, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. The bottom of this overlay aligns loosely with the bottom of the hero image, creating a visual foundation opposite the right-side registration column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero, aligned to the left hero column; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down into the form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-status-banner is integrated into the right-hand registration stack within the hero. It sits directly below the start-time-selector, with 16px vertical spacing separating the two components, and spans the full width of the right column (approximately 25–30% of the content width, about 4 of 12 columns). Styled as a shallow pill-like card or ribbon, it uses a bright brand color (lime or orange) to indicate availability, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. When the event is nearly full or closed, the banner’s color and intensity increase, visually competing with the start-time selector to signal urgency or unavailability. It maintains at least 24px of space above the registration-summary-panel below, clearly grouping it with the time selection and separating it from the monetary summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right-column stack; it scrolls out of view with the hero but its message can be duplicated or echoed in form-level messaging below if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The start-time-selector is the topmost and most dominant element in the right-hand hero column, mirroring the large yellow PayPal donate block from the mockup. It sits aligned to the right edge of the constrained content area, starting roughly halfway down the hero height so its center is near the user’s eye-line on load. It occupies about 25–30% of the content width (around 4 columns) and is shaped as a prominent card or pill with a bright brand color background (often yellow), rounded corners, and 16–24px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. Inside, a concise label sits at the top, with time options arranged as either a dropdown or a row/stack of pill buttons, each with at least 8–12px padding. The component has at least 24px of breathing room above and to the left (gutter) and aligns visually with the top of the event-info-section’s text block, reinforcing the connection between event details and the action to choose a start time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s right column. On smaller viewports, it shifts to appear directly below the event-info-section, still near the top of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The participants-list-form appears just below the hero area, in the left column that continues from the event-info-section. There is a clear 48–64px vertical gap between the bottom of the hero and the top edge of this form block, visually marking the transition from high-level event overview to active registration. The form spans roughly 7–8 of 12 columns, aligned flush with the left edge of the event-info-section above. It is contained within a light, flat card with 24px internal padding and a clear heading pinned near the top-left. Individual participant entries are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between entries, and ‘add participant’ / ‘remove’ controls appear horizontally aligned to the right or below each entry, with 8–12px vertical padding for touchable areas. A 24–32px gap separates this component from the primary-contact-form below, preserving comfortable density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; as users scroll, this form moves under the fixed/sticky right-hand summary so that the call-to-action remains visible while participants are being added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-contact-form is placed immediately below the participants-list-form in the same left column, maintaining alignment with the event-info-section and participants block edges above. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the participants-list-form, with the two visually read as a sequential flow of the registration process. It occupies the same width (about 7–8 columns) and is also housed in a light card with 24px internal padding. The heading, set near the top-left, is followed by stacked field groups with 16px vertical spacing, ensuring comfortable scanning. The bottom edge of this form sits roughly parallel with or slightly above the bottom of the right-hand registration-summary-panel, keeping the overall page balanced and preventing an overly long left-only column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls normally beneath the sticky summary on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-summary-panel lives in the right column directly beneath the registration-status-banner in the hero, forming the middle weight of the vertical registration stack. It spans the same width as the start-time-selector (about 4 of 12 columns or 25–30% of content width) and is separated from the status banner by 16–24px of vertical space. Styled as a flat, white or very light card, it has 24px of padding on all sides, with bold line items for participant count, per-person cost, and total, separated by 8–12px gaps. When the user scrolls down into the forms, this panel (along with the registration-submit-form component, when visually paired) becomes sticky: it pins to the top-right of the viewport, starting approximately 88–96px below the main header so as not to collide with global banners. This placement ensures the summary remains visible as the user edits participants and contact info in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right column once the user has scrolled past the hero; it maintains its horizontal position and a fixed offset from the top viewport edge until the bottom of the left column content is reached, at which point it scrolls off normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-submit-form is tightly integrated with the registration-summary-panel in the right-hand stack, visually forming the bottom portion of that card cluster. In the hero, it appears immediately beneath the summary card, separated by 16px of vertical space, and aligned to the same width and right edge. The main “Register Now” button spans nearly the full width of the right column, leaving 16–24px of horizontal padding on each side, with 12–16px vertical padding inside the button itself, echoing the generous CTAs from the mockup. Any secondary elements (such as terms text or a checkbox) sit directly below the button with 8–12px vertical spacing. As the page scrolls, the button and related controls remain visually grouped with the sticky summary panel, ensuring that the total cost and the primary action are always adjacent. The bottom of this group maintains at least 48px of margin above the bottom of the viewport when sticky, preventing a cramped feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Moves with the registration-summary-panel as part of a sticky right-hand CTA cluster; when the summary panel sticks, the submit form’s main button sticks directly beneath it within the same fixed-right column space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful submission, the registration-confirmation-panel replaces the form-focused content in the left column, starting at the same vertical position where the participants-list-form previously began. It spans the same 7–8 column left width and is separated from the bottom of the hero by a comfortable 48–64px gap. The panel is a visually celebratory card with a white or light background, possibly using a band of vivid brand color (yellow or blue) along the top edge, and 24–32px of internal padding. A bold confirmation heading sits at the top-left, with concise summary text and next-step options stacked beneath, separated by 16px vertical spacing. The right-hand registration-summary-panel remains visible, now reinforcing the confirmed registration details instead of a pre-submit total. The overall composition maintains the two-column balance, with the confirmation card roughly centered vertically in the viewport alongside the still-sticky summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, but it appears conditionally, replacing the participants and primary-contact forms; it scrolls normally while the summary panel on the right may remain sticky for a brief distance depending on page length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-events-link is positioned at the very top-left of the main content area, just below any global error banner and below the site header. It sits on a white or very light background above the hero photo, aligned to the same left margin as the event-info-section. There is about 24px of vertical space between this link and the start of the hero image, and 16–24px of margin above it to separate it from any banners. The link itself is styled like a compact text-plus-icon pill with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, ensuring it is easily tappable but visually subordinate to the hero content below. It acts as the first point in the F-pattern, catching the eye briefly before attention moves down and into the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the content column; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-error-banner appears directly beneath the main site header and above all event-specific content, including the back-to-events-link and hero. It spans the full width of the viewport, matching the header’s width and aligning with the content column edges. The banner uses a strong, high-contrast color (such as a saturated red overlaid with a subtle transparency) and contains concise error messaging centered vertically within 16–24px of padding. It adds approximately 48px of height when visible, pushing the hero and all other content downward but maintaining the same overall layout proportions. The banner’s prominence ensures that global issues, such as loading failures or registration-wide errors, are perceived before users engage with the hero or forms.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow under the header; when present, it occupies its own row and pushes content down. It does not stick on scroll, to avoid competing with the sticky registration summary.

name: Fundraising Campaigns
title: Fundraising Campaigns
description: Page where users learn how personal fundraising supports child hunger relief and select an active fundraising campaign to fundraise for.

core_features: Load all fundraising campaigns from the backend using getFundraisingCampaignsList and keep them in in-memory page state.
Display an introductory explanation of how personal (peer-to-peer) fundraising works and how it supports child hunger relief.
Filter the campaign list by status (e.g., active, upcoming, archived) and campaign type (e.g., peer_to_peer) using URL parameters to represent the current filters.
Sort campaigns (e.g., recommended, ending soon, newest, alphabetical) using URL parameters for sort state and client-side sorting of the loaded list.
Highlight a subset of campaigns considered seasonal or urgent (e.g., ending soon or currently active with approaching end dates), based on FundraisingCampaign fields.
Display campaigns in a card-based list; when a user clicks a card, navigate to fundraising_campaign.html with the campaignId URL parameter populated from the selected campaign’s id.
Support pagination of the campaign list on the client side, with the current page encoded in a URL parameter.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for the campaign list, with retry behavior if getFundraisingCampaignsList fails.
user_workflows: Browse and choose a campaign to fundraise for: (1) User navigates to fundraise.html via header, footer, or direct link. (2) On page load, the page calls getFundraisingCampaignsList and stores the returned FundraisingCampaign array in state. (3) The introductory section explains what personal fundraising is and how it helps address child hunger. (4) The filter bar reads any existing URL parameters (status, campaignType, sort, page) and applies those filters and sort to the loaded list. (5) The urgent/seasonal highlight component identifies a subset of campaigns (e.g., active campaigns close to their endDate) and displays them prominently. (6) The main campaign list shows the filtered, sorted, paginated campaigns in card format. (7) The user reviews cards and clicks the one they want to fundraise for. (8) The click event navigates the browser to fundraising_campaign.html?campaignId=<selected_campaign_id>.

Adjust filters and sort order: (1) User changes a filter (e.g., selects a different status or campaign type) or sorting option in the filter bar. (2) The filter component updates the in-memory filter state and resets the page number to the first page. (3) The filter component updates the URL parameters (status, campaignType, sort, page) without reloading the page, so the URL reflects the new state. (4) The campaign list recomputes the filtered and sorted subset from the loaded FundraisingCampaign array and re-renders the cards. (5) If no campaigns match the filters, an empty-state message is shown with an option to clear filters.

Focus on urgent or seasonal campaigns: (1) User arrives on fundraise.html with an optional focus URL parameter (e.g., focus=urgent). (2) The URL state manager reads focus and sets a local focus state. (3) The urgent highlight component filters the campaigns to those considered urgent based on the configured rules applied to FundraisingCampaign fields (e.g., active status and endDate close in time). (4) If focus=urgent, the page may prioritize that section (e.g., presenting it first and optionally auto-scrolling). (5) The user can click any highlighted campaign card to navigate to fundraising_campaign.html?campaignId=<id> or click a control to jump to the full list view.

Paginate through campaigns: (1) User uses pagination controls (next/previous or direct page selection) at the bottom of the campaign list. (2) The campaign list component updates the in-memory current page state and writes the new page value to the URL parameter page. (3) The same loaded list is re-sliced client-side according to the current page, and the displayed cards update. (4) The user can share or reload the URL and return to the same pagination and filter state since it is encoded in URL parameters.

Recover from loading errors: (1) On page load, if getFundraisingCampaignsList fails, the data-loader component sets an error state. (2) An error-state component is displayed with a retry button. (3) When the user clicks retry, the page calls getFundraisingCampaignsList again. (4) On success, error state is cleared, and the campaign list and highlight components render as normal.
interactions: On page load, automatically call getFundraisingCampaignsList, then apply URL-based filters and sorting to the returned FundraisingCampaign list.
On filter change (status or campaign type), update in-memory filter state, reset pagination to the first page, and synchronize the new state to the URL parameters.
On sort option change, update in-memory sort state, synchronize to URL parameters, and re-sort the in-memory campaign list.
On pagination control interaction, update the current page state, write the new page to the URL, and re-slice the campaign list for display.
On click of a campaign card in either the urgent highlight strip or the main list, navigate to fundraising_campaign.html?campaignId=<id>, where <id> is the card’s bound FundraisingCampaign.id.
On click of a 'clear filters' or 'show all campaigns' action in an empty state or filter bar, reset filter-related URL parameters to defaults and recompute the visible campaign list.
On retry click in the error state, re-trigger getFundraisingCampaignsList and re-render list components on success.

On URL change (e.g., browser back/forward navigation), re-parse URL parameters and update filter, sort, page, and focus states without re-calling getFundraisingCampaignsList, re-rendering the list according to the new parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters to drive and reflect state. The campaigns themselves are fetched once per page load using getFundraisingCampaignsList and stored in memory; all filtering, sorting, and pagination are applied client-side.

URL parameters:
- status: Controls which FundraisingCampaign.status values are included in the main list. Expected values align with the FundraisingCampaign.status enum (active, upcoming, archived). A special value (such as all) can indicate no status filtering. If status is absent, the default behavior is to show campaigns intended for personal fundraising (typically active campaigns, with optional inclusion of upcoming ones, as defined by implementation rules).
- campaignType: Controls filtering by FundraisingCampaign.campaignType. Expected values are the FundraisingCampaign.campaignType enum members (peer_to_peer, organizational, match) or an 'all' case for no type filtering. If absent, the default can prioritize campaign types that support personal fundraising, such as peer_to_peer.
- sort: Controls client-side sorting of the visible campaign list. Supported values are implementation-defined strings such as recommended, ending_soon, newest, or alphabetical. The component maps each sort value to a comparator function that uses FundraisingCampaign fields (e.g., endDate, startDate, name). If sort is absent, a default sort mode (e.g., recommended) is used.
- page: Represents the current list page (1-based integer). When page is absent or invalid, the first page is used. Changing filters or sort resets page to the first page.
- focus: Represents an optional focus mode (e.g., urgent). If focus has a value that corresponds to a known focus mode, certain components (such as the urgent highlight) alter their behavior, e.g., emphasizing urgent campaigns. If absent or unknown, the page behaves in its standard mode.

State interactions:
- On initial load, the URL state manager reads status, campaignType, sort, page, and focus from the URL. It sets internal state accordingly and passes it to the filter, list, and highlight components.
- When the user changes filters, sort, or page via UI controls, the corresponding component updates internal state and then updates the browser URL parameters to match, without full reload.
- When the user navigates with browser back/forward, the URL state manager detects changes in URL parameters (via popstate or equivalent) and updates the internal state, causing the filter bar, highlight strip, and campaign list to re-render to match the URL.
- getFundraisingCampaignsList is not parameterized; it is called only based on page lifecycle (e.g., initial load or explicit retry), not on filter changes. All list manipulation after loading is entirely client-side using the stored FundraisingCampaign array.

components.functionality: On page initialization (and on explicit retry), calls getFundraisingCampaignsList to retrieve all fundraising campaigns. Stores the resulting array of FundraisingCampaign objects in page-level state, along with loading and error flags. Exposes this state to dependent components (filter bar, highlight strip, campaign list). Does not apply filtering, sorting, or pagination; it only fetches and caches the raw data.

Parses and manages URL parameters that represent page state: status, campaignType, sort, page, and focus. On page load, reads these parameters and initializes shared filter/sort/pagination/focus state objects. On any state change triggered by user interactions (filter changes, sorting, pagination, or focus toggles), updates the URL parameters to match the new state without reloading the page. Listens for browser navigation events and updates internal state when URL parameters change, ensuring the UI remains in sync with the URL.

Displays explanatory text describing how personal (peer-to-peer) fundraising works, how supporters create their own fundraising pages tied to a campaign, and how funds raised contribute to child hunger relief programs. May include a brief, generic outline of steps (choose a campaign, create a page on the campaign detail screen, share link, receive donations). Does not depend on dynamic data and is purely informational.

Provides interactive controls for filtering and sorting the campaign list. Includes controls mapped to URL-driven state, such as: (1) a status selector bound to an internal status filter that restricts campaigns based on FundraisingCampaign.status, (2) a campaignType selector bound to a campaign type filter that restricts campaigns based on FundraisingCampaign.campaignType, and (3) a sort selector bound to sort mode (e.g., recommended, ending_soon, newest, alphabetical). When the user changes selections, the component updates shared filter and sort state, resets the current page to the first page, and triggers the url-state-manager to update the URL parameters. It also has an optional 'clear filters' or equivalent control that resets filters and sort to default values.

Displays a subset of campaigns considered urgent or seasonally important. It receives the full FundraisingCampaign list and current filter/sort/focus state. It applies internal rules to identify urgent or seasonal campaigns using fields such as FundraisingCampaign.status and FundraisingCampaign.endDate (e.g., active campaigns whose endDate is within a configured time window). It can optionally respect campaignType filters if configured to do so. When focus state indicates urgent emphasis, this component may display more prominently. Each highlighted campaign is presented as a concise card and is fully clickable to navigate to its campaign detail page. It can also include a control that scrolls or jumps the user to the full campaign list.

Displays the main list of campaigns in a paginated, filterable, and sortable grid or list. Receives the full FundraisingCampaign array along with current status filter, campaignType filter, sort mode, and page number from shared state. Applies filters to exclude campaigns that do not match state (e.g., status and campaignType), applies the selected sort order using FundraisingCampaign fields (such as endDate, startDate, and name), and then slices the resulting array according to the current page and configured page size. If the filtered result is empty, it shows the list-empty-state component instead of campaign cards. For successful non-empty results, it renders a collection of campaign-card instances.

Represents a single fundraising campaign entry in either the urgent highlight or main campaign list. Binds directly to a single FundraisingCampaign instance. Displays core functional information such as the campaign name, a short portion of the description, optional date range derived from startDate and endDate, and optional high-level metadata derived from defaultGoalAmount or status. The entire card is clickable. When clicked, it triggers navigation to fundraising_campaign.html?campaignId=<FundraisingCampaign.id>. It may also expose an explicit 'View campaign' action that fires the same navigation handler.

Provides controls for navigating between pages of the filtered campaign list. Receives the total number of campaigns after filters are applied and the configured page size to calculate the total number of pages. Displays current page (derived from URL/page state) and allows users to move to the next/previous page or directly select a specific page. When a user interacts with the controls, it updates the shared page state, which triggers the url-state-manager to update the page URL parameter and causes the campaigns-list component to re-slice and re-render the list.

Displays when the filtered campaign list contains no FundraisingCampaign entries. Shows a short explanatory message indicating that no campaigns match the current filters and offers an action (such as 'clear filters' or 'show all campaigns'). When this action is used, it resets filter-related state to defaults via the filter bar and url-state-manager, triggering a new filtering pass that likely restores visible campaigns.

Displays when getFundraisingCampaignsList fails in the campaigns-data-loader component. Shows a functional error message and provides a retry control. When the user activates retry, it signals the data-loader to call getFundraisingCampaignsList again. On a successful retry, this component is hidden in favor of the normal campaign list and highlight components.

Shows a loading indicator while getFundraisingCampaignsList is in progress. It becomes visible when the campaigns-data-loader sets a loading flag before an API call and hides automatically when loading is complete (either success or error). It does not manage any data directly; it only reflects the data-loader's loading state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header on top (outside this spec), followed by a full-width hero-style band that contains the 'fundraising-intro' on the left and the 'urgent-campaigns-highlight' stack on the right, over a darkened background photo. Beneath this hero band, the 'campaigns-filter-bar' runs horizontally as a slim control strip, followed by the main 'campaigns-list' grid occupying most of the scrollable area, with 'campaigns-pagination' at the bottom. State and data components (data-loader, url-state-manager, loading/empty/error states) are layered into the list area without breaking the primary vertical structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header on top (outside this spec), followed by a full-width hero-style band that contains the 'fundraising-intro' on the left and the 'urgent-campaigns-highlight' stack on the right, over a darkened background photo. Beneath this hero band, the 'campaigns-filter-bar' runs horizontally as a slim control strip, followed by the main 'campaigns-list' grid occupying most of the scrollable area, with 'campaigns-pagination' at the bottom. State and data components (data-loader, url-state-manager, loading/empty/error states) are layered into the list area without breaking the primary vertical structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero-style band with 'fundraising-intro' and 'urgent-campaigns-highlight' occupies roughly the first viewport height beneath the header. Inside this band, the left content column for the intro takes about 55–60% of the constrained content width and the right urgent highlight stack uses around 30–35%, leaving a slim right gutter for the vertical social bar motif. Below the hero, the filter bar is shallow (about one standard row of controls) and spans the constrained content width. The campaigns list stretches down the page, using the full content width with a multi-column card grid. Major vertical gaps between the hero, filter bar, list, and pagination sit in the 48–64px range, with 24–32px between stacked elements within each section.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays moderate: the hero band focuses on a concise headline, short copy, and one or two CTAs on the left, balanced by a focused vertical stack of 2–3 urgent highlight cards on the right. The filter bar maintains a single row of essential controls only. The campaign list shows a manageable number of cards per viewport (e.g., 6–9 on desktop), each with clear hierarchy and breathing room via generous internal padding. Empty, error, and loading states are intentionally sparse, using a single illustration or icon plus a short message, ensuring the page never feels cluttered even when multiple system messages are in play.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern: users first hit the top-left brand logo and nav (outside this scope), then their gaze is pulled across the top by a bright primary header CTA. From there, the eye drops diagonally into the hero to the large, bold 'Fundraising Campaigns' / mission statement on the left, then moves right to the vivid urgent highlight stack (echoing the bright yellow donate block). After scanning the urgent cards downward, attention naturally falls to the filter bar directly beneath the hero, then scans down into the grid of campaign cards. Pagination is discovered at the bottom as users reach the end of available content, while the vertical social share bar at the far right remains a tertiary, always-visible pathway for sharing.

layout.overall_description: The Fundraising Campaigns page extends the hero-driven, two-column layout from the original mockup into a campaign selection experience. Below the familiar white header, a full-width hero band with a darkened photo of children or volunteers sets a warm yet focused tone. Inside a centered, constrained content container, the left two-thirds of this hero is dominated by a bold, stacked 'Fundraising Campaigns' heading, a short, kid-centric explainer about how personal fundraising fights child hunger, and a primary 'Start a Fundraiser' style CTA. On the right third, a tall, slim column echoes the original donate stack: a bright, colored top card highlights the most urgent campaign, with one or two smaller urgent cards stacked beneath, all framed as clickable entry points. Along the far-right edge of the viewport, a narrow vertical yellow strip holds social share icons, running independently of the main grid. Below the hero, a clean, pill-based filter bar spans the content width, allowing users to refine campaigns. Under that, a multi-column grid of colorful campaign cards fills the screen, each card resembling a slightly larger, more detailed version of the hero’s trust badges. Loading, empty, or error states replace the grid with centered, minimal panels when needed, and pagination sits at the bottom center with ample breathing room. Throughout, sections are separated with generous vertical spacing and strong alignment, blending a playful nonprofit brand personality with a disciplined, corporate-leaning structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual component positioned logically at the top of the main content area, conceptually just inside the page’s primary content container and above the hero band in the source order. It does not create visible space or alter the grid; instead, it orchestrates when the 'fundraising-intro', 'urgent-campaigns-highlight', 'campaigns-filter-bar', and 'campaigns-list' become populated. When data is being fetched, it triggers the 'loading-indicator' within the campaigns list region rather than occupying any standalone area on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; occupies no physical space in the layout and scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is an invisible layer tied to the main campaigns area, conceptually anchored alongside the data loader at the top of the content section. It influences which campaigns are shown, which page of pagination is active, and what filters are applied, but it does not present any visible UI. It is effectively layered behind the 'campaigns-filter-bar', 'campaigns-list', and 'campaigns-pagination', wiring their interactions to the browser URL without adding any extra space, borders, or visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; integrated into the page’s logic only, with no independent positioning or spacing in the rendered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fundraising intro forms the left column of the main hero band directly under the header. The hero itself spans the full width of the viewport with a darkened photographic background. Inside a centered, max-width content container (roughly 1120–1200px), the intro occupies about 55–60% of the width on desktop. It is vertically centered within the hero height, which fills roughly 85–90% of the initial viewport beneath the header. The intro’s content is arranged in a tall, left-aligned stack: a small overline or label at the top, a large bold multi-line heading beneath (mirroring the 'No / Child / Hungry' vertical impact from the mockup), a short 2–3 line mission statement, and a horizontal row of primary and secondary CTAs. Each text block is separated by 16–24px, with about 48px of breathing room above the heading and below the CTA row. The right edge of this intro column aligns visually with the left edge of the 'urgent-campaigns-highlight' column, maintaining a clear two-column composition within the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the hero; it scrolls with the page and maintains its proportional width within the responsive grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The campaigns filter bar sits immediately below the hero band, separated from it by a generous 48–64px vertical gap to respect the spaciousness of the design. It is contained within the same centered max-width content container as the hero’s inner content, spanning roughly 100% of that container width (about 80–90% of the viewport on large screens). Visually, it presents as a shallow, pill-based control strip: filters are arranged in a single row where possible, with dropdowns, chips, and a search box left-aligned. On desktop, the filter bar height is modest (around 56–72px including padding), with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding around the controls. There is 24–32px of space below the filter bar before the top of the 'campaigns-list', letting it feel like a distinct yet tightly connected control layer over the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that scrolls with the page; optionally can become sticky at the top of the viewport when the user scrolls past the hero, but always maintains the same horizontal alignment as the main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The urgent campaigns highlight occupies the right column of the hero band, echoing the narrow vertical donate stack from the mockup. Inside the same max-width content container as the fundraising intro, this highlight column takes approximately 30–35% of the content width on desktop and is aligned to the right edge of the container. It is vertically aligned so that its top sits roughly in line with the middle third of the fundraising intro, creating a gentle stagger that feels dynamic rather than rigid. Within this column, a dominant top card (about 60–70% of the column height) uses a vivid brand color background (often yellow) to showcase a primary urgent campaign, with bold title, short description, and a compact CTA button. Directly beneath this, separated by 16–24px, one or two smaller urgent cards are stacked vertically, each with white or lightly tinted backgrounds, subtle borders, and 16–24px padding, mirroring the trust/testimonial badges in the original mockup. The entire stack floats visually over the darkened hero photo, with soft shadow and rounded corners ensuring legibility and touch-friendly feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the hero’s right column; scrolls with the page and remains aligned to the right within the hero’s content container. On smaller screens, it stacks beneath the 'fundraising-intro' while keeping its vertical card stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The campaigns list forms the main body section beneath the filter bar. It is housed in the same centered max-width container used above, beginning about 24–32px below the bottom of the 'campaigns-filter-bar'. The list uses a 12-column responsive grid, where each 'campaign-card' spans roughly 4 columns on large desktops (3 cards per row), 6 columns on tablets (2 per row), and full width on narrow mobile screens. Horizontal gutters between cards are around 24px, with vertical spacing between card rows at 32–40px. At the top of the list area there is a subtle section title or result summary aligned to the left, with about 16–24px of space before the first row of cards. The entire list region has ample padding at the bottom (about 64px) to make room for 'campaigns-pagination' and to prevent visual crowding. Empty, error, and loading states appear centered within this same rectangular zone, temporarily replacing the card grid while maintaining the same overall footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content block; cards flow in rows within a responsive grid. System states (loading/empty/error) occupy this block’s area without changing its overall position on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each campaign card is a discrete tile within the campaigns list grid, visually inspired by the trust cards under the donate stack in the mockup but scaled up with more detail. On desktop, a card fills approximately one-third of the content width (4 of 12 columns), with a fixed ratio image or color block at the top spanning the full card width. Below the media, the card content is vertically stacked with 16–24px internal padding: a small category label or badge, a bold campaign title on 2 lines, a short 2–3 line description, and a progress or impact indicator (such as a progress bar or key metric). At the bottom, right-aligned or full-width, sits a primary or secondary CTA button with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Cards have subtle rounded corners and a faint shadow or 1px border to stand off from the white background, with 16px minimum space between cards on all sides within the grid. Hover states can mimic the playful energy of the brand via slight elevation and color tints while respecting the clean layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard grid items that expand/shrink responsively based on the number of columns per breakpoint; they remain within the campaigns list’s flow and scroll naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The campaigns pagination sits at the bottom of the campaigns list section, appearing about 40–48px below the last row of campaign cards. It is horizontally centered within the same content container, spanning roughly 60–80% of the width but visually concentrated in the center, following the mockup’s preference for balanced, minimalist navigational elements. Pagination controls (previous/next, page numbers) are arranged in a single row, with each button given pill-like shapes, 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, and consistent gaps of 8–12px between them. The area above and below the controls remains open, ensuring that even with many pages, the pagination row feels airy and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the bottom of the list; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the campaign cards and remains in normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no campaigns match the current filters, the empty state occupies the same rectangular area as the 'campaigns-list'. Within that region, content is centered both horizontally and, when viewport height allows, vertically between the filter bar above and the pagination space below. The empty state appears as a single, friendly card or panel about 50–60% of the content width, with generous 32–40px internal padding. It includes a simple illustration or icon related to children/fundraising at the top, a short headline, a one-line explanatory message, and a clear CTA to adjust filters or explore all campaigns again. The surrounding area remains mostly blank, mirroring the mockup’s use of negative space to keep system feedback calm and readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the grid of campaign cards within the campaigns list area when active; remains centered and scrolls with the page as normal content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also inhabits the campaigns list region, appearing in place of campaign cards whenever a loading or data error occurs. It is visually similar in size and positioning to the empty state: a centered card roughly 50–60% of the content width with 32–40px internal padding. The card uses a slightly more urgent color accent (e.g., orange or warm red border or icon) while preserving the overall clean, nonprofit aesthetic. Inside, a bold, short error headline is followed by a concise explanation line and a primary action such as 'Try again' or 'Reload campaigns'. There is ample white space above and below the content, ensuring the message stands out without overwhelming the user or disrupting the page’s structural calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the campaign card grid within the campaigns list area when an error is present; remains centered and scrolls in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is layered over the campaigns list region whenever data is being fetched, following the mockup’s pattern of isolating key actions against darker backgrounds. When active, a semi-transparent overlay covers the entire campaigns list area from just below the filter bar down to just above where pagination would appear. At the center of this overlay sits a compact loading cluster: either a spinner or small animated icon accompanied by a short, friendly line of text like 'Loading campaigns…'. Optionally, skeleton card placeholders can align to the underlying grid to suggest the location and size of incoming campaign cards, maintaining the sense of structure even while content is unavailable. The rest of the page (header, hero, filter bar) remains visible and static, with the overlay confined strictly to the list area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the campaigns list region without shifting any underlying layout; it is fixed to the bounds of that section and scrolls away once loading is complete or when the user scrolls past the list area.

name: Fundraising Campaign Detail & Page Creation
title: Fundraising Campaign Details
description: Displays details for a selected fundraising campaign and lets users create a personal fundraising page with a custom goal, story, and shareable link.

core_features: Load and display the selected fundraising campaign details using getFundraisingCampaignDetails based on URL parameters.
Show a 'Back to all campaigns' navigation link returning the user to the campaigns listing page.
Provide a 'Create Fundraising Page' action that reveals and focuses a personal fundraising page creation form.
Allow the user to configure a personal fundraising page: title, fundraising goal amount, story/description (with a minimum length), and a custom URL ending (slug).
Allow the user to choose whether the personal fundraising page is an individual or team page (isTeamPage flag).
Validate form input before submission (required fields, numeric goal, minimum story length, valid URL slug format).
On form submission, call createPersonalFundraisingPage and, on success, show a confirmation state on the same page including the generated full URL.
Display any error or informational messages returned by createPersonalFundraisingPage when creation fails.
Provide a 'Copy link' button next to the personal fundraising page URL that calls copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink and copies the link to the clipboard.
Show feedback when the link is successfully copied, using data returned by copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink (e.g., success flag, message, lastLinkCopiedAt).
user_workflows: View campaign details workflow: (1) User arrives at fundraising_campaign.html with campaignId and/or campaignSlug in the URL. (2) Page calls getFundraisingCampaignDetails with the provided parameters. (3) While loading, the page shows a loading state in place of campaign content. (4) On success, campaign details (name, description, dates, status, type, default goal amount, primary image) are displayed. (5) If loading fails or the campaign is not found, an error state is shown with an option to return to the campaigns listing.

Create a personal fundraising page workflow: (1) From the campaign detail view, the user selects the 'Create Fundraising Page' button. (2) The page scrolls to and focuses the personal fundraising page form, which is associated with the loaded campaign. (3) The user enters a page title, goal amount, story, and custom URL ending, and selects whether it is an individual or team page. (4) On form submission, client-side validation runs (required fields, minimum story length, numeric goal, slug format). (5) If validation passes, the page calls createPersonalFundraisingPage with campaignId, title, story, goalAmount, customUrlSlug, and isTeamPage. (6) If the API response success flag is false, the error message is displayed and the form remains editable. (7) If success is true, the form is locked or hidden and a confirmation state appears showing the created personal fundraising page details and its fullUrl.

Copy and share personal fundraising link workflow: (1) After a personal fundraising page is successfully created, the confirmation section shows the generated URL. (2) The user clicks the 'Copy link' button. (3) The page calls copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink with the personalFundraisingPage.id received from the creation response. (4) If success is true, the returned fullUrl is copied to the clipboard and the UI briefly shows a copy success message, possibly including an indication based on lastLinkCopiedAt. (5) If success is false, an error message from the response is shown and no success indication is displayed.

Navigate back to campaigns listing workflow: (1) From any state where campaign details are visible (including after creating a personal page), the user clicks 'Back to all campaigns'. (2) The browser navigates to fundraise.html without additional parameters.
interactions: On page load, the script reads URL parameters campaignId and campaignSlug. If at least one is present, it triggers getFundraisingCampaignDetails; otherwise, it shows an error or navigation prompt.
If both campaignId and campaignSlug are present, both are passed to getFundraisingCampaignDetails as provided by the URL, and the response determines which campaign is shown.
Clicking the 'Create Fundraising Page' button toggles the visibility of the personal fundraising page form, moves keyboard focus to the first field, and may scroll the page to keep the form in view.
Typing in the goal amount field updates a local numeric state bound to the goalAmount parameter for createPersonalFundraisingPage, with inline validation for numeric and greater-than-zero values.
Typing in the title field updates the bound title state that will be sent to createPersonalFundraisingPage, with inline required-field validation.

Typing in the story field updates the bound story state and a live character/word count; if the story length is below the configured minimum word count, the form shows a validation message and prevents submission.
Typing in the custom URL ending field updates the bound customUrlSlug state, with validation for allowed characters and required non-empty value.
Toggling the individual/team selection updates the isTeamPage boolean bound to createPersonalFundraisingPage.

Submitting the personal fundraising page form triggers client-side validation; if validation passes, it calls createPersonalFundraisingPage with the current field values and disables the submit button until a response is received.

On success from createPersonalFundraisingPage, the page stores the returned PersonalFundraisingPage object in state, reveals the confirmation view with the fullUrl, and may hide or collapse the creation form.
On failure from createPersonalFundraisingPage, the page displays the returned message near the form, re-enables the submit button, and leaves the user-entered data intact for corrections.

Clicking the 'Copy link' button in the confirmation view calls copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink with the personalFundraisingPage.id; on a successful response, the returned fullUrl is copied to the clipboard and the UI updates with a non-intrusive success message.
If copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink returns an error (success is false), an error message is shown instead of a success confirmation.
Clicking 'Back to all campaigns' triggers a navigation to fundraise.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads URL parameters campaignId and campaignSlug on load. If neither is present, the page enters an 'invalid URL' state where no campaign is loaded and only an explanatory message and link to fundraise.html are shown. If at least one parameter is present, the page calls getFundraisingCampaignDetails with both parameters as provided. While waiting for the API response, a loading state is active for all campaign-dependent components. When the API returns a FundraisingCampaign object, the page stores it in a campaign state object; this state is required before the personal fundraising page form can be interacted with, and the form is disabled until the campaign is successfully loaded. If getFundraisingCampaignDetails fails or returns no campaign, the page enters an error state where the form and creation features are hidden or disabled, and an error message plus navigation back to fundraise.html is presented. The personal fundraising page creation state is local to the current page session and includes the current form values, validation status, pending submission status, and the created PersonalFundraisingPage object once received from createPersonalFundraisingPage. The confirmation view is shown only when there is a successfully created PersonalFundraisingPage in state. The page does not rely on localStorage; all state derives from the current URL parameters and the responses of getFundraisingCampaignDetails, createPersonalFundraisingPage, and copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink during the active session.

components.functionality: Parses campaignId and campaignSlug from the page URL, calls getFundraisingCampaignDetails with those parameters, and manages loading, success, and error states for downstream components that depend on the FundraisingCampaign data.

Displays key information about the loaded fundraising campaign, such as the campaign name, status, type, and date range. Provides the 'Back to all campaigns' navigation link and the primary 'Create Fundraising Page' button that focuses the creation form.

Shows the campaign description and any additional campaign-specific informational fields (e.g., default goal amount, primary image), so users understand the purpose and context before creating a personal fundraiser.

Controls the visibility and focus of the personal fundraising page creation form. When triggered by the 'Create Fundraising Page' button, it ensures the form is shown and the first input field receives focus. It can also collapse the form if needed.

Collects user input for creating a personal fundraising page linked to the current campaign. Fields include title, goal amount, story, custom URL ending, and an individual/team selector. Performs client-side validation before calling createPersonalFundraisingPage.
Displays validation errors and API response messages related to personal fundraising page creation, including errors returned by createPersonalFundraisingPage when the success flag is false.

Shown after a successful personal fundraising page creation. Displays a summary of the created page details (title, goal, and status) and prominently shows the shareable URL (fullUrl) received from createPersonalFundraisingPage, along with the 'Copy link' control.

Provides the 'Copy link' button next to the personal fundraising page URL. When clicked, calls copyPersonalFundraisingPageLink with the current PersonalFundraisingPage id, copies the returned fullUrl to the clipboard, and updates the UI with success or error feedback based on the API response.
Displays an error message and navigation options when getFundraisingCampaignDetails fails or returns no campaign, and hides or disables all components that require a valid FundraisingCampaign.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header, the page opens with a hero-like campaign detail header that spans full width and most of the first viewport, mirroring the original hero pattern. Inside this band, content follows a two-column composition: the left ~60% shows campaign identity and key stats, and the right ~30% is a narrow vertical stack dedicated to creating a personal fundraising page (toggle, status, form or confirmation, and copy-link control). Under this hero band, the left column continues with a longer campaign description, while the right column continues the card stack. Error and loading states either overlay or temporarily replace this hero region.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header, the page opens with a hero-like campaign detail header that spans full width and most of the first viewport, mirroring the original hero pattern. Inside this band, content follows a two-column composition: the left ~60% shows campaign identity and key stats, and the right ~30% is a narrow vertical stack dedicated to creating a personal fundraising page (toggle, status, form or confirmation, and copy-link control). Under this hero band, the left column continues with a longer campaign description, while the right column continues the card stack. Error and loading states either overlay or temporarily replace this hero region.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The campaign detail header band occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height with a darkened photographic background. Within it, the left column takes about 55–60% of the content width, the right creation column about 25–30%, with the remaining margin acting as breathing room. Below the hero, vertical spacing between major sections (header band and description panel) uses 48–64px, and between elements in the right column stack uses 24–32px. Loader and error states occupy the central 50–60% width of the hero band, visually dominating while active.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate: the hero band highlights only essential information—campaign title, key stats, and the primary 'create your page' action—against a simplified, darkened photo. The right column uses card-like containers with generous padding to separate form fields, confirmation info, and link controls from the busy background. The description panel below can hold more text but is broken with headings and whitespace to keep it airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left brand in the global header, then moves across to the top-right where the personal-page-form-toggle card sits as a bright primary CTA. From there, it drops diagonally into the hero’s left center where the campaign title and stats sit, then glances back right to the stacked creation card (form or confirmation). Scrolling down, the user follows the left column into the detailed campaign description, while the right column remains visually cohesive (and partially sticky) so the primary action and resulting share link stay in peripheral view.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a focused campaign hero plus a right-hand creation rail. Under the global site header, a full-width hero band with a darkened photo background introduces the campaign. On the left, the campaign-detail-header’s text—title, short subtitle, and high-level stats—sits in a tall block occupying over half the width, echoing the bold brand mark from the reference mockup. On the right, a bright, card-like column begins with the personal-page-form-toggle at the very top, followed by slim creation-status-messages, and then either the personal-fundraising-page-form or the personal-page-confirmation-view with the fundraising-link-copy-control integrated inside. This right column is visually similar to the donate/payment/trust stack from the mockup hero: a strong CTA at the top and supporting elements below. As users scroll, the left side transitions into a content section where the campaign-description-panel spans roughly two-thirds of the width, maintaining alignment with the hero’s left column, while the right column continues the stacked creation card. Loader and error states center themselves over or within the hero band so that system feedback is immediately visible before content or form interaction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the campaign-loader appears as a semi-opaque dark overlay covering the entire campaign hero region and the first portion of the content, starting immediately below the global site header and extending down approximately one full viewport height. At the center of this overlay, a compact loading indicator and short status text sit in a small, rounded card spanning about 25–30% of the page width, horizontally centered and vertically centered within the visible area. Underneath, the campaign-detail-header, right-hand creation column, and description panel are dimmed but non-interactive, clearly signaling a loading state while keeping the spatial structure visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport while active, covering underlying components in the hero and upper content area; scroll is disabled or visually blocked beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The campaign-detail-header occupies a full-width hero band immediately below the global navigation, with a height of roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport. A darkened campaign photo fills this band edge-to-edge. Inside, content is constrained to a centered column following a 12-column grid. The left 6–7 columns (about 55–60% width) contain the campaign’s text: a small breadcrumb or category at the top left, then the campaign title as a large, bold heading positioned slightly above vertical center, followed by a short, one or two-line description. Beneath the description, a horizontal row of key stats (goal, raised, supporters) is aligned along the bottom of this left block. The right 3–4 columns (about 25–30% width), aligned to the right margin of the content area and starting near the top of the hero, are reserved as the anchor area for the personal-page-form-toggle card and its vertical stack, visually echoing the donation stack from the reference mockup. The bottom 10–15% of the hero band remains mostly open, allowing the left and right content blocks to float within the photographic background without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow; the background image spans full width while the inner content remains centered and constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The campaign-description-panel sits directly below the campaign-detail-header, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. It aligns with the same centered content width and grid. The panel occupies roughly the left two-thirds of the available width (about 7–8 of 12 columns), starting flush with the left edge of the campaign-detail-header content. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the upper portion of the right-hand creation column so the two feel like a single, two-column section when viewed together. Inside the panel, the content is vertically stacked: section heading at the top, followed by rich text (paragraphs, lists, optional imagery), each separated by 24–32px vertical spacing. The rightmost third of the page, in line with this panel, remains reserved for the ongoing personal fundraising column, maintaining the same column split established in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow, extending downward as needed; its height grows with content while the right column visually tracks alongside.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personal-page-form-toggle appears as the topmost element in the right-hand column that begins inside the campaign-detail-header. Positioned in the upper-right quadrant of the hero band, it sits flush with the right edge of the constrained content area and vertically aligned slightly below the top of the hero, mirroring the location of the bright donate button in the reference mockup. The toggle itself is a bold, pill-like or card-like control spanning roughly the full width of this right column (about 3–4 grid columns, 25–30% of the page width), with ample padding (around 16–24px on all sides) and a vivid brand color background (e.g., yellow). It stands out against the darkened photo, signaling the primary action to 'Create your fundraising page' or to switch between viewing and editing. A small amount of space (16–24px) separates this toggle from any element above (such as the top of the hero) and 24–32px from the creation-status-messages below, establishing it as the first touchpoint in the right-hand stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as the anchored top of the right column; on taller screens it may remain near the top of the viewport together with the right column’s card stack (via sticky behavior) until the hero region scrolls out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personal-fundraising-page-form resides directly beneath the creation-status-messages in the right-hand column, forming the main body of the vertical stack. It occupies the same width as the toggle (about 3–4 grid columns, 25–30% of the page) and is presented as a high-contrast card with a white or very light background on top of the darker hero/photo or solid section color. Its top edge starts within the lower half of the hero band and continues down into the content area, so that when users scroll, the left campaign-description-panel extends while this form card remains visually tied to the top-right column. Inside the card, form fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, and the primary submit button is anchored near the bottom of the card, spanning most of the card’s width, similar in visual weight to the hero’s donate CTA. The entire card has 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px external spacing below, separating it from any subsequent elements such as the confirmation panel or link control when in alternate states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only when the toggle is in a 'create/edit' state; scrolls with the page but can be configured to be sticky within the right column on larger viewports so it remains partially in view while the left description scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The creation-status-messages component sits in the right-hand column immediately below the personal-page-form-toggle and above the personal-fundraising-page-form or personal-page-confirmation-view. It is a slim horizontal banner that spans the full width of the right column, with a height of about one to two lines of text plus 8–12px vertical padding. When visible, it uses a vivid but slightly softer brand color (e.g., soft green for success, orange for warnings, red for errors) to stand out against the card background while remaining secondary to the main CTA. The top of this banner is separated from the toggle by approximately 24px, and its bottom edge sits 16–24px above the top of the form or confirmation card, creating a clear but compact relationship. Visually, it reads like a contextual trust/feedback strip akin to the payment icons row in the original mockup: directly beneath the main action, reinforcing system feedback.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right column stack; appears and disappears without shifting the overall column anchor point, simply expanding or collapsing the vertical space between the toggle and the form/confirmation card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a personal fundraising page has been successfully created, the personal-page-confirmation-view replaces the personal-fundraising-page-form in the same right-column footprint. It is a card occupying the same width (3–4 grid columns) with similar padding (16–24px) and vertical position, starting where the form would normally begin. Inside, the content is arranged in a vertically stacked layout: a bold success heading at the top, a short, friendly message, and key page details (title, goal, cover image thumbnail) arranged with clear spacing. The fundraising-link-copy-control is visually nested near the lower half or bottom of this card, forming a final, share-oriented call to action. The confirmation card maintains at least 24–32px of whitespace around it, separating it from the creation-status-messages above and any additional tertiary information below, and it uses a lighter, celebratory color accent at the top edge or in an icon area to differentiate it from the neutral form state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the personal-fundraising-page-form, occupying the same right column card area; scrolls with the page and can participate in the column’s sticky behavior so that the confirmation and share controls stay visible while the user explores more campaign content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fundraising-link-copy-control is positioned within the lower section of the personal-page-confirmation-view card, aligned to the same right column width. It appears as a compact, pill-like row spanning roughly 80–100% of the confirmation card’s width, with the shareable URL text on the left and a prominent copy button on the right. This row sits above the bottom padding of the confirmation card by about 16–24px, with 16px of internal horizontal padding to keep the text and button comfortably spaced. Its visual treatment echoes the bright CTA style from the hero—using a bold color for the copy button—while the background of the row remains light for readability. The control is visually separated from the confirmation summary text above by 16–24px of vertical space, emphasizing its role as the next logical step after reading the success message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the confirmation card; it moves with the confirmation view as a unit, maintaining its relative position near the bottom of that card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If the campaign fails to load, the campaign-load-error-panel appears in place of the normal hero content, occupying the same full-width band directly below the global navigation. Within this band, instead of the photo and two-column layout, a centered error card spans about 40–50% of the page width and is vertically centered in a space roughly 50–60% of the viewport height. The card uses a light background with strong, high-contrast error accents and a concise message explaining the issue, with a retry button and possible navigation options stacked beneath the message. All other campaign-specific components (campaign-detail-header content, description panel, personal page form/confirmation, and related controls) are hidden or visually suppressed, ensuring that the user’s attention is entirely on resolving the error before any content appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the page flow but conditionally replaces the campaign-detail-header and subsequent content areas; it occupies the top content region until the error is resolved and normal content can render.

name: Advocacy & Write Your Representative
title: Advocacy & Write Your Representative
description: This page lets users learn about the organization’s advocacy priorities and send customized letters to their representatives using ZIP-based lookup, topic-based templates, and selectable delivery methods.

core_features: Load and display advocacy overview content and priority areas using getAdvocacyOverviewContent.
List available advocacy topics and allow topic selection using getAdvocacyTopics.
Preselect an advocacy topic based on the topicId URL parameter when present.
Provide a ZIP code lookup form, optionally prefilled from the zipCode URL parameter, and retrieve representatives using lookupRepresentativesByZip.
Display representatives returned for a ZIP code and allow users to select which recipients should receive the letter.
Fetch and display a pre-written letter template for the selected topic using getAdvocacyLetterTemplate, with an editable first-paragraph area for user custom text.
Build the final letter body by combining template text and user-edited content, tracking the custom text segment separately.
Collect required sender information fields (full name, email, street address, city, state, postal code) within a structured form.
Offer delivery method options (e.g., send email through the site, generate print-ready PDF) and bind them to the sendAdvocacyLetter parameters.
Submit the advocacy letter to selected representatives via sendAdvocacyLetter and display success or error messages from the response.
Provide a navigation link to the Advocacy FAQs page (faq.html).
user_workflows: View advocacy priorities: User opens advocate.html → Page reads URL parameters (topicId, zipCode) → Page calls getAdvocacyOverviewContent → Overview text and priority areas are displayed for reading, independent of the letter tool.

Start letter with preselected topic: User arrives with a topicId in the URL → Page calls getAdvocacyTopics → The topic whose id or topicCode matches topicId is selected in the topic selector → Page calls getAdvocacyLetterTemplate with that topicId → The corresponding letter template is loaded into the letter editor.

Start letter without preselected topic: User opens advocate.html without topicId → Page calls getAdvocacyTopics → No topic is selected by default or the page uses a neutral initial state → User selects a topic from the list → Page calls getAdvocacyLetterTemplate with the chosen topicId → Letter editor displays the template for editing.

Look up representatives by ZIP: Page reads zipCode from the URL (if present) and pre-fills the ZIP field → User enters or edits the ZIP code → User submits the ZIP lookup form → Page calls lookupRepresentativesByZip with the entered zipCode → A list of representatives is displayed and user checks or unchecks which recipients to include.

Customize the letter: Once a topic is selected and template loaded, the letter editor shows the full template with a clearly marked editable first paragraph → User types additional custom content into the editable area, meeting the minimum word guidance → Component updates both the complete bodyText to be sent and the customTextAdded segment for tracking.

Enter sender information and delivery options: User fills in sender full name, email, street address, city, state, and postal code in the sender info form → User selects one or both delivery options (send email through site, generate print-ready PDF) → Form validates that at least one delivery method is chosen and required contact fields are filled.

Submit letter and view confirmation: User reviews selected topic, representatives, letter text, delivery options, and sender info → User clicks the submit button on the main advocacy letter form → Page compiles topicId, zipCode, final bodyText, customTextAdded, delivery flags, sender information, and recipientIds → Page calls sendAdvocacyLetter → On success, confirmation state is shown with message from the API (and optional instructions if a PDF is generated); on failure, an error message is displayed and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Navigate to Advocacy FAQs: From any state on this page, user clicks the ‘Advocacy FAQs’ link → Browser navigates to faq.html without additional parameters (or with parameters if later extended) to read detailed help content.
interactions: On page load, an initialization routine reads topicId and zipCode from the current URL query string and triggers appropriate data fetches (topics list, overview content, optional ZIP-based representative lookup, and optional letter template preload).
Changing the selected advocacy topic in the topic selector triggers a URL update (changing the topicId parameter) and calls getAdvocacyLetterTemplate for the newly chosen topicId.
Submitting the ZIP lookup form triggers validation of the ZIP input and, if valid, calls lookupRepresentativesByZip, updates the representative list, and synchronizes the zipCode URL parameter.
Checking or unchecking a representative in the recipients list updates the collection of recipientIds that will be used when sendAdvocacyLetter is invoked.

Typing in the editable first paragraph of the letter editor updates both the customTextAdded value and the combined bodyText that merges template and user content; the component can show validation feedback if the custom text does not meet minimum guidance.
Typing into sender information fields updates internal form state, enabling or disabling the submit button depending on whether required fields and a delivery method selection are complete.
Toggling delivery options (email sending and PDF generation) updates boolean flags bound to sendAdvocacyLetter parameters and may trigger inline hints or validation if no delivery method is selected.

Clicking the main ‘Submit Letter’ button triggers client-side validation (required fields, at least one recipient selected, at least one delivery method chosen) and then calls sendAdvocacyLetter if validation passes, while disabling the button until the request completes.

Handling sendAdvocacyLetter responses: on a successful response, the page enters a confirmation state showing the returned message and key details; on failure, the page displays the message from the API as an error and re-enables editing and resubmission.
Clicking the ‘Advocacy FAQs’ navigation element triggers a standard link navigation to faq.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters and in-memory state to manage context without localStorage. On initial load, topicId and zipCode are read from the URL. If topicId is present, after getAdvocacyTopics returns, the topic selector component attempts to match it to an available topic by id or topicCode and sets that as the active topic; if no match is found, the selector remains in an unselected or default state. Whenever the user changes the topic in the selector, the page updates the topicId parameter in the URL (e.g., advocate.html?topicId=...&zipCode=...) using browser history APIs without reloading the page, allowing deep linking to that topic’s state. If zipCode is present in the URL on load, the ZIP field is pre-filled, and the page may automatically call lookupRepresentativesByZip with that zipCode once the user-visible components are ready, populating the representatives list. When the user updates the ZIP and submits the lookup form, the zipCode query parameter is updated to the new value to preserve context if the page is reloaded or shared. All other transient form states (representative selection, edited letter text, sender details, delivery options, and submission status) are maintained in component-level memory and are not stored in the URL or localStorage. After a successful submission, the page can optionally add a lightweight status indicator to the URL (such as a submission flag or a server-generated identifier) only if such behavior is aligned with backend handling of advocate.html, but the primary confirmation state is maintained in-memory and derived from the sendAdvocacyLetter response.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches and displays general advocacy overview text and a list of priority areas to provide context for the letter-writing tool.

Fetches available advocacy topics, displays them as a selectable control, and manages the currently selected topic, including preselection from the topicId URL parameter and triggering template loading when a topic is chosen.

Provides an input for ZIP code entry, optionally prefilled from the zipCode URL parameter, and submits the ZIP to look up representatives. Manages validation of the ZIP and controls when representative results are shown.

Displays the list of representatives returned from lookupRepresentativesByZip, allows the user to select or deselect recipients, and maintains the current list of recipientIds for the sendAdvocacyLetter call.
When a topic is selected, calls getAdvocacyLetterTemplate to retrieve the corresponding template and passes the bodyText and editableSectionsInfo to the letter editor component.

Displays the full pre-written letter template for the selected topic, highlights the portion that is editable, and lets the user add or modify text in that editable section.
