Maintains both the combined bodyText to be sent and the customTextAdded segment, and can enforce guidance such as a minimum word count for the custom section.

Allows the user to choose whether to send the letter via email through the site, generate a print-ready PDF, or both, and binds those selections to the sendEmailThroughSite and generatePrintReadyPdf parameters for sendAdvocacyLetter.

Collects required sender details (full name, email, street address, city, state, and postal code, plus optional address line 2). Validates that required fields are present and in acceptable formats before allowing submission of the advocacy letter.

Aggregates the selected topicId, ZIP code, final letter bodyText, customTextAdded, delivery option flags, sender information, and recipientIds, performs final validation, then submits the data using sendAdvocacyLetter and handles success and error responses.
Shows real-time status for the sendAdvocacyLetter operation, including loading state during submission, and displays the success or error message property from the response.
Provides a clickable link labeled according to the outgoing navigation definition that takes users to faq.html for additional information about advocacy topics and processes.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing fixed-height header, the page opens with a full-width advocacy hero section that mirrors the original donate hero: a darkened photo background with the main content constrained to a centered 12-column grid. The hero is split into a loose two-column composition: the left 55–60% houses the advocacy-overview-section as a large statement block, while the right 25–30% is a tall, narrow interactive stack containing the advocacy-topic-selector, the zip-lookup-form, and the representative-selection-list. A slim far-right strip (about 10–15% width) is reserved for the site’s social bar, visually separated from the main grid. Below this hero, the page transitions into a lighter, form-focused workspace where the letter-template-loader, letter-editor, delivery-options-selector, sender-info-form, advocacy-letter-submit, submission-status-display, and advocacy-faq-link are arranged in a two-column layout with the main drafting experience on the left and supporting details and submission controls on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing fixed-height header, the page opens with a full-width advocacy hero section that mirrors the original donate hero: a darkened photo background with the main content constrained to a centered 12-column grid. The hero is split into a loose two-column composition: the left 55–60% houses the advocacy-overview-section as a large statement block, while the right 25–30% is a tall, narrow interactive stack containing the advocacy-topic-selector, the zip-lookup-form, and the representative-selection-list. A slim far-right strip (about 10–15% width) is reserved for the site’s social bar, visually separated from the main grid. Below this hero, the page transitions into a lighter, form-focused workspace where the letter-template-loader, letter-editor, delivery-options-selector, sender-info-form, advocacy-letter-submit, submission-status-display, and advocacy-faq-link are arranged in a two-column layout with the main drafting experience on the left and supporting details and submission controls on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes roughly 10–15% of the viewport height with a white background, followed by a hero that fills the remaining 85–90% of the first screen. Inside that hero, the left advocacy overview area uses around 60% of the grid width and vertically hugs the central band of the hero, leaving generous top and bottom padding. The right interactive stack uses roughly 30% of the grid width, aligned to the right edge of the content container with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px between stacked elements. Below the hero, the main form area is constrained to the same max content width; the left form column occupies about two-thirds of this width and contains the letter-template-loader, letter-editor, and delivery-options-selector in a vertical sequence with 32–48px gaps between them, while the right column occupies one-third and stacks the sender-info-form, advocacy-letter-submit, and submission-status-display with slightly tighter vertical spacing. Section breaks between the hero and the lower form area follow the 48–64px vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero follows a moderate density: a bold headline and short copy on the left and a compact but legible form stack on the right, each wrapped in generous padding and sitting against a dark overlay to keep the busy photo background subdued. Each form element in the right hero column is encapsulated in card-like containers or clear field groups with ample breathing room. Below the hero, density increases slightly but stays organized through clear grouping: the letter-editor is large and central with significant padding, while delivery-options-selector and sender-info-form are compressed but still comfortably spaced. Supporting elements like the advocacy-faq-link and submission-status-display are visually lighter, using smaller type and minimal chrome to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the original F/Z pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top-left brand logo in the global header, travels horizontally across the nav to any right-aligned advocacy or donate CTA, then drops diagonally into the hero onto the large advocacy-overview-section headline on the left. From that headline, attention shifts right to the bright, button-like controls at the top of the hero’s form stack (advocacy-topic-selector and zip-lookup-form). After interacting there, the eye moves down within the right stack to the representative-selection-list. As the user scrolls, the flow continues from the top of the left column (letter-template-loader and the letter-editor title area), down through the body of the letter, then across to the right column to review sender-info-form, choose delivery-options-selector, and finally land on the advocacy-letter-submit button. Post-submission, the submission-status-display appears immediately near the submit control so the user’s gaze doesn’t have to travel far. The advocacy-faq-link sits at a secondary focal point near the bottom or side of the main interaction area, discovered after the primary tasks are understood.

layout.overall_description: The page feels like a focused advocacy workspace built on top of the same bold hero structure as the donation homepage. A fixed-height white header with centered navigation anchors the top, while a full-width hero image of families or volunteers is darkened with an overlay. Within this hero, a wide left block carries a large, playful but serious advocacy message, using the brand’s bright colors on top of the dark image, echoing the original ‘No Child Hungry’ treatment but now framed around speaking up and writing representatives. On the right, a tall, card-like panel floats over the photo, starting with a vivid, pill-shaped topic selector and a compact ZIP-lookup form, followed by a concise list of matching representatives. The far-right edge holds a slim vertical social bar running the height of the hero. As the user scrolls, the background transitions to a clean, light surface and the interface becomes more utilitarian: the left side of the page turns into a large, comfortable writing area with a template loader status line and a generous text editor, while the right side stacks the user’s contact information, delivery method options, and a strong submit CTA. Spacing follows an 8px-based rhythm, with generous section breaks and card padding that keep the multi-step task from feeling overwhelming. The net effect is a visually engaging but highly structured advocacy flow that strongly echoes the original site’s hero layout and visual rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advocacy-overview-section is positioned in the upper-left portion of the hero area, occupying roughly 55–60% of the content width and centered vertically within the hero’s height band (leaving about one quarter of the hero height as padding above and below). It aligns with the same content max-width as the header, creating a straight left edge with the logo. Inside this block, a large headline and 1–2 lines of supporting copy are left-aligned and stacked, with 16–24px spacing between lines and 32–48px of padding from the hero’s top edge and from the right edge where the right-hand form stack begins. Visually, it overlaps the darkened photo background without a card border, relying on the dark overlay plus bright brand colors for contrast. To maintain the moderate content density, there are no dense lists here—just a bold statement and a short description, with ample negative space around them that creates a clear focal anchor before the eye travels right to the interactive stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; maintains its left alignment and approximate width ratio on desktop, while vertically centering within the hero area until the page scrolls past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advocacy-topic-selector sits at the top of the right-hand hero stack, aligned to the right edge of the main content container and occupying about 25–30% of the total page width. It appears as a bright, pill-like horizontal control or dropdown bar, roughly the height of a standard button plus 8–12px, with 16–24px horizontal padding inside. There is 32–48px of horizontal space separating it from the left advocacy-overview-section. Vertically, it aligns roughly with the center of the overview headline block so that when the user scans from left to right, their eye lands naturally on it. Below the selector, a 16–24px gap separates it from the zip-lookup-form. The control is visually dominant within this column, using the brand’s vivid yellow or lime background to echo the mockup’s PayPal donate button and to signal it as the starting point of the interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-fixed but can become sticky within its column on taller viewports so that, as the user scrolls through the hero or slightly below it, the selector remains visible at the top of the right column until the hero area is fully passed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zip-lookup-form is placed directly under the advocacy-topic-selector within the same right-hand hero stack, maintaining the same width and right alignment. It appears as a compact, card-like form row with a text input for ZIP code and a clear action button (e.g., ‘Find My Reps’) aligned horizontally, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and a subtle border or soft shadow to distinguish it from the dark background. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the topic selector above and the representative-selection-list below. Together with the topic selector, it visually forms a two-step starting pillar: first choose a topic, then enter location. This mirrors the way the donate button and payment icons formed a vertical action stack in the original hero, but here expressed as stacked form controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right hero stack; it scrolls away with the hero but may be included in a sticky behavior along with the topic selector on larger screens, keeping the starting controls visible as long as the hero is in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The representative-selection-list sits immediately below the zip-lookup-form, still within the narrow right-hand hero column, sharing its width and right alignment. It is presented as a vertical list of representative cards, each card spanning the full width of this column with 12–16px internal padding and 8–12px vertical spacing between cards. The list area’s height is constrained to roughly one third to one half of the hero height; if there are more items, the list becomes scrollable within this panel to preserve the hero’s vertical balance. Each card uses a light background overlay or bordered style to stand out against the darker hero; name and title are left-aligned within each card, with a small checkbox or toggle on the right. A 16–24px gap separates this list from the bottom of the hero or from any tertiary elements like the advocacy-faq-link if it’s docked nearby. The list visually completes the right-hand vertical stack, echoing the trust badges placement under the payment icons in the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained scroll region within the hero on desktop; it scrolls within its own panel if the number of representatives exceeds the vertical space, while the rest of the hero content remains static until the entire hero scrolls out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The letter-template-loader appears just below the hero, at the top of the main content area in the left column, aligning with the left edge of the content grid and spanning roughly two-thirds of the page width. It presents as a slender horizontal status row or slim card that sits directly above the letter-editor, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, communicating loading or template-chosen status. There is 48–64px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero and this loader to clearly mark the transition from hero to workspace. The loader is visually understated but clearly connected to the letter-editor beneath it, perhaps with a subtle colored left border or icon that matches the editor’s header to tie them as a unit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the left form column; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page, without sticky behavior, serving as an entry status cue rather than a persistent control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The letter-editor occupies the main body of the left column, directly beneath the letter-template-loader and spanning about two-thirds of the page width. It is the largest single component on the page, styled as a light card or outlined panel with 24–32px internal padding and a height that comfortably fills much of the first scrollable viewport below the hero (for example, 50–70% of the visible area on a typical desktop), with its bottom extending beyond the fold as content grows. A short editor title and brief instruction line sit at the top, followed by a large text area that expands vertically as the letter grows. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the loader and the editor’s top edge, and 32–48px between the bottom of the editor and the delivery-options-selector below it. The editor’s left edge aligns with the advocacy-overview-section’s left edge above, creating a strong vertical alignment that continues the visual flow from hero message to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; scrolls naturally with the page. The text area itself can have its own internal scroll if extremely long, but the preferred behavior is auto-expansion up to a reasonable height before internal scrolling is introduced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-options-selector is placed directly below the letter-editor within the same left column, maintaining the two-thirds width and left alignment. It appears as a compact card-like section with 16–24px internal padding, containing a short title and 2–3 horizontally or vertically arranged options (e.g., email, printed letter, in-platform message) each with icon and label. A 32–48px vertical gap separates it from the bottom of the letter-editor, and 24–32px separates it from the content that follows vertically (if any additional left-column content exists). Its height is modest, roughly one quarter of the letter-editor’s initial height, to ensure the main focus remains on writing while still keeping the delivery choice close to the context of the letter itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; no special sticky or floating behavior. It scrolls with the letter-editor as part of the continuous drafting flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sender-info-form is positioned at the top of the right-hand column of the lower form area, aligned to the right edge of the content grid and spanning roughly one-third of the page width. It starts at approximately the same vertical level as the top of the letter-editor, creating a two-column balance below the letter-template-loader. Styled as a tidy card with 24px internal padding, it contains vertically stacked input fields (name, email, address) with 8–12px gaps between fields and 16–24px between field groups. A 32–48px horizontal gap separates it from the left column. Vertically, there is 24–32px of space between the bottom of this form and the advocacy-letter-submit component below it. The size of this form is sufficient to hold all necessary fields without feeling cramped, but visually secondary to the large letter-editor to preserve the writing experience as the focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the main content. On taller screens, it appears fully visible alongside the top portion of the letter-editor; on shorter screens, it may require minimal scrolling but remains grouped immediately above the submit controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advocacy-letter-submit component is placed directly under the sender-info-form in the right column, maintaining the same width and alignment. It appears as a prominent CTA section: a full-width button or pair of buttons within a shallow card or bar, with 16–24px top and bottom padding and 24px side padding. The primary submit button uses the brand’s bold yellow or orange, echoing the bright donate CTA from the hero and standing out clearly against the lighter background. A 24–32px vertical gap separates this component from the sender-info-form above, and a smaller 16–24px gap separates it from the submission-status-display below. As the user scrolls, the bottom edge of this component tends to stay near the lower third of the viewport on desktop, ensuring the primary action remains easy to access after the user has filled in their information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky footer within the right column on desktop: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it can remain pinned within the viewport’s lower edge of the right column until the end of the form content, mimicking the prominence of the hero’s main CTA. On smaller screens, it behaves as a normal block-level element to avoid obscuring content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-display sits immediately beneath the advocacy-letter-submit component in the right column, using the same width and right alignment but with a lower visual weight. It presents as a slim status bar or small card with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, showing success, error, or progress messages. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the submit control and this display, so it reads clearly as feedback tied directly to the action above. When inactive, this area may be hidden or appear as a very subtle placeholder line; when active, it can briefly use bright accent colors (green for success, red for error) that still respect the overall clean aesthetic. The component’s height is modest so as not to push the primary CTA too far up, keeping the action and feedback tightly coupled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically in place beneath the submit component; it does not have its own sticky behavior but moves with the advocacy-letter-submit when that component is in its sticky state, ensuring feedback is always adjacent to the action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advocacy-faq-link is treated as a tertiary navigation element positioned near, but not competing with, the core interaction. On desktop, it sits at the bottom-left of the right-hand hero stack, just beneath the representative-selection-list, aligned with the left edge of that column and inset from the hero’s bottom by 16–24px. It appears as a simple text link or small-pill link with an icon, using a brand accent color but smaller typography than primary CTAs. Alternatively, on longer pages where a hero-level link is insufficient, a second instance can appear under the main form area, flush left beneath the delivery-options-selector or at the bottom of the right column beneath the submission-status-display, with 32–48px top margin to separate it clearly. In both positions, it occupies minimal vertical space and about one-third of the page width or less, acting as a lightweight escape hatch for users who want more information without distracting from the main advocacy flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky text or pill-style link; in the hero it scrolls away with the hero, and in the lower area it scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: The About Us page loads organizational background content from the backend and lets users navigate between mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, and financial transparency sections, with a clear path to donate.

core_features: Fetch and display About Us content (mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, financial highlights) via the getAboutUsContent interface on page load.
Provide in-page navigation to jump between content sections (mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, financial transparency).
Allow deep-linking and direct access to a specific section using URL parameters (e.g., ?section=leadership).
Show leadership information as a structured list of people with expandable bios based on the leadership array from getAboutUsContent.
List partners with descriptions using the partners array from getAboutUsContent.
Display financial transparency highlights using the financialHighlights array from getAboutUsContent, emphasizing organizational credibility.
Provide a clearly labeled 'Donate now' call-to-action button that navigates to donate.html from relevant sections (e.g., alongside mission/financial information).
user_workflows: View overall About Us content: 1) User opens about.html. 2) The page calls getAboutUsContent. 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) Once loaded, mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, and financial highlights sections are rendered. 5) User scrolls through the page to read details.

Jump to a specific section from the URL: 1) User opens about.html?section=financial. 2) The page reads the section parameter and sets the active section to 'financial'. 3) After getAboutUsContent resolves, the page scrolls/focuses the financial section and highlights that item in the section navigation. 4) User may then use navigation to move to other sections, updating the section parameter in the URL.

Navigate between sections using in-page navigation: 1) User is on about.html and sees a section navigation control. 2) User clicks a navigation item (e.g., Leadership). 3) The active section state updates and the URL parameter section=leadership is set (using pushState/replaceState). 4) The page scrolls/focuses the leadership section. 5) If the user reloads, the same section remains active using the section parameter.

Explore leadership details: 1) User navigates or scrolls to the leadership section. 2) The leadership-list component renders items from the leadership array returned by getAboutUsContent. 3) The user clicks an individual leader entry to expand or collapse the bio text. 4) The expanded/collapsed state is managed client-side without additional API calls.

Review partners: 1) User opens the partners section via navigation or scrolling. 2) The partners-list component displays each partner name and description from the partners array in getAboutUsContent. 3) If the list is long, the user can expand/collapse the list using a 'show more/show less' control, which only changes local view state.

Check financial transparency and proceed to donate: 1) User opens or navigates to the financial transparency section. 2) The financial-highlights-panel displays key financialHighlights items from getAboutUsContent. 3) The user clicks the 'Donate now' CTA button within or near this section. 4) The browser navigates to donate.html. 5) No additional API calls are made on the About Us page as part of this navigation.
interactions: On initial page load, the page automatically calls getAboutUsContent with no parameters and transitions from a loading state to a content state, or to an error state if the call fails.
If getAboutUsContent fails, the user sees an error message with a 'Try again' button; clicking this button triggers a retry of getAboutUsContent.

Clicking items in the section navigation (e.g., Mission, History, Approach, Leadership, Partners, Financials) updates the active section, adjusts the URL section parameter, and scrolls/focuses the corresponding section.
Manually changing the section parameter in the URL (via direct link or browser navigation) updates the active section state and scrolls the page to that section after the content is loaded.
Clicking on a leadership item toggles the expanded/collapsed state of that leader’s bio text, allowing users to read more or less detail without leaving the page.

If a partners list is long, clicking a 'show more' control reveals additional partner entries and toggles to 'show less' to collapse back to a smaller set; this interaction only affects visible items, not the underlying data.
Clicking the 'Donate now' button triggers a standard navigation to donate.html using a normal anchor/link behavior.

Standard browser back/forward navigation updates the section parameter-based state (e.g., moving back from section=financial to section=mission updates the active section and scroll position accordingly).
page_functionality.state_logic: The About Us page uses URL parameters to control section-level state. A single query parameter section is read on page initialization and on URL changes. Allowed values (used internally) correspond to content sections derivable from the getAboutUsContent payload, such as 'mission', 'history', 'approach', 'leadership', 'partners', and 'financial'. If section is missing or not recognized, a default section (e.g., mission) is activated. When a user clicks a section navigation item, the page updates the section parameter using the History API (pushState or replaceState) without reloading the page, then scrolls and focuses the selected section. The about-section-router component ensures that changes in the section parameter (including browser back/forward and deep-linked URLs) always drive which section is considered active, and other components use this activeSection value to handle highlighting and focus. No other URL parameters are required by this page, and navigation to donate.html does not append or depend on any query parameters. All other UI states (like which bios are expanded, or whether a long list is expanded) are maintained in memory only and are not stored in the URL or persistent storage.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component calls the getAboutUsContent interface, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, and financialHighlights data to child components.

Reads and interprets the section URL parameter to determine the currently active content section (e.g., mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, financial) and exposes this as activeSection. Updates the URL when the active section changes due to user interaction and listens for browser navigation events to keep the UI in sync with the URL.

Provides an in-page navigation menu for the About Us sections. It highlights the active section based on activeSection from about-section-router and, when a nav item is clicked, notifies the router to update activeSection and the section URL parameter, then scrolls/focuses the corresponding section element.

Displays the organization's mission text from getAboutUsContent.mission. It renders when mission is available and uses the activeSection value to manage focus or emphasize this section when selected by navigation or via URL parameter.

Shows the organization's history using the history string from getAboutUsContent. It is scroll-targetable and responds to activeSection changes so that when the history section is selected, it is brought into view and can receive focus for accessibility.

Presents the organization's approach to ending child hunger using the approach content from getAboutUsContent. It is a dedicated scroll target and integrates with the section navigation and routing logic for activation and focus.

Renders the leadership array from getAboutUsContent as a list of people, each with a name, title, and an expandable/collapsible bio. It supports long bios by defaulting to a collapsed state and allowing users to expand individual entries to read more.

Displays partners from getAboutUsContent.partners as a structured list of organizations with their descriptions. If the list is long, it can show an initial subset with a control to expand or collapse the full list.

Shows high-level financial transparency information using the financialHighlights array from getAboutUsContent (label and value pairs). It may support grouping or simple expansion if many highlights exist and ensures these details are emphasized when the financial section is active.
Provides a prominent 'Donate now' button that navigates users from the About Us page to donate.html, typically displayed near mission or financial information to convert trust into action.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s top-to-bottom, two-column hero pattern in the main About layout: a constrained central content band below the global header, with a dominant left column for the currently active About section (mission, history, etc.) and a narrower right column for a vertical stack of high-intent actions and trust elements (donate CTA, section navigation, financial highlights). A slim, fixed vertical social bar remains on the far right edge of the viewport, visually independent of the main grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s top-to-bottom, two-column hero pattern in the main About layout: a constrained central content band below the global header, with a dominant left column for the currently active About section (mission, history, etc.) and a narrower right column for a vertical stack of high-intent actions and trust elements (donate CTA, section navigation, financial highlights). A slim, fixed vertical social bar remains on the far right edge of the viewport, visually independent of the main grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a constrained content width (roughly 1100–1200px) centered on the page. Below the header/hero, the About content area uses ~60–65% of the width for the main text/content column and ~25–30% for the right-side action/trust column, with the remaining ~10–15% visually reserved for the vertical social strip and outer gutters. Vertical spacing follows the base 8px scale: ~48–64px between the hero and the About content area, and ~24–32px between stacked elements (nav, CTA, panels) inside each column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density moderate: each About section shows focused, scannable copy with headings and short paragraphs in the left column, while the right column hosts a few, clearly separated interactive and summary elements. Generous padding inside cards and buttons and consistent section spacing prevent the multi-section content (mission, history, partners, etc.) from feeling crowded. Only one major content section is visible at a time, controlled by the section router, so the page never shows all sections simultaneously.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z scanning pattern: the eye starts at the top-left site logo in the global header, moves across to the top-right primary DONATE button, then drops into the About area. Within the About band, the gaze first hits the right-column donate CTA (bright yellow, near the top), then sweeps diagonally left to the large About heading and active section content. From there, it moves right again to the section navigation and financial highlights in the right column, then finally explores the far-right vertical social bar. This arrangement keeps the path to donate and key trust indicators highly visible while still centering the organizational story.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar white global header and hero treatment, the About Us page opens into a wide but visually contained content band that echoes the mockup’s hero composition. Centered on the viewport, a 12-column grid divides the space into a dominant left content column for the active About section and a narrower right column for actions and trust elements. The left side feels editorial: a bold “About Us” heading followed by whichever section is active—Mission, History, Approach, Leadership, Partners, or Financial Transparency—rendered as clean, left-aligned text blocks with generous line spacing and 24–32px separation between subsections. On the right, a tall, card-like stack rises: at the top, a bright, pill-like DONATE CTA in brand yellow, directly beneath it the About section navigation rendered as playful yet tidy pill tabs or list items, and below that a compact financial highlights panel summarizing key numbers as trust badges. The entire stack floats over a soft, lightly tinted background panel that subtly separates it from the white main content area, mirroring the mockup’s right-hand donate stack over a dark overlay. Along the far-right edge of the screen, outside the constrained content width, a vertical yellow strip remains pinned with social icons in a single column, echoing the original hero’s social bar. Overall, the layout feels like a grounded, informational counterpart to the homepage hero: same structure, same emphasis on donation and trust, but with the spotlight turned toward deeper storytelling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader sits as a non-visual, full-width logical layer behind the visible About Us content band. It conceptually spans the same central width as the 12-column grid but does not occupy any distinct visual area or change spacing. It initializes and feeds data into the visible content sections (mission, history, approach, leadership, partners, financial transparency) so that when the user chooses a section from the About navigation on the right, the left-column content updates without the page layout shifting.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, background process; it aligns with the page’s main content area but does not render any visible UI or affect spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-router also operates as an invisible controller layered in the same main content zone, directly managing which of the About sections is visible in the left column at any given time. It treats the mission-section, history-section, approach-section, leadership-list, partners-list, and any financial transparency content as overlapping views in the same rectangular area—approximately 60–65% of the page width, centered under the hero and aligned to the left of the right-side action column. When a user clicks a navigation item in the About section navigation, the router swaps the content within this left-column panel without altering the surrounding layout, ensuring smooth transitions and consistent vertical positioning of headings and text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible state controller, conceptually covering the entire left content column and toggling which section component is mounted within that shared space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-nav appears in the upper portion of the right-hand column, just below the prominent About donate CTA, occupying roughly 80–100% of that column’s width (about 25–30% of the overall content width). It is rendered as a vertically stacked set of pill-like tabs or compact cards labeled Mission, History, Our Approach, Leadership, Partners, and Financials, each with ample clickable padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). The nav stack sits inside a subtly elevated, light-tinted panel that visually groups it with the donate button and the financial highlights panel below, with about 16–24px spacing between each nav item and 24px separation from the donate CTA above and the financial highlights below. The nav aligns to the right edge of the main content column grid while staying 24–32px away from the far-right social bar, creating a clear vertical spine of interactive elements that users can quickly scan down to jump between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page but can optionally become sticky within the right column once the user scrolls past the initial hero, staying pinned near the top of the action column to keep section navigation visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section occupies the main left content column directly under the About page title, spanning roughly 60–65% of the central page width (around 7–8 of 12 columns), aligned to the left with a comfortable 32–48px margin from the hero above. Within this rectangle, the mission text is arranged in a vertical flow: a bold section heading, a short lead paragraph, and one or two supporting paragraphs or bullet blocks, each separated by 16–24px. The right edge of the mission-section aligns with the inner edge of the right column that holds the donate CTA and About section nav, leaving a clear vertical gutter of about one grid column between the two. Only one major section (mission or another) is visible in this left-area at a time, so when Mission is active, it visually dominates the page’s center, drawing the eye diagonally from the upper-right donate CTA across to the mission heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mounted within the shared left-column content area controlled by the section router; it scrolls naturally with the page and is replaced by other sections when the user navigates.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section shares the exact same physical footprint as the mission-section: the main left column beneath the hero, spanning about 60–65% of the content width, aligned so that its left edge matches the mission-section’s left edge and its right edge aligns with the gutter before the right column. When active, it replaces the mission-section within this space, presenting a chronological story using stacked text blocks, subheadings, and possibly a simple vertical timeline, all separated by 16–24px to keep density moderate. The top of the history-section content begins at roughly the same vertical position as the mission-section heading would, preserving a consistent top baseline so the layout does not jump when switching sections via the About section nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mounted in the same left-column area managed by the section router; scrolls in sync with the page content and swaps with other sections based on navigation state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The approach-section also lives in the shared left content column, occupying the same rectangle (about two-thirds of the constrained width) just beneath the About title. When active, it is composed as a series of short, structured blocks—e.g., pillars or steps of the organization’s approach—stacked vertically with 24–32px gaps between blocks so the content feels modular and easy to scan. Each block aligns to the left margin of this column and stops short of the right gutter before the action/trust column, preserving the visual separation between explanatory content on the left and actions/trust on the right. Its heading appears at the top of this column, at the same approximate vertical position as the other section headings, maintaining a consistent visual rhythm across section changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same scrollable, router-controlled left-column area; it is swapped in when the user selects the Approach item in the About section nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The leadership-list appears in the same left-column section area when Leadership is selected, spanning roughly 60–65% of the page width and aligned with all other sections. Within this space, leaders are displayed as a vertical list or a loose grid of cards (2 columns max) depending on viewport width, each card having 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px spacing between cards, echoing the trust badges in the original mockup. Portraits, names, and titles are left-aligned within each card, but the overall list maintains a clear right margin before the action column. The first row of leadership cards begins directly below a Leadership heading, which is positioned in line with where other section titles appear, preserving consistent vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mounted into the left-column area via the section router; the list scrolls as a longer content block if there are many leaders, pushing the right-column stack to scroll alongside it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The partners-list also takes over the left column when the Partners section is active, using the same width and alignment as the other sections. Partners are presented as a tidy vertical list or small logo grid, with each logo or name row given 16–24px vertical spacing and enough horizontal breathing room so the layout doesn’t compete with the right column. A Partners heading sits at the top of this area, and the content flows downward, stopping well before the right edge gutter to keep the action/trust column visually distinct. The partner logos or names are sized and spaced similarly to the payment/trust icons in the mockup’s hero, reinforcing a sense of credibility without overwhelming the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears within the left-column content area under control of the section router; scrolls vertically with the main content, with the right-column stack remaining aligned alongside it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financial-highlights-panel is positioned in the lower portion of the right-hand column, directly beneath the About section nav with about 24–32px vertical spacing. It takes up nearly the full width of the right column (around 25–30% of the page’s content width) and is styled as a compact, high-contrast card with 16–24px internal padding, mirroring the trust badges/cards beneath the donate button in the mockup. Inside, one to three key financial metrics (e.g., percentage of funds to programs, annual impact, or rating badges) are arranged either as a vertical stack or a small two-column grid, each separated by 8–16px so they feel distinct yet cohesive. The panel’s top aligns visually with the bottom of the nav stack, helping to complete the vertical right-column composition that starts with DONATE, then nav, then financial trust, leading the eye downward before it reaches the page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the right column; if the right column is made sticky within the viewport, the financial-highlights-panel remains visible below the nav and donate CTA until the user scrolls past the About content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-donate-cta is the topmost and most visually striking element in the right-hand column, placed roughly level with or just below the main About heading in the left column. It occupies about 70–90% of the right column’s width, centered or slightly left-aligned within that column, and is rendered as a bright yellow, pill-like button or short horizontal bar with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. The button sits inside the same light-tinted background block that contains the section nav and financial highlights, with about 16–24px padding around the entire stack, clearly separating this vertical action column from the left content column. Above it is a modest gap from the hero (around 32–48px), and below it is a 24px gap before the About section nav begins, echoing the donate-then-trust stack from the original hero. Its placement at the upper-right of the main content region ensures it is hit early in the user’s F/Z scan, before attention drops diagonally into the main About copy on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Optionally sticky within the right column so that as the user scrolls through lengthy About content in the left column, the donate CTA remains pinned near the top of the viewport, maintaining a persistent, high-visibility call to action.

name: Contact Us
title: Contact Us
description: Page where visitors can view contact information for the organization and submit a general inquiry using a contact form, with optional routing to FAQs.

core_features: Retrieve and display contact details (mailing address, phone, general email, additional contact emails) using the getContactPageInfo interface.
Provide a general contact form with fields for full name, email, subject, and message, and submit it using the submitContactForm interface.
Show inline feedback (success or error) based on the response from submitContactForm.
Offer navigation to the FAQ page via a link whose label text is provided by getContactPageInfo.
Support URL parameter–based pre-filling of the contact form fields (e.g., subject, message) to streamline inquiries from other parts of the site.
user_workflows: View contact information workflow: 1) User navigates to contact.html (via header, footer, or internal link). 2) On page load, the page controller calls getContactPageInfo with no parameters. 3) The contact-info component receives the response object and renders mailingAddress, phoneNumber, generalEmail, and any additionalContacts entries. 4) The FAQ link text is also populated from faqLinkText in the same response.

Submit a general inquiry workflow: 1) User navigates to contact.html, optionally with URL parameters that pre-fill subject and/or message. 2) User enters full name, email, subject, and message into the contact form. 3) On form submit, client-side validation checks that required fields are not empty and that the email has a valid format. 4) If validation passes, the page calls submitContactForm with the current field values (fullName, email, subject, message). 5) When a response is returned, the form-status component shows a success state if success is true, or an error state if success is false, using the message field from the response. 6) If configured by the application, the form may clear fields or keep them populated based on the response.

Navigate to FAQs from Contact page workflow: 1) User views the contact page with contact details and the contact form visible. 2) Next to or near the contact form, the FAQ link label is rendered using faqLinkText from getContactPageInfo. 3) User clicks the FAQ link. 4) Browser navigates to faq.html (no additional parameters required; additional parameters may be appended by the broader application if needed).

Deep-linked subject pre-fill workflow: 1) User clicks a "Contact Us" link elsewhere on the site that points to contact.html with query parameters, for example contact.html?subject=<encoded-subject>&message=<encoded-message>. 2) On page load, the contact-form component reads the URL parameters and pre-populates the subject and message fields. 3) User reviews or edits the pre-filled values and submits the form, which then calls submitContactForm with those (possibly modified) values. 4) Feedback is shown as in the main submission workflow.

Post-submit URL state workflow (optional): 1) After a successful submission, the page logic may update the URL (e.g., adding submitted=1) via history APIs without reloading. 2) If the page is reloaded with submitted=1 present, the initial page controller reads this parameter and instructs the form-status component to show a passive confirmation state while leaving form fields cleared or pre-filled according to the application’s rules. 3) This allows users returning to the URL to see that a recent submission was recorded without re-submitting the form.
interactions: On page load: The page controller invokes getContactPageInfo with no parameters. The response populates the contact-info component fields (mailingAddress, phoneNumber, generalEmail, additionalContacts) and the FAQ link label (faqLinkText).
Click on FAQ link: When the user clicks the FAQ link, the browser navigates to faq.html. This interaction uses standard anchor navigation and does not call any interface.

Typing into form fields: As the user types into full name, email, subject, and message fields, the contact-form component updates its internal state. URL parameters are not changed automatically; they are only read on initial load.

Form validation on submit: On submit event of the contact form, the contact-form component prevents default browser form submission, validates required fields (fullName, email, subject, message), and checks email format. If validation fails, the form-status component displays inline error messages without calling submitContactForm.

Call to submitContactForm: If validation passes, the contact-form component calls submitContactForm with the four parameters fullName, email, subject, message derived from current field values. While awaiting a response, form controls may be disabled and a loading indicator may be shown by the form-status component.

Handle submitContactForm response: When submitContactForm resolves, the form-status component reads success and message from the response. If success is true, it renders a success confirmation and optionally instructs the form to clear or preserve fields. If success is false, it renders an error message. No additional interfaces are called.

URL parameter handling for initial state: On first render, the page controller parses URL parameters. Recognized parameters (for example subject and message, and optional submitted) are passed as initial props to the contact-form and form-status components. The form pre-fills its subject and message fields if values are present; the form-status component shows a passive status if submitted indicates a prior successful submission.

Retry after failure: After a failed submitContactForm call (success is false), the user may adjust form fields and press submit again. Each subsequent submit repeats the same validation and interface call flow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters to control initial form state and feedback behavior, without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence. On initial load of contact.html, the page controller reads the current URL’s query string. If a `subject` parameter is present, its decoded value is used as the initial value of the contact form’s subject field. If a `message` parameter is present, its decoded value is used as the initial value of the contact form’s message field. Other required fields (fullName and email) start empty unless the broader application passes them via URL parameters in a similar manner. Optionally, a `submitted` parameter (e.g., `submitted=1`) can be used to display a passive confirmation state on load: the form-status component interprets this parameter and shows that a message was recently sent, without re-calling submitContactForm. None of these URL parameters trigger additional interface calls beyond the single getContactPageInfo call on page load and the explicit submitContactForm call on form submission. URL parameters are only read (not written) by default; if the application updates them after submission, it should do so via standard history APIs, and the page will respond accordingly on subsequent loads.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and URL-based state for the Contact Us page. On load, it calls getContactPageInfo to fetch contact details and FAQ link text, parses URL parameters for subject, message, and optional submitted flags, and passes this data as props to child components. It does not render UI itself but orchestrates data flow between contact-info, contact-form, faq-link, and form-status components.

Displays core contact information for the organization using data from getContactPageInfo. It shows the mailing address, phone number, general email, and a list of additional contacts where each item includes a label and an email. Provides no editing; it is read-only and purely informational.

Provides a general inquiry form with fields for full name, email, subject, and message. Initializes subject and message from URL parameters if present. Performs client-side validation on submit, and if valid, calls submitContactForm. It forwards the response to the form-status component and may clear or retain fields depending on the outcome and page configuration.

Displays real-time status messages related to the contact form. This includes validation errors before a submitContactForm call, a loading state while the call is in progress, and success or error states once the response is received. It reads the success and message fields from the submitContactForm response, and optionally reads a submitted URL parameter on load to show a passive confirmation if the page was loaded after a redirect.

Renders a navigational link to the FAQ page. The link’s text is taken from faqLinkText in the getContactPageInfo response, ensuring it remains consistent with backend configuration. Clicking the link navigates to faq.html as defined in the outgoing navigation specification.

Displays the list of additional contacts (such as specific departments or program contacts) returned by getContactPageInfo.additionalContacts. Each item shows a label and an associated email address, which may be rendered as mailto links. This provides users with direct contact options beyond the general email and form.

Parses URL query parameters relevant to the Contact Us page and exposes them as a simple state object for other components. It extracts values such as subject, message, and submitted from the URL on initial load and when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation). It does not modify the URL itself.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed-height header at the top, then a full-width hero-style Contact section that fills the rest of the initial viewport. Within this hero, use a two-column composition: left column for the page title and core contact information (replacing the large logotype), right column for the main interactive element—the contact form—stacked with its status banner and FAQ link. The far-right edge of the viewport still accommodates the global vertical social bar, sitting outside the main 12-column content grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed-height header at the top, then a full-width hero-style Contact section that fills the rest of the initial viewport. Within this hero, use a two-column composition: left column for the page title and core contact information (replacing the large logotype), right column for the main interactive element—the contact form—stacked with its status banner and FAQ link. The far-right edge of the viewport still accommodates the global vertical social bar, sitting outside the main 12-column content grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header keeps roughly 10–15% of the viewport height with a white background, while the Contact hero takes the remaining 85–90%, using a darkened photo background. Inside the hero, allocate about 55–60% of the content width to the left column (contact-info-panel plus additional-contact-list) and 30–35% to the right column (contact-form stack). Leave the final 10–15% of the total page width visually clear for the existing fixed social bar. Maintain generous internal padding for the form card and contact cards (16–24px) and use 48–64px vertical spacing between major stacked groups.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the density moderate and breathable, mirroring the hero in the mockup: a few highly relevant elements (title, key contact details, form, optional extra contacts) instead of long copy. Use the dark overlay on the background photo to push the text and form card forward, with clear separation via padding and 24–32px gaps between subcomponents (e.g., between contact-info-panel and additional-contact-list, or between form and FAQ link). Avoid overly long lists; break secondary info into compact rows or small cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the same F/Z pattern: the user’s gaze starts at the global brand logo in the header (top-left), moves horizontally across the navigation to the bright header CTA (e.g., Donate) on the top-right, then drops diagonally into the hero’s left column where a large, bold 'Contact Us' heading and a few key contact cues sit. From there, the eye is pulled right toward the bright, pill-like submit button within the contact-form card. After noticing the form fields and CTA, the gaze flows downward to the FAQ link and any form status message, then back to the left for the additional-contact-list as a secondary support. Finally, the far-right vertical social bar is discovered as a tertiary path.

layout.overall_description: Below the clean, white header, the Contact Us page opens into a full-height hero section with a darkened photo background of volunteers, echoing the home hero’s mood. Centered within a constrained content width, a 12-column layout divides the hero into a broader left column and a narrower right column. On the left, a large, multi-line 'Contact Us' heading and succinct contact summary sit in front of the image, styled with the same bold, kid-forward typography as the brand logotype in the mockup. Beneath this, compact rows list primary contact channels as small, lightly elevated cards. On the right, a bright, white contact-form panel floats over the dark background, aligned vertically to feel level with the left heading. Inside this panel, neatly stacked fields lead to a vivid, brand-colored submit button that echoes the original yellow Donate CTA. A slim form-status banner appears just above the form when needed, and a low-emphasis FAQ link sits directly below the submit button, offering a secondary path. The global social strip remains pinned along the far-right of the viewport in a yellow bar, keeping the familiar vertical icon stack. The result is a hero-like Contact section where the left side reassures and informs, while the right side invites immediate interaction, all framed by the playful yet modern nonprofit aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller acts as the main hero wrapper for the Contact Us content, sitting directly below the global header. It spans the full viewport width, with a background photo covered by a dark overlay that fills approximately 85–90% of the initial viewport height. Within this wrapper, the content is centered in a fixed-width container (roughly 70–80% of the page width), aligned using an implicit 12-column grid. The controller divides this inner content into two columns: the left column occupies about 7 of 12 columns (around 55–60% of the container width) for the contact-info-panel and additional-contact-list, while the right column uses about 4 of 12 columns (around 30–35%) for the contact-form stack, leaving about 1 column (10–15% of total viewport width) visually clear at the far right for the existing social bar. Vertical alignment keeps both columns roughly centered within the hero, with 48–64px of breathing room above and below their tallest elements. The controller applies consistent horizontal gutters of 24–32px between columns and 24–32px vertical spacing between stacked subcomponents inside each column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in the normal page flow, occupying the full width and a fixed minimum height of one viewport minus the header. It enforces a two-column grid layout on larger screens and can allow responsive behavior (stacking columns vertically with the left content above the form) on narrow viewports without changing its visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel is positioned in the left column of the hero, anchored about one third down from the top of the hero area so it feels visually centered but slightly elevated. It spans roughly 70–80% of the left column width, aligning its left edge with the left margin of the inner content container and leaving 24–32px of whitespace to the right before the start of the form column. At the top of this panel, a bold, multi-line 'Contact Us' headline mirrors the vertical rhythm of the original 'No / Child / Hungry' logotype—stacked on two to three lines and occupying about the upper half of the panel height. Below the headline, 1–2 short lines of supportive copy and the primary contact details (e.g., general email, primary phone) are arranged in a compact stack with 8–16px vertical gaps. The entire panel sits directly above the additional-contact-list, with a clear 24–32px gap between them. Content sits directly on the dark overlay (no card background), relying on white or near-white text and generous line spacing to maintain legibility against the photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column of the grid, maintaining alignment with the contact-form’s top edge as the viewport resizes. On smaller screens where columns stack, it remains the first content block, appearing above the contact-form with the same vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form sits in the right column of the hero and is treated as a card-like panel floating above the darkened background. It occupies about 90–100% of the right column width, with its left edge aligned to the internal column gutter and its right edge stopping short of the far-right social bar, leaving a visible gap so the vertical social strip remains distinct. The form card uses a white or very light background, subtle rounded corners, and a soft shadow, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Its top edge is vertically aligned with the first text line of the contact-info-panel headline on the left, reinforcing the horizontal eye line. Inside, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between them, keeping the overall height balanced: name and email first, followed by message and any category/routing controls. At the bottom of the stack, a vivid brand-colored primary button (yellow or lime) spans roughly 60–80% of the card width, aligned left, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding that echoes the energetic Donate button treatment in the mockup. A 16–24px vertical gap separates the button from the FAQ link below it. The card leaves 24–32px of space above it for the optional form-status-banner, so when the banner appears, the form shifts downward without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card anchored in the right column of the hero layout. Its height expands based on content, pushing downward within the hero but maintaining top alignment with the left column content. On smaller viewports where columns stack, the form appears immediately below the contact-info-panel, maintaining its card styling and internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status-banner appears directly above the contact-form card within the right column, visually tied to the form but separated by a small gap. It spans the same width as the contact-form (roughly 90–100% of the right column width) and maintains a consistent left alignment. When visible, it forms a low-profile horizontal strip, about 48–64px tall, with 16–24px internal padding. The background color is a tinted status color (e.g., soft green for success, soft red/orange for errors) that stands out against the dark hero overlay but is less intense than the main CTAs. The banner sits 8–16px above the top edge of the form card, creating a clear but tight visual connection. Body text inside is centered vertically and left-aligned, with optional small status icon at the left. When not active, the banner collapses entirely, allowing the form to shift up and reclaim its space, preserving the hero’s clean look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered element within the right column stack. It does not reserve permanent vertical space; instead, it pushes the contact-form downward when shown. It scrolls with the page content and is not fixed or sticky, ensuring that status messages remain visually associated with the form location.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-link is positioned immediately below the primary submit button inside the contact-form card, functioning as a subtle secondary action. It aligns to the left edge of the button text, sitting 16–24px beneath the button to create a clear hierarchy while still reading as part of the same interaction group. The link is presented either as a text link with a small arrow/chevron icon or as a slim pill-style button with minimal border, occupying about 50–60% of the card width to remain visually lighter than the main CTA. Its color contrasts the white card background but uses a lower-emphasis brand tone (e.g., blue or purple text) to keep it secondary. The placement ensures that after scanning the form fields and main button, the user’s eye naturally drops to this alternative path before leaving the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored within the contact-form card’s layout. It moves with the form content as the page scrolls and adjusts its position relative to the submit button if the form content grows or shrinks. On smaller viewports, it remains directly under the primary button, preserving its secondary but tightly associated relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The additional-contact-list is placed in the left column, directly below the contact-info-panel, acting as a structured set of secondary contact options (e.g., departmental emails, mailing address, press contact). It spans roughly the same width as the contact-info-panel (70–80% of the left column width) and aligns with its left edge. A 24–32px vertical gap separates the bottom of the contact-info-panel from the top of this list, clearly defining it as a follow-up section. Each item in the list is rendered as a compact row or small card with 16–24px internal padding, subtle dividing lines or light card backgrounds, and 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. The overall list height is kept moderate so it occupies the lower half of the left column without extending beyond the form card height in the right column when possible, preserving visual balance across the hero. Icons and short labels (e.g., phone icon + ‘Media Inquiries’) align left, with any secondary text or links on the right of each row, maintaining a clean, scan-friendly layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked list within the left column. It expands downward as items are added, but remains constrained within the hero’s visible area on common desktop viewports. On narrower screens where columns stack, it appears directly below the main contact-info-panel and above any content that might follow the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-handler is a non-visual logic component associated with the contact-page-controller. It does not occupy any visible space on the page. Functionally, it tracks URL parameters or hash fragments to control the initial state of the contact experience—for example, pre-selecting a topic in the contact-form, triggering a specific message in the form-status-banner, or deep-linking to a particular contact method in the additional-contact-list. Its position in the component tree can be thought of as sitting behind the hero layout, orchestrating state before any visible elements render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No rendered spatial behavior; it operates entirely behind the scenes without consuming layout space. It affects the configuration and visibility of other components (such as form-status-banner and contact-form defaults) but does not scroll or display within the UI.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: Page for searching, filtering, and viewing frequently asked questions about the organization’s work, donations, sponsorships, volunteering, events, fundraising, and advocacy, with URL-based state and a path to contact support.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries from the getFaqEntries interface on page initialization.
Display FAQs grouped or filterable by their dynamic category values returned by getFaqEntries.
Provide keyword search over questions and answers, with the search term stored in the URL (e.g., q parameter) for deep-linking and sharable states.
Allow filtering by a selected FAQ category via URL parameter (e.g., category), combining with keyword search when both are present.
Show a list of matching FAQ entries, each with expandable/collapsible answers (accordion behavior).
Support direct linking to a specific FAQ entry via URL parameter (e.g., id) so that entry is expanded and focused on load.
Display an informative empty state when no FAQ entries match the current filters, with a direct link to the Contact page.
Provide a persistent "Still need help? Contact us" link that navigates to contact.html.
user_workflows:
Find an answer by searching:
1. User navigates to faq.html (no query parameters).
2. The page controller calls getFaqEntries and stores the returned FAQ entries.
3. The search input initializes its value from the q URL parameter (empty on first load).
4. User types a keyword in the search field and submits the form.
5. The search component updates the URL to include q=<searchTerm>, preserving any existing category parameter if present.
6. The page controller re-computes the filtered FAQ list using the updated q URL parameter and the previously loaded entries.
7. The FAQ list component displays only entries whose question or answer text matches the search term.
8. User expands an entry to read the full answer.

Browse by topic/category:
1. User navigates to faq.html.
2. The page controller calls getFaqEntries and derives the unique set of category values from the returned entries.
3. The category filter component reads the current category URL parameter (if any) and highlights the matching category, or defaults to showing all when no category is specified.
4. User clicks a category filter control.
5. The category filter updates the URL to set category=<selectedCategory>, preserving any existing q parameter.
6. The page controller re-applies filters based on the updated URL: it selects only entries whose category matches the category parameter, and also matches the q parameter if present.
7. The FAQ list displays the filtered entries for that category.

Open a specific FAQ via shared link:
1. A user receives a link in the form faq.html?id=<faqId>&category=<optionalCategory>&q=<optionalSearchTerm>.
2. On navigation to this URL, the page controller calls getFaqEntries and waits for entries to load.
3. Once entries are loaded, the controller applies filters using category and q (if present) and identifies the entry whose id matches the id URL parameter.
4. The FAQ list renders the filtered entries and passes the active FAQ id to each accordion item.
5. The accordion item whose id matches the URL id is rendered in an expanded state, and the list scrolls or focuses to that item so the user sees the specific answer immediately.
6. If the user collapses this entry, the id parameter is removed from the URL; if they expand another entry, the URL id parameter is updated to the newly selected FAQ id.

Contacting support from FAQ:
1. User browses or searches FAQs and either finds no relevant answers or sees an empty results state.
2. The page displays a "Still need help? Contact us" link.
3. User clicks this link.
4. The browser navigates to contact.html as defined in the site navigation (no additional parameters required).
interactions:
Page load:
- Event: onLoad in the page controller.
- Behavior: Calls getFaqEntries once, stores the array of FAQ entries in memory, then reads current URL parameters (q, category, id) and computes the filtered and active FAQ state passed to child components.

Keyword search input:
- Event: onChange on the search input updates the local input value.
- Event: onSubmit on the search form prevents default navigation, updates the q URL parameter with the current input value (or removes it if empty), and re-derives the filtered list from the updated URL and the loaded FAQ entries.
- Behavior: When q changes, the FAQ list is re-filtered client-side without another call to getFaqEntries.

Category filter selection:
- Event: onClick on a category option.
- Behavior: Updates the category URL parameter to the clicked category value (or removes it to show all categories), while preserving the current q parameter if present.
- Behavior: After URL change, the page controller recomputes the subset of FAQ entries to show based on category and q.

FAQ accordion toggle:
- Event: onClick on a FAQ question header (and onKeyPress for keyboard accessibility).
- Behavior on expand: Sets this FAQ entry as the active one, updates the id URL parameter to the entry’s id, and collapses any previously active entry if single-open behavior is desired.
- Behavior on collapse: Clears the active FAQ id in local state and removes the id parameter from the URL.
- Behavior: The answer content is shown or hidden based on whether the entry’s id matches the active id from URL/state.

Empty state and contact navigation:
- Condition: When the filtered FAQ results array is empty after applying category and q filters.
- Behavior: The empty-state component displays an explanatory message and the "Still need help? Contact us" call-to-action.
- Event: onClick on the contact link navigates the browser to contact.html.

URL change handling:
- Event: onUrlChange (e.g., via back/forward navigation or programmatic updates).
- Behavior: The page controller re-reads q, category, and id parameters, recomputes filters and active item, and updates child components so the view always reflects the current URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The FAQ page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for view state derived by the page controller after loading data from getFaqEntries. Supported parameters include: (1) q: the keyword search term. When present and non-empty, the page controller filters the FAQ entries so only those whose question or answer text contain the term are included. When q is removed or empty, no search-based filtering is applied. (2) category: the selected FAQ category. The controller filters the entries to only those whose category field equals this value. When the category parameter is absent, all categories are included. The set of possible category values is derived dynamically from the category fields of the entries returned by getFaqEntries. (3) id: the active/expanded FAQ entry. When id is present, the entry whose id matches this value is considered active and is expanded by the accordion component if it is part of the current filtered set; other entries are collapsed unless the design allows multiple open items. When id is absent, no entry is forced open. All filters (category, q) are applied purely client-side on the results of getFaqEntries; no additional data fetching is triggered by URL changes. Browser navigation (back/forward) updates these parameters and, via the onUrlChange handler, causes the page controller to recompute filtered results and active entry state so that the UI always matches the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state across the FAQ page. On page load, calls getFaqEntries to retrieve all FAQs. Derives the set of distinct categories from the category field of each entry. Reads the current URL parameters (q, category, id), computes the filtered list (by category and search term), and determines which entry (if any) is active based on id. Passes entries, categories, filters, and active entry id as props to the search, category filter, FAQ list, empty state, and contact CTA components. Listens for URL changes (e.g., history navigation) and re-runs the filtering logic to keep the UI in sync with the URL.

Provides a text input for keyword searching across FAQ questions and answers. Initializes its displayed value from the q URL parameter when the page loads or when the URL changes. When the user submits the form, it updates the q parameter in the URL (adding it when non-empty, removing it when cleared) without calling getFaqEntries again, relying on the page controller’s onUrlChange logic to re-filter the already-loaded data. Optionally supports live search by updating the URL after a short delay on input change.

Displays available FAQ categories for filtering, derived from the category fields of all entries provided by the page controller. Highlights the currently active category based on the category URL parameter, and includes an option to clear the category filter (show all) when no parameter is set. When a category option is clicked, the component updates the category URL parameter to the selected category value (or removes it when the "all" option is chosen), preserving the current q parameter. The page controller then recomputes and updates the filtered FAQ list based on the new URL.

Renders the current list of FAQ entries after filtering by category and search term, or shows nothing if there are no entries (the empty-state component handles messaging). Receives from the page controller: an array of FAQ entries to display, the active FAQ id (if any), and possibly the current search term for highlighting matches. For each entry, it instantiates a FAQ accordion item, passing through the entry’s id, question, answer, category, and a boolean flag indicating whether it should be rendered in the expanded state (id matches the active id). It also ensures the active item is scrolled into view or focused when the active id changes.

Represents a single FAQ entry with a clickable question header and a collapsible answer body. Receives the entry’s id, question, answer, and a boolean indicating whether it is currently active/expanded. When the user activates the header (click or keyboard action), the component checks if it is already active. If it is inactive, it triggers a handler to set this id as active and update the id URL parameter accordingly; if another item was active, that one becomes inactive. If it is already active and the user collapses it, the component clears the active state and removes the id parameter from the URL. The answer content is shown only when the item is active. It does not call getFaqEntries directly; it relies on its parent for data.
