Displays when the page controller determines that, after applying the current URL-based filters (q and category), there are zero FAQ entries to show. It may optionally display the current search term or category name from URL parameters to help users understand why no results were found. It also includes a prompt directing users to contact support for additional help, typically rendering or embedding the shared 'Still need help? Contact us' CTA component.

Provides the "Still need help? Contact us" link specified for this page. When activated, it navigates the user to contact.html using standard browser navigation. It can be displayed persistently at the bottom of the FAQ list or reused within the empty state component. It does not pass additional parameters to contact.html, matching the defined outgoing navigation behavior.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header at the very top (outside this page's components), followed by a full-width FAQ hero section that fills the remaining initial viewport. Within that hero, content is composed in a loose two-column layout: a large, expressive FAQ title and short intro copy on the left, and a narrow, high-focus utility column on the right that stacks the FAQ search form and category filter together in a single card. Beneath the hero, the FAQ list occupies the main central column in a clean vertical stack of accordion items. Independent of the grid, a slim, fixed contact CTA strip hugs the far-right edge of the viewport, echoing the social bar pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header at the very top (outside this page's components), followed by a full-width FAQ hero section that fills the remaining initial viewport. Within that hero, content is composed in a loose two-column layout: a large, expressive FAQ title and short intro copy on the left, and a narrow, high-focus utility column on the right that stacks the FAQ search form and category filter together in a single card. Beneath the hero, the FAQ list occupies the main central column in a clean vertical stack of accordion items. Independent of the grid, a slim, fixed contact CTA strip hugs the far-right edge of the viewport, echoing the social bar pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height with a white background. The FAQ hero controlled by the page controller fills the remaining 85–90% of the first viewport and uses a darkened photo background. Inside the hero, the left ~55–60% of the content width is reserved for the prominent FAQ title/brand expression, while the right ~25–30% is occupied by the search and filter card. The last ~10–15% on the far right is reserved for a fixed vertical contact CTA strip, which sits on top of the hero and subsequent content. Below the hero, the FAQ list spans about 70% of the page width, centered or slightly left of center, leaving generous side gutters for breathing room and the floating right-edge contact CTA.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a moderate content density. The hero shows only a handful of elements: a big playful FAQ heading on the left, and a compact search-and-filter card on the right, both surrounded by generous negative space to keep the busy photo background in check. The FAQ list below is a vertical stack of accordion items with clear separation between questions, avoiding long, unbroken text blocks. Spacing follows the 8px base system, with 48–64px between the hero and the list, and 16–24px between individual FAQ items. The fixed right-edge contact CTA is visually slim and unobtrusive, maintaining a tertiary feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow echoes the original F/Z pattern. The user starts at the top-left global logo in the header, then scans across the centered navigation toward the top-right global donate button. As the eye drops into the hero, it first meets the large, playful FAQ heading on the left, then moves diagonally to the bright, carded FAQ search form on the right, which is the primary interaction point. From there, attention flows downward to the category filter strip directly beneath the search field, and then continues vertically into the FAQ list of accordion items below. Finally, as a tertiary pathway, the eye notices the bright, vertical contact CTA strip fixed along the far-right edge, always available as a backup action for unanswered questions.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar white header bar, the FAQ page opens into a full-width hero section with a darkened photograph of volunteers, tinted by a subtle overlay. Within a centered content container, the left side is dominated by a large, playful “FAQ / Have Questions?” heading stacked over one or two short lines of explanatory text, echoing the bold logotype treatment from the home hero. On the right, occupying about a third of the content width, a bright, card-like panel floats above the background: at its top sits a prominent search field labeled “Search FAQs”, with generous padding and a clear call-to-action button. Directly beneath the search, a tight horizontal band of colored category pills lets users filter by topic, mirroring the payment-strip-under-donate pattern from the mockup. The far-right edge of the viewport carries a narrow, vertical yellow strip that stays fixed as the user scrolls; it contains a rotated or stacked “Need more help? Contact us” CTA, mimicking the social bar’s position and behavior. Scrolling down, the hero gives way to a clean, light background area where the FAQ list is vertically stacked in the central column, each accordion card clearly separated and easy to scan. When no results are found, the list area is replaced by a friendly empty state centered in the same footprint, with copy that subtly points back to the right-edge contact CTA. The result is a page that feels consistent with the photo-driven, high-contrast hero of the mockup, but tuned for search, filtering, and reading rather than a single donation action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller occupies the full width of the viewport beneath the global header and defines the overall grid and hero composition. It establishes a max-width content container (roughly 1120–1280px) centered on the page, aligned with the header’s content width. The top portion it controls is a hero section that fills about 85–90% of the initial viewport height and uses a full-bleed background photo with a dark overlay extending edge to edge. Inside this hero, the controller splits the inner content into a 12-column grid: the left 7–8 columns are reserved for the large FAQ heading and intro copy, while the right 3–4 columns are reserved for the stacked search-and-filter card. A vertical gutter of 24–32px separates the left and right columns. The hero’s main content is vertically positioned so that both the heading and the search panel sit slightly above the vertical center of the viewport, leaving a comfortable buffer (48–64px) below them before the fold. Below the hero, the page controller places the FAQ list section on a light, solid background, maintaining the same centered content width and grid alignment so that the top edge of the FAQ list lines up visually with the content boundaries of the hero above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural container in the normal document flow, controlling full-width background behavior for the hero and standard flow for the content below. It does not scroll independently or stick; it simply defines the grid and sections that other components plug into.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search form is placed inside the hero’s right column, occupying the top portion of a vertically stacked utility panel. Visually, it appears as a bright, card-like block (e.g., white or lightly tinted surface) floating on top of the dark photo, with soft shadows and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. This card sits in the rightmost 3–4 grid columns, aligned flush to the right edge of the main content container and inset about 24–32px from the top edge of the hero’s inner content zone. The card’s width is roughly 25–30% of the total page width on desktop. The search input spans nearly the full width of the card (leaving 16–24px padding on left and right), with a clear text field and a bold, high-contrast search button either attached to the right of the field or positioned directly beneath it. Vertical spacing between the label, input, and button follows the base 8px scale (8–16px increments), ensuring clarity without feeling cramped. Below the search form, space is reserved for the category filter bar so that the two read as a single functional stack. This placement makes the search form the primary focal point of the right side of the hero, analogous to the yellow “Donate Now” block in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the hero section in the normal flow of the page. It scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page and does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ category filter sits immediately below the search form inside the same right-column card, forming a compact vertical stack of related controls. It occupies the full internal width of the card (minus 16–24px horizontal padding), and its top edge is placed about 16–24px beneath the bottom of the search field/button, echoing the tight visual relationship between the PayPal button and the payment-icon strip in the original hero. The filter itself presents as a horizontal row of pill-shaped buttons that wrap onto a second line if needed; the pills are spaced at 8–12px from each other, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding per pill, matching the clickable sizing used in the navigation and other pills. The overall height of the filter band is relatively shallow compared to the search block, so it reads as a supporting control rather than a separate section. Within the hero, the combined search-and-filter card does not extend to the very bottom; there is at least 32–48px of empty space below the lowest pill and the bottom edge of the card to preserve breathing room. This positioning reinforces the feeling that the category pills are a quick, secondary refinement immediately available after the main search action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the right-column card in the normal flow. It moves together with the search form as the page scrolls and does not stick or detach from the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list is positioned directly below the hero section, starting approximately 48–64px beneath the bottom edge of the hero content to create a clear section break. It resides in the same centered content container as the hero, aligned so that its left edge matches the left edge of the hero’s large FAQ heading above. On desktop, the list occupies about 70% of the page width, equivalent to roughly 8–9 of the 12 grid columns, leaving a generous right-side gutter that visually accommodates the fixed right-edge contact CTA strip without making the list feel crowded. The list is a vertical stack of accordion items, each card spanning the full width of this list column. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between items, stemming from the base 8px grid, which keeps each question clearly delineated. The overall block has top and bottom padding of 48–64px so that the list feels like a substantial, but not overwhelming, reading area. When many FAQs are present, the list can extend well below the fold, with the fixed right-edge contact CTA remaining visible as the main content scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow under the hero, scrolling naturally with the rest of the page content. It does not have its own scrolling container; the browser viewport handles scrolling over the entire list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item accordion appears as a full-width card within the FAQ list column. The card’s width matches the list’s width (approximately 70% of the page, aligned left within the main content container). It has 16–24px of internal padding, with a slightly elevated surface (subtle shadow or border) that lifts it off the background in the same way as the trust badges and donor cards in the mockup. The top portion of the card holds the question row: a bold, single-line question aligned to the left, with an expand/collapse icon aligned to the far right. This header row has 12–16px of vertical padding to create a large, clickable target. When expanded, the answer content drops down within the same card, indented slightly from the left edge of the question text and separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. The answers use comfortable line spacing and are constrained to moderate text length so that each accordion doesn’t feel like a dense wall of text. Vertical spacing between accordion items is 16–24px, creating a rhythm similar to the stacked trust cards in the hero of the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each accordion item is part of the FAQ list’s standard flow. Individual items expand and collapse in place, pushing other items down or up without overlapping. They do not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ empty state occupies the same spatial footprint as the FAQ list, appearing in its place whenever search and filter criteria return no results. It is centered within the list column, with a total width of about 50–60% of the page (still aligned left with the main content column) and generous vertical padding of 64px above and below the core message. Visually, it appears as a light, friendly panel or illustration on a neutral background, echoing the playful child-centric aesthetic of the brand but with restrained detail to avoid clutter. The main empty-state message is centered within this panel, with a short explanatory line beneath it. Additional supporting text subtly directs users to refine their search or adjust filters, and explicitly references the always-visible contact CTA on the right edge (e.g., “Still can’t find what you’re looking for? Use the Contact Us button on the right.”). The panel leaves ample whitespace to its right (the same gutter as the list), ensuring that the fixed vertical contact strip remains clearly visible and contextually relevant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the FAQ list in the normal document flow when active. It scrolls with the page like any other block and does not introduce its own scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ contact CTA is implemented as a slim, vertical strip fixed to the far-right edge of the viewport, mirroring the social icon bar from the original mockup. It sits on top of the hero and all subsequent sections, starting just below the global header and extending downward approximately 160–240px, depending on content. The strip is about 56–72px wide, using a bright brand color (typically the vivid yellow) to stand out against both the dark hero background and the lighter body content. Within the strip, the content is stacked vertically: a small help or chat icon near the top, and either vertically stacked or rotated text reading “Contact Us” or “Need Help?” in a clear, high-contrast type. Internal padding of 16px keeps the content away from the edges. The strip is visually separated from the main content by the page’s right gutter; it does not align with the 12-column grid, reinforcing its role as an always-available tertiary action. On scroll, the contact strip remains pinned to the right side of the viewport, so users scanning long lists of FAQs always have a quick path to direct support.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the right edge of the viewport (position: fixed), independent of the main layout grid. It maintains a constant position relative to the viewport as the user scrolls the page, starting just below the header and not intersecting with the primary content area.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that retrieves and displays the organization’s privacy policy content, with section navigation, deep links, and in-page search using data from getPrivacyPolicyContent.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest privacy policy content (last updated date and all sections) using getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Provide an auto-generated table of contents based on returned sections, allowing users to jump to specific sections.
Support deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters so links from donation, event, sponsorship, advocacy, and communications forms can open the relevant policy section.
Offer an in-page text search of the privacy policy that filters or highlights sections based on a query reflected in the URL.
Allow users to generate and copy a direct link to the currently viewed section, updating the URL state without reloading the page.
Handle loading and error states for the privacy policy fetch, with a user-triggered retry on failure.
user_workflows:
View the full privacy policy:
1. User navigates to privacy.html (e.g., from footer link).
2. On page initialization, the privacy-policy-data-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. While loading, a loading state is displayed.
4. When data is returned, the privacy-policy-header shows the last updated date, the privacy-policy-toc lists all section titles, and the privacy-policy-section-list renders all sections in order.
5. The user scrolls through the content to read the policy.

Jump to a specific section from another page (deep link):
1. User clicks a “Privacy Policy” link on a form (e.g., donations, event registrations, advocacy tools) that opens privacy.html with a section-related URL parameter, such as privacy.html?section={sectionSlug}.
2. On load, the privacy-policy-permalink-handler reads the section parameter and stores an activeSection identifier in page state.
3. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, privacy-policy-toc marks the corresponding section as active, and privacy-policy-section-list scrolls to and/or visually emphasizes that section.
4. The user can scroll normally or click other TOC entries to move between sections, with the URL’s section parameter updating accordingly.

Search within the privacy policy:
1. User opens privacy.html or is already on the page.
2. The privacy-policy-search-form reads any existing q parameter from the URL and pre-fills the search input.
3. The user types a query and submits the search (or triggers search on change).
4. The search component updates the q parameter in the URL (e.g., privacy.html?q={query}&section={currentSectionSlug}).
5. privacy-policy-section-list uses the query to filter the displayed sections or highlight matching text within sections.
6. The user can clear the search, which removes the q parameter and restores the full list of sections.

Copy a direct link to a specific section:
1. User scrolls to a section or clicks it from the table of contents.
2. privacy-policy-permalink-handler updates the section parameter in the URL to match the currently focused section.
3. The user clicks a “Copy link” action associated with that section (e.g., in the section header).
4. The handler constructs a URL including the current section parameter and copies it to the clipboard.
5. The user can share this URL; recipients opening it arrive at privacy.html with the same section parameter, triggering the deep-link workflow.

Handle errors when loading the policy:
1. User navigates to privacy.html.
2. getPrivacyPolicyContent fails (e.g., network or server error).
3. privacy-policy-error-state displays an error message and a “Try again” control.
4. User clicks retry; privacy-policy-data-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent again.
5. On success, the standard display, navigation, and search behavior becomes available.
interactions:
Click footer link or other direct links:
- Interaction: User clicks a link labeled “Privacy Policy” from the footer or from consent notices.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to privacy.html, optionally with URL parameters like section and q.
- Components involved: Browser navigation; after load, privacy-policy-data-loader, privacy-policy-permalink-handler.

Load privacy policy content:
- Interaction: Page initializes.
- Behavior: privacy-policy-data-loader automatically calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters.
- Result: On success, lastUpdated and sections are stored in state and propagated to other components; on failure, privacy-policy-error-state is shown.

Use table of contents navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks a section title in privacy-policy-toc.
- Behavior: onSectionLinkClick prevents a full page reload, sets the activeSection state, updates the URL’s section parameter, and scrolls to the corresponding section in privacy-policy-section-list.
- Result: That section becomes active; other components reflect this state (e.g., highlighting in TOC).

Scroll through sections:
- Interaction: User manually scrolls the page.
- Behavior: As the user scrolls, the currently visible section can be detected; privacy-policy-permalink-handler may update activeSection and synchronize the section parameter in the URL (using history.replaceState or similar) without reloading.
- Result: The URL always reflects the section currently in view, allowing easy link sharing from the browser’s address bar.

Search within the policy:
- Interaction: User types into the privacy-policy-search-form input and submits the form (or triggers a search on change).
- Behavior: onSearchSubmit reads the input value, updates the q parameter in the URL, and updates a searchQuery state.
- Result: privacy-policy-section-list filters or highlights sections based on searchQuery, and the search term is retained in the input via data binding. Clearing the search removes q from the URL and resets the display.

Copy section permalink:
- Interaction: User clicks a “Copy link” control in a section header.
- Behavior: onCopyLinkClick constructs a URL that includes the current section parameter, copies it to the clipboard, and may provide a temporary success indication.
- Result: The user has a shareable URL that, when opened, deep-links to that section.

Retry after load error:
- Interaction: User clicks a “Try again” button in the error state.
- Behavior: onRetryClick triggers getPrivacyPolicyContent again via privacy-policy-data-loader.
- Result: On success, the content is rendered; on subsequent failure, the error state is re-shown.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters to control and reflect state, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence.

URL parameters:
- section: Identifies the currently focused/active section of the privacy policy. The value is compared against a derived identifier for each section (for example, a slug based on sections[i].title or a stable identifier provided in the content). Behavior:
  - On initial load, privacy-policy-permalink-handler reads section. If present, it sets activeSection state.
  - After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the handler matches section to a specific entry in the sections array. If matched, privacy-policy-section-list scrolls to that section and may visually emphasize it.
  - When the user clicks a TOC entry or scrolls to a new section, the handler updates activeSection and synchronizes the section parameter using the browser history API (e.g., updating the query string) without reloading.

- q: Represents the current in-page search query.
  - On initial load, privacy-policy-search-form reads q from the URL and initializes the search input and searchQuery state.
  - When the user submits a new search, onSearchSubmit updates q in the URL and searchQuery in state.
  - privacy-policy-section-list uses searchQuery to determine which sections to show or what terms to highlight. If q is empty or missing, all sections are shown with no highlight.

- mode (optional): Can be used to support alternative render modes such as a print-friendly layout (e.g., mode=print).
  - On load, privacy-policy-permalink-handler reads mode.
  - Components like privacy-policy-section-list and privacy-policy-toc can adjust their behavior (for example, hiding controls that are irrelevant in a print view) based on mode, without altering how getPrivacyPolicyContent is called.

State derivation:
- The only remote state is the result of getPrivacyPolicyContent, which returns lastUpdated and sections.
- Local UI state (activeSection, searchQuery, loading/error flags) is derived from:
  - URL parameters (section, q, mode).
  - The structure of the sections array returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
- Whenever the URL changes via browser navigation (back/forward), privacy-policy-permalink-handler re-reads the parameters and updates activeSection and searchQuery to keep the UI synchronized with the URL.

components.functionality: Calls getPrivacyPolicyContent on initialization, manages loading and error states, and exposes lastUpdated and sections to other components.
Displays the page title and the last updated date for the privacy policy using data from the loader component.
Generates a table of contents from the sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent and allows users to jump to specific sections, updating the activeSection state and URL section parameter.

Renders all privacy policy sections (title and body) from getPrivacyPolicyContent, applies optional highlighting or filtering based on searchQuery, and scrolls to or visually emphasizes the activeSection defined by URL state.
Provides a text input for searching within the privacy policy, synchronizes the search query with the q URL parameter, and emits searchQuery updates consumed by the section list.
Reads and writes URL parameters (section, q, optional mode), maintains activeSection and searchQuery in page state, and constructs permalinks for sections for sharing or copying.
Shows an error message and retry control when getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, and hides itself when data becomes available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed-height header (site-wide), then a full-width hero-like intro band specific to the Privacy Policy, followed by the main content area. Inside the hero band, content is arranged as a loose two-column layout: on the left, a large, typographic page title and brief description (replacing the large logotype); on the right, a narrow vertical stack with the in-page search form at the top and a compact table of contents beneath it (mirroring the donate CTA stack and trust cards). Below this band, the main policy sections appear in a left content column, while the TOC and search remain in a right sidebar. Non-visual components (data loader, permalink handler) sit logically around this layout without occupying visual space, and the error state temporarily replaces the main content region when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed-height header (site-wide), then a full-width hero-like intro band specific to the Privacy Policy, followed by the main content area. Inside the hero band, content is arranged as a loose two-column layout: on the left, a large, typographic page title and brief description (replacing the large logotype); on the right, a narrow vertical stack with the in-page search form at the top and a compact table of contents beneath it (mirroring the donate CTA stack and trust cards). Below this band, the main policy sections appear in a left content column, while the TOC and search remain in a right sidebar. Non-visual components (data loader, permalink handler) sit logically around this layout without occupying visual space, and the error state temporarily replaces the main content region when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header continues to occupy roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height with a white background. The Privacy Policy hero band fills the next 25–30% of the viewport, with a subtle, darkened background (image or flat color) and constrained inner content width. Within that band, about 60–65% of the width is allocated to the left title/description area and about 25–30% to the right search/TOC stack, with the remaining margin used as breathing room and alignment gutters. Beneath this, the main content area runs vertically: the section list takes about 60–65% of the width on the left, and the TOC/search sidebar keeps its 25–30% width on the right, with generous gutter space between columns. Vertical gaps between major sections follow the 48–64px rhythm, with 24–32px between stacked elements and internal card padding at 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout balances the inherently text-heavy nature of a privacy policy with the mockup’s moderate density. The hero band contains only a title, a short one- or two-line explanation, and the search + TOC stack, leaving plenty of negative space around them. The main policy text is broken into clearly separated sections with bold headings, ample line height, and 24–32px spacing between sections to avoid visual clutter. The right column (search + TOC) is visually compact but not cramped, using pill-like and card-like surfaces with 16–24px internal padding to echo the mockup’s CTAs and trust badges while keeping interactions distinct and scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow still follows an F/Z pattern: the eye begins at the global brand logo in the header on the top left, scans across the navigation to any primary site CTA at the top right. As the user moves into the page content, the eye drops diagonally into the hero band, landing first on the large 'Privacy Policy' title on the left. From there it moves horizontally to the right column’s bright, pill-like search box (taking the place of the hero donate button). Below that, the eye naturally moves downward through the table of contents links, then sweeps left into the detailed section content. As the user scrolls, the sticky right column keeps the search and TOC visible, providing a continuous secondary focal path analogous to the social/CTA bar in the original mockup, while the main reading flow stays in the left content column.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar site shell of a clean white header and a photo- or color-backed hero, the Privacy Policy page presents a focused, reading-friendly layout that echoes the original donate hero. Beneath the global navigation, a full-width intro band appears with a slightly darkened background and a constrained content container centered on the page. On the left side of this band, a large, bold 'Privacy Policy' heading and a short explanatory sentence are stacked and aligned slightly above the vertical center, reminiscent of the oversized logotype in the mockup. On the right, a tall, narrow panel houses a prominent, yellow-accented search field at the top and a neatly boxed list of section links beneath it, treating them visually like the donate button and trust badges. As the user scrolls past this hero band, the page transitions into a two-column main area: a wide left column for the full policy content and a narrower right sidebar that sticks within the viewport, keeping the search and section navigation always within reach. Generous vertical spacing, clear typographic hierarchy, and card-like surfaces maintain a playful yet professional nonprofit feel, while the underlying grid and column proportions preserve the mockup’s balance between a bold brand presence and a tidy, modern UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual infrastructure component positioned conceptually at the top of the Privacy Policy content region, just below the global header and above the hero band. While it occupies no dedicated visual area in the layout, its loading state can trigger a semi-transparent, dark overlay across the hero band and main content columns (similar in tone to the hero photo overlay) with a small, centered spinner or progress indicator. This overlay stays within the constrained content width, not full-bleed, and aligns vertically in the middle of the viewport, subtly dimming the title, search, TOC, and sections beneath while data is being retrieved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual by default; when active, it displays a centered loading indicator over the main content container using a modal-like overlay that scroll-locks the content area beneath until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-header component sits at the top of the page’s main content, inside a hero-like band that appears directly below the fixed-height site header. This band spans the full browser width but uses an inner content container centered on the page, constrained to roughly 1080–1200px. Within this container, the header content occupies the left 60–65% of the width. The title 'Privacy Policy' is rendered in large, bold type, roughly equivalent in visual weight to one line of the mockup’s large logotype, and is placed slightly above the vertical center of the hero band. Immediately beneath it, with 16–24px of vertical spacing, a short one- or two-line description of the policy appears in a smaller but still readable font. Both are left-aligned, with a generous left margin aligning with the main grid. There is ample space above and below the text (approximately 48–64px from the top of the band to the title, and similarly from the description to the bottom of the band), maintaining a clean, modern look and preventing the header from feeling crowded against the search/TOC column on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrolling page; its hero band moves out of view as the user scrolls down to read the policy, without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-toc appears as a vertically oriented navigation card in the right column, first within the hero band and then continuing down alongside the main content. In the hero band, it is positioned directly below the search form within the right 25–30% of the content container, forming a cohesive vertical stack. The TOC is enclosed in a soft, light card surface (white or very light neutral) with subtle shadow or border, 16–24px of internal padding, and rounded corners to echo the mockup’s trust badges. Its top edge sits approximately 16–24px below the search form, and its bottom edge initially aligns roughly with the bottom of the hero band. The TOC items are presented as a clean, single-column list with 8–12px vertical spacing between links, and each item has a comfortable click area, aligned left with minimal indentation. As the user scrolls into the main content area, this TOC card, together with the search form above it, behaves as a sticky sidebar: it stays pinned near the top-right of the viewport within its column, maintaining a 24–32px offset from the top so it never collides with the global header, while the left column scrolls more freely.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the right sidebar and becomes sticky once the user scrolls past the hero band’s initial position, staying fixed within that column while respecting top and side padding within the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-section-list forms the main reading area directly beneath the hero band, aligned with and visually extending from the left side of the privacy-policy-header. It occupies approximately 60–65% of the content container’s width, starting from the left grid margin, with a clear gutter (about one column width) separating it from the right sidebar that holds the search form and TOC. The top of the first section begins 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero band, preserving the major section spacing rhythm. Each policy section within the list is stacked vertically with generous spacing: a section heading in bold typography at the top, followed by its body copy, then a 24–32px gap before the next section heading. Long paragraphs use comfortable line length (roughly 60–80 characters per line) and increased line spacing for readability. Deep links map directly to these headings, which align horizontally with the TOC entries on the right so that scanning from a TOC item to the corresponding section is intuitive. The overall effect is a calm, scrollable column of content with enough breathing room that it does not feel dense, despite the length of the policy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main document flow; it scrolls independently while the right sidebar (search + TOC) remains sticky alongside it on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-search-form is positioned prominently at the top of the right column, mirroring the hero donate button’s visual emphasis. Within the hero band’s content container, it occupies the right 25–30% of the width, aligned to the top of the band so that its top edge approximately lines up horizontally with the baseline of the 'Privacy Policy' title on the left. The search input appears as a pill-like or softly rounded rectangular field with a bright yellow or accented outline/fill to echo the brand’s primary CTA treatment. It spans nearly the full width of the right column, with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding, and includes a clear search icon aligned to the left or right inside the field. There is at least 16–24px of whitespace above it and 16–24px below it before the TOC card begins, separating it as the first interactive focal point in the right stack. As the page transitions from the hero band into the main content area, the search form remains at the top of the sticky right sidebar, staying visible as the user scrolls, maintaining consistent horizontal alignment and column width with the TOC beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right sidebar in tandem with the TOC; once the user scrolls past the hero band, the search form pins near the top-right of the viewport, always visible for quick in-page searches.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-permalink-handler is an invisible state-sync layer integrated with both the TOC and the section list. It does not occupy any visible footprint on the page but influences how the left content column and right TOC behave during navigation. When the URL contains a section fragment or a user clicks a TOC link, the handler smoothly scrolls the main section list column so that the corresponding section heading aligns slightly below the top of the viewport (leaving about 64–80px padding from the top, accounting for the global header and sticky sidebar). Simultaneously, it highlights the relevant TOC item (for example, changing its background or text color) while subtly de-emphasizing others, and can optionally display a brief, unobtrusive highlight glow around the targeted section heading for a moment to orient the user. All of this happens within the constrained content container, preserving the layout proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates as a behavioral layer that intercepts scroll and link events to manage smooth in-page scrolling and active-state sync between the TOC and the section list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-error-state appears only when content retrieval fails, taking over the main content region beneath the global header. In this state, the usual hero band and column layout are effectively replaced: instead, a centered error card is displayed within the same constrained content container, aligned horizontally in the middle of the page and vertically offset from the top by about 96–120px to feel visually centered without colliding with the fixed header. The card spans roughly 50–60% of the content container width, leaving generous margins on either side. It uses the same light card aesthetic as the TOC—rounded corners, subtle elevation, and 24px internal padding—with a succinct error title at the top, a short explanatory message beneath, and any retry or support action buttons aligned left or center at the bottom. The background around the card remains clean and uncluttered, echoing the mockup’s darkened hero overlay simplicity, so the user’s attention is focused on the error message. The search form, TOC, and section list are either hidden or dimmed behind the card, ensuring the error state is clearly communicated as the primary content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the main content flow but visually centered; when active, it replaces or obscures the normal hero and main content, and scrolls normally with the page if the user moves up or down.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the full, dynamically loaded terms and conditions for using the website and participating in donations, sponsorships, events, fundraising campaigns, and other activities.

core_features: Retrieve and display the latest terms and conditions using getTermsAndConditionsContent, including last-updated metadata and all sections.
Generate a dynamic table of contents from the returned sections to allow quick navigation within the page.
Support deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters so that other pages (e.g., donation or registration forms) can link directly to relevant terms.
Allow users to switch between viewing all sections at once or focusing on a single section, controlled via URL parameters (e.g., view mode and section selection).

Provide an optional context-aware notice based on URL parameters (e.g., context=donation, context=sponsorship) to help users understand which part of the terms may be most relevant to their current task.
user_workflows: Viewing the full terms: 1) User navigates to terms.html without parameters; 2) On page load, the controller calls getTermsAndConditionsContent; 3) The page displays the last updated date and all sections in a scrollable layout; 4) A dynamic table of contents is built from the section titles for in-page navigation.

Navigating via table of contents: 1) User clicks a section item in the table of contents; 2) The click handler updates the URL query parameter ?section=<derived-section-slug> and optionally ?view=single; 3) The page state updates to highlight that section and scrolls it into view; 4) If view=single, only that section body is shown; otherwise, all sections remain visible but scrolled.

Arriving from a donation or registration form: 1) User clicks a link such as terms.html?context=donation&section=<slug>; 2) On load, the controller reads context and section from the URL; 3) getTermsAndConditionsContent is called to fetch current terms; 4) A context notice references the context parameter; 5) The section matching the section parameter is focused and, if view is not set, the controller can default to view=single for that section; 6) Any subsequent navigation within the page continues to update URL parameters so the user can return directly to the same view.

Switching between full and focused view: 1) User clicks a control to toggle between 'all sections' and 'single section' views; 2) The click handler updates the URL parameter view=all or view=single (preserving the current section parameter if present); 3) The section viewer component re-renders to either show all sections or only the currently selected section; 4) The URL reflects the new state so reloading or sharing preserves the chosen view.
interactions: On initial page load or when URL query parameters change, the controller triggers getTermsAndConditionsContent and passes the response to child components.

When a user clicks a table-of-contents entry, the terms-table-of-contents component emits an onSectionClick event with the selected section identifier; the controller translates this into a derived slug for the URL (?section=...) and updates the browser history, then instructs the section viewer to focus/scroll to that section.

When a user clicks a 'View all sections' or 'View only this section' toggle, the terms-view-mode-toggle component emits onViewModeChange, updating the view URL parameter and causing the section viewer to re-render accordingly.

When the user follows a deep link containing ?section=... (and optionally ?view=single), the controller uses these parameters to determine the initially selected section and view mode, so the section viewer scrolls and focuses correctly without additional user action.

When the user arrives with a ?context=... parameter, the context-notice component reads this value from the controller, displays a generic explanatory message keyed to that context value, and does not alter any backend calls (which always use getTermsAndConditionsContent without parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL query parameters and the response from getTermsAndConditionsContent. The controller always calls getTermsAndConditionsContent with no arguments on initial load or hard refresh. The following query parameters are used: 1) section: a slug-like identifier derived on the client from section.title (e.g., lowercased and sanitized) to indicate which section should be considered 'selected'. If section is present, the section viewer highlights and scrolls to the matching section; if absent, no specific section is selected. 2) view: determines layout mode; if view=single and section is present, only the selected section is rendered; if view=all or view is absent, all sections are rendered, with the selected one simply highlighted and scrolled into view if section is set. 3) context: an optional string passed from other pages (e.g., donation, sponsorship, events, or fundraising) to indicate the user's originating context. This value is consumed only by a context notice component, which uses it to render generic contextual language; it does not influence which terms are fetched. The controller listens for changes to these URL parameters (including navigation via the browser's back/forward buttons) and updates the selected section and view mode accordingly without additional backend requests unless the page is reloaded.

components.functionality: Orchestrates the Terms & Conditions page: calls getTermsAndConditionsContent, parses URL parameters (section, view, context), maintains in-memory state for selected section and view mode, and passes normalized data and state down to all child components.
event_handlers: onInit: On initial page load, read URL parameters, call getTermsAndConditionsContent, then initialize state (selectedSectionSlug, viewMode, context).

onUrlChange: When the query string changes (e.g., via internal navigation or browser back/forward), recompute selectedSectionSlug, viewMode, and context, then notify child components to re-render or re-scroll as needed.

onSectionSelected: Handler invoked when a child component requests a section change; updates URL parameter section=<slug> (and optionally view=single) using history.pushState so state is shareable and persistent.
onViewModeChanged: Handler invoked when view mode is toggled; updates URL parameter view=all or view=single (preserving section if present) using history.pushState.

onContentLoaded: After getTermsAndConditionsContent resolves, map raw sections into an internal representation that includes a derivedSlug for each section title for consistent matching against the section URL parameter.

components.functionality: Displays the 'last updated' information for the terms based on the lastUpdated value returned by getTermsAndConditionsContent.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Triggered when the controller provides the lastUpdated value; renders or updates the displayed date accordingly.

components.functionality: Builds a clickable table of contents from the dynamically loaded terms sections, enabling users to navigate quickly to any section and updating the URL parameters when a section is selected.
event_handlers: onSectionClick: When a user clicks a section title in the table of contents, emit the corresponding section's derived slug to the controller so it can update URL.query.section and optionally set view=single.
onStateUpdate: When the controller notifies that selectedSectionSlug has changed (due to URL changes or other interactions), update the active/selected state of the corresponding TOC item.

components.functionality: Renders the terms sections returned by getTermsAndConditionsContent and controls which sections are visible or emphasized based on the current view mode and selected section, also handling scroll-to-section behavior.
event_handlers: onRender: On initial render after content load, check controllerState.viewMode and controllerState.selectedSectionSlug to determine whether to render all sections or only the selected one and to perform any scroll-to-section action.

onSectionHeaderClick: If section headers are clickable within the viewer, emit a section selection event to the controller (similar to the TOC) so the URL.query.section is updated and that section becomes the selected one.
onViewModeChange: When notified by the controller that view mode has changed, re-render to either show all sections (view=all) or only the selected section (view=single).

components.functionality: Provides controls for the user to switch between viewing all sections and viewing only the currently selected section, reflecting and updating the view URL parameter.
event_handlers: onToggleToAll: When the user selects the 'all sections' mode, emit a viewModeChange event to the controller to set URL.query.view=all (leaving section unchanged, if present).

onToggleToSingle: When the user selects the 'single section' mode and a selectedSectionSlug exists, emit a viewModeChange event to the controller to set URL.query.view=single; if no section is selected yet, optionally request the controller to treat the first section as selected before applying the mode.
onStateSync: When the controller updates the current view mode (e.g., due to URL changes), update the toggle’s active state so the UI stays in sync.

components.functionality: Shows a brief contextual notice at the top of the page based on the context URL parameter (e.g., donation, sponsorship, event, fundraising), helping the user understand why they may have been sent to this page.
event_handlers: onInit: On initial load, read the context value passed down by the controller and render an appropriate generic message keyed to that value.
onContextChange: When the controller detects a change in the context URL parameter, update or hide the notice accordingly.

components.functionality: Manages scrolling and focus behavior for deep-linked sections based on the section URL parameter and section slugs derived from the terms content.
event_handlers: onSelectedSectionChange: When controllerState.selectedSectionSlug changes (either from URL changes or internal navigation), locate the DOM element corresponding to that section and smoothly scroll it into view and/or set focus for accessibility.
onContentLoaded: After sections are available, initialize a mapping from each section’s derived slug to its DOM element ID so that subsequent selection changes can be resolved efficiently.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column desktop grid with a constrained content width for the header and hero inner content, while background image and color bars extend full width.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing fixed-height header, the Terms & Conditions page uses a hero-like top band for context and metadata, followed by a full-width, two-column content layout with a slim, independent vertical anchor bar on the far right. The left column is dedicated to the main terms content, the right column stacks interaction and navigation controls (view mode toggle and table of contents), and the far-right strip holds deep-link anchors similar to the social bar pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing fixed-height header, the Terms & Conditions page uses a hero-like top band for context and metadata, followed by a full-width, two-column content layout with a slim, independent vertical anchor bar on the far right. The left column is dedicated to the main terms content, the right column stacks interaction and navigation controls (view mode toggle and table of contents), and the far-right strip holds deep-link anchors similar to the social bar pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header still occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height. Directly beneath it, a shallow but full-width context banner consumes roughly another 10–15% for the page title and notice. The remaining 70–80% of the initial viewport height is a two-column reading zone: about 60–65% width for the terms content on the left, 25–30% width for the TOC and view-mode controls on the right, and a slim 5–8% width reserved at the far right for the vertical deep-link anchor bar that sits just outside the main content container, echoing the social strip in the hero.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate: long text is present but broken into visually separated sections with generous 24–32px spacing between them. The right column uses card-like containers with 16–24px internal padding to keep navigation elements clear and tappable. The page avoids cramming; instead, it relies on comfortable line lengths, ample white space around interaction controls, and a restrained amount of supporting metadata visible at once.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The reading path mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern: the eye starts at the top-left of the context banner (page title and notice), glances to the top-right metadata (last updated), then moves diagonally down to the prominent view-mode toggle at the top of the right column. From there, the gaze shifts left into the main terms content for reading, occasionally drifting right to the TOC for navigation. The vertical deep-link anchor strip on the far right acts as a tertiary visual path, discovered after primary content and TOC, similar to how the social bar is noticed last in the original hero.

layout.overall_description: Under the existing site header, a full-width, lightly tinted context band stretches across the page, clearly announcing that this is the Terms & Conditions area. Within this band, the title and short explanatory text sit on the left, while a compact “Last updated” indicator aligns to the right, forming a balanced top row. Below this, the page resolves into a calm, two-column reading layout centered within a constrained content width. On the left, a spacious column holds the full text of the terms, with sections stacked vertically and separated by generous breathing room. On the right, a narrower but visually strong column begins with a bright, pill-like view-mode toggle aligned to the top—mirroring the hero’s prominent CTA—followed by a carded table of contents listing all major sections. Just beyond the right edge of this main content block, a slim vertical bar in a brand accent color is fixed or sticky along the viewport’s right side, hosting a vertical series of subtle deep-link indicators. Together, these elements recreate the original mockup’s asymmetrical, photo-hero energy in a text-heavy context: strong left content, a guided action/navigation stack on the right, and a tertiary vertical strip for advanced navigation, all framed by generous spacing and a modern nonprofit aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-page-controller sits as an invisible, full-width layer directly beneath the global header and above the footer, spanning 100% of the available page width and the full content height. It conceptually wraps the entire Terms & Conditions experience—including the context banner at the top, the left/right content columns, and the right-edge anchor bar—controlling loading states and data without occupying its own visible space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural wrapper that covers the whole content area below the header; it scrolls with the page and does not have fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-last-updated-indicator appears inside the full-width context banner at the top of the terms page. It is right-aligned within the constrained content width, sitting on the same horizontal line as the page title and notice copy. The text is compact and set in a smaller, secondary style, occupying roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the banner’s inner width. Vertically, it is centered within the banner’s height, with about 12–16px of padding above and below, matching the comfortable spacing of navigation items in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the context banner; it scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-table-of-contents is housed in the right column of the main content area, directly below the view-mode toggle. It sits within a card-like container that spans the full width of this right column (approximately 25–30% of the overall content width). The card has 16–24px internal padding on all sides, with each TOC item listed in a vertical stack separated by 8–16px. The top of the TOC card aligns visually with the upper portion of the first screen of the terms text on the left, creating a clear left-right alignment. The card extends downward, but its height is capped to show a reasonable number of section links at once; overflow content scrolls within the page, and the card maintains at least 24px of breathing room between its bottom edge and any content below (if present).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default it scrolls with the page, but on larger viewports it can become sticky within the right column, anchoring itself just below the header/context banner once scrolled, similar to a fixed CTA block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-viewer occupies the dominant left column of the main content area beneath the context banner. It spans roughly 60–65% of the constrained content width and is left-aligned within the main grid, leaving consistent margins on both sides. The text is laid out with a comfortable line length; each major section begins with a heading, followed by body text, and is separated from the next section by 24–32px of vertical space, echoing the generous spacing seen between hero elements. The top of the section viewer starts about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the context banner, creating a clear break between the header area and the reading area. As users scroll, this column becomes the primary scrolling surface, with the TOC and anchor bar acting as navigational companions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable content column that moves naturally with the page; no fixed positioning, but the layout is anchored to a 12-column grid with this viewer spanning roughly 7–8 columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-view-mode-toggle is positioned at the very top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the first visible lines of the terms-section-viewer on the left. It appears as a prominent pill or segmented control occupying about 60–80% of the right column’s width, centered or slightly left-aligned within that column. Vertical padding of 8–12px and horizontal padding of 16–24px give it a button-like presence similar in weight to the hero’s primary CTA. There is 16–24px of space below the toggle before the start of the TOC card, visually establishing it as the topmost action control in the right stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, it can become sticky within the right column once the user scrolls past the context banner, maintaining a constant position near the top of the viewport while the terms text scrolls beneath, reinforcing its role as a mode switcher.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-context-notice is a full-width horizontal band immediately below the global header, visually analogous to a shallow hero overlay strip. It stretches across 100% of the viewport width but constrains its inner content to the site’s standard max width. Within this band, the left two-thirds are used for the page title (e.g., “Terms & Conditions”) and a brief explanation line stacked vertically, while the right third houses the last-updated indicator. The band has 24–32px vertical padding, giving it a strong but not overwhelming presence, and approximately 32–48px of space separates its bottom edge from the main two-column content area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section header that scrolls away with the page; its background can use a soft brand color or a subtle tinted overlay to differentiate it from the white main content background, echoing the hero’s overlay effect.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-deep-link-anchor-manager manifests as a slim vertical strip along the far right side of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s yellow social bar. It sits just outside or flush with the right edge of the main content container, occupying a narrow 5–8% of the page width or a fixed 48–64px, whichever is smaller, and extends from slightly below the bottom of the header/context band down toward the lower portion of the viewport. Within this strip, small circular or icon-based markers are stacked vertically, each representing a major section of the terms. There is generous 16–24px vertical spacing between markers, and the strip itself uses a vivid brand color (e.g., yellow) to subtly stand out without competing with primary content. The left edge of the strip aligns visually with the right edge of the TOC column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky or fixed relative to the viewport on desktop: it remains visible along the right edge as the user scrolls through the terms, updating state to indicate the current section and acting as a quick navigation aid, similar to the persistent social bar in the original hero.
